EDCS-00380334.translation: Marcus Junius Germanus, standard-bearer of the Ligurian cohort, lived 50 years and served for 18 years. He rests here.
EDCS-00380334.summary: This is a military tombstone for Marcus Junius Germanus, a standard-bearer, recording his age and service length.

EDCS-00380335.inscription_type: carmina, inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-00380335.translation: To my lady Flavia Cyriaceta, my lifelong companion. You always wished for this, and it has happened to you, husband. The pyre for you did not deceive you, the reward of your wish. For I too wished to give up my spirit in your hands. Chaste, faithful guardian, endowed with all virtues. Since she left her property to the poor, not even for her own descendants. She lived 26 years and 24 days. She died in the third hour of the night, in peace. Her father and Flavia Arnovia, her mother, for their daughter. Demeter, her husband, made [this] for his companion.
EDCS-00380335.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Cyriaceta, who died young and was remembered for her virtues and generosity, erected by her husband Demeter and her parents.

EDCS-00380336.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aurelius lived 67 years.
EDCS-00380336.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Aurelius, recording his age at death.

EDCS-03000422.translation: Lucius Valerius Potitus, son of Lucius, of the Oufentina tribe, Augustal priest, quinquennial magistrate, pontiff of Sulci, curator of sacred rites, made this at his own expense.
EDCS-03000422.summary: This is a dedication by Lucius Valerius Potitus, a prominent local magistrate and priest, for public works, likely a monument or building.

EDCS-03000425.translation: Here lies [3] of good memory. [3] lived [3] years. Rested in peace on the Ides of December.
EDCS-03000425.summary: This is a fragment of a tombstone for an individual named Laur[3], recording their death date.

EDCS-03600032.translation: To the departed spirits. For Pompeia Rhodinetus. Hyginus [made this] for his wife and [3]llisius for his mother [3]. [3] made [this].
EDCS-03600032.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription indicating that Pompeia Rhodinetus was honored by her husband Hyginus and another individual, possibly a son or relative.

EDCS-03600033.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Valerius Faustus and Lucius Valerius Castus, sons, and Antonia Fausta, wife, made [this] for Lucius Valerius Felix, the well-deserving, and for themselves and their descendants.
EDCS-03600033.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Valerius Felix, erected by his wife Antonia Fausta and their sons, also reserving space for themselves and their descendants.

EDCS-03600035.translation: To the departed spirits. Germanus lived 24 years and 3 months. His mother made [this] for her dearest son. // To the departed spirits. Vitalis lived 25 years.
EDCS-03600035.summary: This is a tombstone commemorating two individuals, Germanus and Vitalis, likely mother and son, recording their ages at death.

EDCS-03600037.translation: To the departed spirits. [3]NI [3]N [3]F [3]E. // To the departed spirits. Lucius Valerius Priscianus lived 45 years. Lucius Valerius Priscianus and [3] Valerius Primulus [made this] for [his?] father.
EDCS-03600037.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription for Lucius Valerius Priscianus, indicating he lived 45 years and that the monument was erected by his sons, Lucius Valerius Priscianus (likely a different person or named after his father) and Valerius Primulus.

EDCS-03600040.translation: ] Caius Atilius[3] for his fellow freedman[3]
EDCS-03600040.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Caius Atilius and a dedication to a fellow freedman, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-03600041.translation: To the departed spirits. Asellia Saturnina, mother, [dedicated this] to her well-deserving Ianuarius, who lived 25 years.
EDCS-03600041.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by a mother to her son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03600042.translation: To the departed spirits. His father placed [this monument] for Fructosus, who lived 13 years.
EDCS-03600042.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by a father to his son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700334.translation: To the departed spirits. [---] son of Tarsinius Quiuseius(?), lived 14 years. And Asselina lived 25 years.
EDCS-03700334.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the deaths of two individuals, likely family members, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700335.translation: To the departed spirits. Terentia Antonia lived 5 years. Her parents made [this].
EDCS-03700335.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by parents to their daughter, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700336.translation: To the departed spirits. Primus Germanus lived 39 years. Miaricora Turi lived 80 years.
EDCS-03700336.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lives of two individuals, likely a couple, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700337.translation: To the departed spirits. Turi Torvenius lived 62 years, made [this]. Fruniti made [this] for his wife, who lived 49 years, well-deserving.
EDCS-03700337.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the deaths of a couple, likely erected by the surviving husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700338.translation: [To the departed spirits.] Marcus Balerius Balerianus lived 75 years. His sons made this tomb for him.
EDCS-03700338.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Balerius Balerianus, erected by his sons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700339.translation: Ietoccor, son of Torcer, lived 32 years. His father made [this] for his son.
EDCS-03700339.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by a father to his deceased son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700341.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Balerianus lived 44 years. He made [this] inscription for his deserving wife.
EDCS-03700341.summary: A funerary epitaph where the deceased made their own tomb inscription for their wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700342.translation: To the departed spirits. Gocaras Nercaunis, aged 35 years and 1 day, made this tomb for his beloved wife.
EDCS-03700342.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by a man for his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700343.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Julius Agathangelus, patron, made this for his well-deserving Julia Helpidia, who lived 91 years.
EDCS-03700343.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Julia Helpidia by her patron, Gaius Julius Agathangelus.

EDCS-03700344.translation: To the departed spirits. Colonei lived 2 years.
EDCS-03700344.summary: A very brief funerary epitaph recording the short life of Colonei.

EDCS-03700345.translation: Gnaeus Coruncanius Faustinus lived 25 years; he rests here.
EDCS-03700345.summary: A funerary inscription for Gnaeus Coruncanius Faustinus, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-04900454.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription mentioning Licinia and Sallia.

EDCS-04900456.translation: To the departed spirits. The Aque(n)sis from the treasury lived 60 years.
EDCS-04900456.summary: A funerary epitaph for an individual identified as Aque(n)sis, possibly associated with a treasury, who lived 60 years.

EDCS-04900457.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Prisca Luci liberta vixit annis XX bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-04900457.translation: To the departed spirits. Her mother made this for Valeria Prisca, daughter of Lucius, who lived 20 years, as deserving.
EDCS-04900457.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valeria Prisca, daughter of Lucius, set up by her mother.

EDCS-04900459.translation: To the departed spirits. Proculus, son of Saturninus, lived 30 (or 41) years.
EDCS-04900459.summary: A funerary epitaph for Proculus, son of Saturninus, noting his age at death.

EDCS-04900460.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Volusius Nercau(s) lived [about] 60 years.
EDCS-04900460.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Quintus Volusius Nercau(s), recording his age at death.

EDCS-04900461.summary: A short inscription indicating a dedication or creation by a figure referred to as 'Dom(inus)'.

EDCS-04900462.summary: A very brief inscription that likely records ownership or a dedication by Ursetus and Nercaunus.

EDCS-04900463.translation: To the spirits of the departed. [He/She] made [this] for his/her well-deserving spouse, Ursetus Nispennius.
EDCS-04900463.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to a well-deserving spouse, Ursetus Nispennius.

EDCS-04900464.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius, son of Caritus, lived 21 years.
EDCS-04900464.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Valerius, son of Caritus.

EDCS-04900467.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Parthenius, his/her well-deserving son, who lived 3 years.
EDCS-04900467.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a son named Parthenius, who died at the age of three.

EDCS-04900470.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Balerius Crescentianus lived 49 years and made [this] for his well-deserving spouse.
EDCS-04900470.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Balerius Crescentianus, who lived 49 years and dedicated a monument to his spouse.

EDCS-04900471.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caecilius Gemellus lived 37 years; his aunt Severa made [this].
EDCS-04900471.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Severa for her nephew, Caecilius Gemellus, who lived 37 years.

EDCS-04900475.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flavia Faventina lived 25 years; her husband Hermes made [this].
EDCS-04900475.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Hermes for his wife, Flavia Faventina, who lived 25 years.

EDCS-05000365.translation: During the censorship of Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus; 29th census.
EDCS-05000365.summary: This inscription records the 29th census during the censorship of Emperor Hadrian Augustus.

EDCS-05000370.translation: To the divine spirits of the Nymphs. Marcus Matromulus, a most distinguished man, governor of Sardinia.
EDCS-05000370.summary: This inscription honors the Nymphs and identifies Marcus Matromulus as a governor of Sardinia.

EDCS-05000371.translation: ... 28 years, 3 months... for his well-deserving spouse.
EDCS-05000371.summary: This is a partial funerary inscription recording the lifespan of a spouse.

EDCS-05000372.summary: This is a partial funerary inscription dedicated to a well-deserving mother.

EDCS-05000373.translation: He/She lived for 23 days, [---] years [---] years. To her/his sweetest son? [---]
EDCS-05000373.summary: A tombstone inscription commemorating a deceased individual, likely a child, with a dedication from a parent, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05000374.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Novatianus lived for 2 years and 3 months. His mother made this.
EDCS-05000374.summary: A funerary epitaph for Novatianus, erected by his mother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200287.translation: To Carsionius, patron, who deserved well. Made this.
EDCS-05200287.summary: A funerary monument, likely an epitaph, honoring a patron named Carsionius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200292.text_edition: \n Numini deo Silvano nemoris Sorabensis Caius Ulpius Severus procurator Augusti praefectus provinciae Sardiniae\n
EDCS-05200292.translation: To Diana and Silvanus of the Nemorian Forest. Gaius Ulpius Severus, procurator of Augustus, prefect of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-05200292.summary: A dedication to deities by a high-ranking imperial official, Gaius Ulpius Severus, who served as procurator and prefect in Sardinia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200294.text_edition: \n Milia passuum XXII domino nostro Imperatori Caesari Galerio Valerio Max[imiano ------\n
EDCS-05200294.translation: 22 miles, from our lord Emperor Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximian.
EDCS-05200294.summary: An milestone inscription recording a distance of 22 miles dedicated to Emperor Galerius Maximian, dating to the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-05200295.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Florentia dulcis nata amoris parentibus cito erepta merita dici famula Dei in pace nnum IIII mensium III amori pii parentes fecerunt\n
EDCS-05200295.translation: Aurelia Florentia, sweet, to good memory, born of the greatest love of her parents, quickly snatched away, deserving to be called a servant of God, departed in peace. She lived for 4 years, 3 months, and 6 days. Her loving parents made this in piety.
EDCS-05200295.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia Florentia, who died at a young age, expressing grief and piety from her parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200297.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Iulio Potito |(centurioni) cohortis praetoriae Sardorum militavit nnos XIV vixit annos XXXV AVS\n
EDCS-05200297.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Julius Potitus, centurion of the Praetorian cohort of the Sardinians. He served for 14 years, lived for 35 years. [---]
EDCS-05200297.summary: A funerary epitaph for Julius Potitus, a centurion in the Praetorian guard, detailing his military service and lifespan, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200298.text_edition: \n Hic effusus est sanguis beatissimi martyris Luxuri celebratur natale eius XII Calendas sic Septembres renobatur sic sup sic temporibus Heliae episcop(i)\n
EDCS-05200298.translation: Here was shed the blood of the most blessed martyr Luxurius. His feast day is celebrated on the Kalends of September. Renovated in the time of Bishop Elias.
EDCS-05200298.summary: An inscription commemorating the martyr Luxurius, noting his feast day and a renovation under Bishop Elias, likely dating to the Early Christian period.

EDCS-05200300.text_edition: \n Hic iacet bonae memoriae Iuliana qui sic bixit sic annis plus minus XLV requi(evit) in pace Idus Ianuarias\n
EDCS-05200300.translation: Here lies Juliana, of good memory, who lived for approximately 45 years. She rested in peace on the Ides of January.
EDCS-05200300.summary: A funerary epitaph for Juliana, recording her name, approximate age at death, and date of repose, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200303.translation: To Caecilia, who lived for [---] years. [---] friend [---].
EDCS-05200303.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Caecilia, mentioning her lifespan and a connection to a friend, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200304.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vi ia quae vixit annis XXXXVIII heredes bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-05200304.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. [Name] who lived 48 years. Her deserving heirs erected this.
EDCS-05200304.summary: A funerary epitaph for a woman named [Name] who lived 48 years, erected by her heirs, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200306.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio H Fausto Lucius H Aristius Lucius H Faustio Lucius H Gemellus patrono vivi fecerunt vixit annis XXXV\n
EDCS-05200306.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius H() Faustus. Lucius H() Aristius, Lucius H() Gemellus, his patron, erected this while alive. He lived 35 years.
EDCS-05200306.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius H() Faustus, who lived 35 years, erected by his patron and freedmen, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200308.text_edition: \n Milia passuum CXVIII Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus divi Antonini Magni Pii filius divi Severi nepos tribunicia potestate III consul pontifex maximus pater patriae proconsul III viam quae a Karalibus Olbiae ducit vetustate collapsam restituit provinciae Sardiniae\n
EDCS-05200308.translation: 118 Roman miles. Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus Magnus Pius, grandson of the deified Severus, holder of tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul, pontifex maximus, father of the fatherland, proconsul for the 3rd time, restored the road that leads from Karalis to Olbia, which had collapsed due to age, [throughout] the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-05200308.summary: A milestone inscription recording the restoration of a road between Karalis and Olbia by Emperor Marcus Aurelius, likely dating to his reign (161-180 AD).

EDCS-05200309.text_edition: \n Milia passuum CXVIII divo Aureliano curante Caesio Eirminiano sic viro egregio praeside provinciae Sardiniae\n
EDCS-05200309.translation: 118 Roman miles. Under the supervision of the deified Aurelian, with Caesius Eirminianus, an outstanding man, governor of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-05200309.summary: A milestone inscription mentioning the road project under the supervision of governor Caesius Eirminianus, likely in the late 3rd century AD, referencing Emperor Aurelian.

EDCS-07000523.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Laurenti Caelesitanae sic se viva fecit\n
EDCS-07000523.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria Laurenti, from Caelesita, made this for herself while alive.
EDCS-07000523.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valeria Laurenti of Caelesita, who erected the monument for herself during her lifetime, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000524.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Iunius Faustinus vixit annos LXX Silvana coniugi bene merenti fecit Dis Manibus Caecilia Candida vixit annos XXXV menses V dies X Iunius Faustinus coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-07000524.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Junius Faustinus lived 70 years. Silvana made this for her deserving husband. // To the Spirits of the Departed. Caecilia Candida lived 35 years, 5 months, 10 days. Iunius Faustinus made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-07000524.summary: A double funerary epitaph, recording the deaths of Lucius Junius Faustinus (aged 70) and Caecilia Candida (aged 35 years, 5 months, 10 days), with monuments erected by their respective spouses.

EDCS-07000525.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Pupio Extricato Marco Pupio Extricato vixit nnos XX Pupia Extricata filio bene merenti piissimo fecit\n
EDCS-07000525.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Pupius Extricatus. Pupia Extricata made this for her most devoted son, Marcus Pupius Extricatus, who lived 20 years.
EDCS-07000525.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Pupius Extricatus, who lived 20 years, erected by his devoted mother, Pupia Extricata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000526.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Minicio Longino vixit annos XXVI Iulia Minicia soror bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-07000526.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Quintus Minicius Longinus. His sister Iulia Minicia made this for her deserving brother, who lived 26 years.
EDCS-07000526.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Minicius Longinus, who lived 26 years, erected by his sister Iulia Minicia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000527.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aurelia Custa sic vixit annos XXVI Aurelius Eperecius sic uxori amantissimae fecit\n
EDCS-07000527.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Custa lived 26 years. Aurelius Eperecius made this for his most loving wife.
EDCS-07000527.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia Custa, who lived 26 years, erected by her loving husband Aurelius Eperecius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000529.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Decimius Vitalis vixit annos LXX Iulian ten coniugi Dis Manibus Caio Decimio Ingenuo vixit annos uos fratres bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-07000529.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Decimius Vitalis lived 70 years. [Wife's name?]. // To the Spirits of the Departed. For Gaius Decimius Ingenuus. His brothers erected this for their deserving brother, who lived 2 years.
EDCS-07000529.summary: A double funerary epitaph, recording the deaths of Decimius Vitalis (aged 70) and Gaius Decimius Ingenuus (aged 2 years), with monuments erected by a spouse and brothers respectively.

EDCS-07000534.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Seneqio sic Tamucaris bixit sic annis XXV bene merenti\n
EDCS-07000534.translation: To the gods of the departed. Senecio Iamucaris lived 25 years. To one well deserving.
EDCS-07000534.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Senecio Iamucaris, who lived 25 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000539.summary: This is a dedication by a society called the Buduntes, likely for a public monument or a shared space, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000540.text_edition: \n Silo Terenti filius turma Eli Faustilli annorum LX militavit annos XXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-07000540.translation: Silo, son of Terentius Anteius Faustillus, aged 60, served for 35 years, is buried here.
EDCS-07000540.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Silo, son of Terentius Anteius Faustillus, recording his age and military service, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000541.text_edition: \n Valerius ius nnorum hic situs est\n
EDCS-07000541.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph recording a name and age, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000542.text_edition: \n Publius Docetius Terti filius Tromentina Ligus\n
EDCS-07000542.translation: Publius Docetius Tertius, son of, from Ligys (tribe Tromentina).
EDCS-07000542.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph or tomb marker identifying a person by name, filiation, and tribal affiliation, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000549.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Beneria, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000551.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication or funerary epitaph mentioning Valerius Maximianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000564.text_edition: \n Milia passuum XLV domino nostro Flavio Claudio Constantino beatissimo ac florentissimo Caesari consuli iterum Postumius Matidianus Lepidus curavit vir clarissimus praeses\n
EDCS-07000564.translation: 45(?) Roman miles. Postumius Matidianus Lepidus, clarissimus vir and praeses, arranged for this to be done for our lord Flavius Claudius Constantinus, most blessed and most flourishing Caesar, consul for the second time.
EDCS-07000564.summary: This is a milestone inscription or record of public works, indicating a distance of 45 miles and commissioned by the governor Postumius Matidianus Lepidus for Emperor Constantinus in his second consulship (dated to 315-325 AD).

EDCS-07000565.text_edition: \n Mislius Cora Benets Celele F Bacoru Sabdaga Obrisio impensis suis faciendum curaverunt Mario et Afinio consulibus\n
EDCS-07000565.translation: Mislius Cora[...], Benets Celele F[...], Bacoru Sabdaga Obrisio paid for this to be made at their own expense. They arranged for it to be done while Marius and Afinius were consuls.
EDCS-07000565.summary: This is a dedication or building record funded by several individuals, noting it was made at their expense and completed during the consulship of Marius and Afinius (date uncertain, possibly late Roman Republic or early Imperial period).

EDCS-07000566.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Disiae Moschidi Eufrosinus sic sorori pientissimae fecit annorum C\n
EDCS-07000566.translation: To the gods of the departed. Eufrosinus made this for his dearest sister, Disia Moschidi, aged 100.
EDCS-07000566.summary: This is a funerary epitaph erected by Eufrosinus for his sister Disia Moschidi, who lived 100 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07600208.text_edition: \n Caius Apsenna Cai filius heic sic heic sic est Pollio\n
EDCS-07600208.translation: Gaius Apsena, son of Gaius, here lies Pollio.
EDCS-07600208.summary: A simple epitaph identifying Gaius Apsena Pollio, likely a tombstone, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07600209.text_edition: \n Aesculapio votum solvit libens merito Lucius Cornelius Sylla sic\n
EDCS-07600209.translation: Lucius Cornelius Sulla willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Aesculapius.
EDCS-07600209.summary: An ex-voto dedication to the god Aesculapius by Lucius Cornelius Sulla, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-08300355.text_edition: \n alumn menses VI dies X Surdinius Felix |(centurio) cohortis I Sardorum\n
EDCS-08300355.translation: Maximil[l]a had this made for her well-deserving wife, a centurion of the 1st Cohort of Sardinians, [---] Felix, who served for 6 months and 10 days [---].
EDCS-08300355.summary: A funerary monument for a centurion of the 1st Cohort of Sardinians, erected by his wife Maximilla, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-08400268.text_edition: \n Bonae memoriae Graeca quiescit in pace vixit annos XX menses II dies XVIII deposita pridie Idus Ianuarias\n
EDCS-08400268.translation: To good memory, Graeca rests in peace. She lived 20 years, 2 months, and 18 days. She was buried the day before the Ides of January.
EDCS-08400268.summary: A Christian epitaph for a woman named Graeca, recording her lifespan and burial date, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-08400269.text_edition: \n Milia passuum CXVIIII Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis pater patriae proconsul viam quae ducit a Karalis sic Olviae sic betustate sic corruptam restituit curante Marco Ulpio Victore procuratore suo egregio viro\n
EDCS-08400269.translation: 117 miles. Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, restored the road which leads from Karalis to Olbia, corrupted by age, under the care of his procurator, the distinguished man Marcus Ulpius Victor.
EDCS-08400269.summary: An imperial milestone inscription recording the restoration of the road from Karalis to Olbia by Emperor Philip the Arab, dated to his reign (244-249 CE).

EDCS-08400271.text_edition: \n Milia passuum CXVIIII domino nostro Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Claudio Pio Felici Invictis sic Auggustis sic pontifici maxiximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae proconsuli viam quae ducit a Karalibus Olbiae vetusti(ate) corruptam restituit curante Marco Aurelio Quintillo procuratore suo egregio viro\n
EDCS-08400271.translation: 117 miles. To our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, restored the road which leads from Karalis to Olbia, corrupted by age, under the care of his procurator, the distinguished man Marcus Aurelius Quintillus.
EDCS-08400271.summary: An imperial milestone inscription recording the restoration of the road from Karalis to Olbia by Emperor Claudius Gothicus, dated to his reign (268-270 CE).

EDCS-08400272.text_edition: \n biam sic quae ducit a Karalibus Olviae sic betustate! corruptam restituerunt curante Marco Antonio Septimio Heraclito procuratore suo egregio viro\n
EDCS-08400272.translation: [---] restored the road which leads from Karalis to Olbia, corrupted by age, under the care of his procurator, the distinguished man Marcus Antonius Septimius Heraclitus.
EDCS-08400272.summary: A milestone inscription recording the restoration of the road from Karalis to Olbia, managed by the procurator Marcus Antonius Septimius Heraclitus, dated to the late Roman period.

EDCS-08400274.text_edition: \n Impperatoribus Caessaribus ddominis nnostris Flavio Valerio Constantio Galerio Valerio Maxsimiano sic Piis Felicibus Inbictis sic Auggustis et ddominis nnostris Flavio Valerio Severo et Galerio Valerio Maxsimiano sic nobilissimis Caessaribus principibus iuventutis viam quae ducit a Karalibus Olviae sic vetustate corrumptam sic restituente Galerio Valerio [Domitiano viro perfectissimo praeside] provinciae Sardiniae d(evoto) n(umini) m(aiestatique) eorum\n
EDCS-08400274.translation: For our lords Emperors Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, the pious, happy, unconquered Augusti, and for our lords Flavius Valerius Severus and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesars, princes of youth, the road which leads from Karalis to Olbia, corrupted by age, was restored by the perfectissimus Gal[erius?] Val[erius?] [Domitianus?], president of the province of Sardinia, devoted to their divine majesty.
EDCS-08400274.summary: A milestone inscription detailing the restoration of the road from Karalis to Olbia by Emperors Constantius I and Galerius Maximian, and their junior colleagues Severus and Maximian II, with the work overseen by the governor of Sardinia, dated to the Tetrarchy (c. 293-305 CE).

EDCS-08600293.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Candidus natione Bessus miles ex classe praetoria Misenatium centuria bati Rufi militavit annos XVIII menses X vixit annos XXXIIX\n
EDCS-08600293.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Gaius Julius Candidus, a soldier of Bessian origin from the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, served for 18 years and 10 months. He lived 38 years.
EDCS-08600293.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Julius Candidus, a soldier from Misenum's fleet, recording his ethnicity, service, and lifespan, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-08600294.text_edition: \n Lucio Rutiliano Rutilius Felix pater fecit vixit annos X\n
EDCS-08600294.translation: Lucius Rutilius Felix made this for his father, Lucius Rutilianus, deserving. He lived 10 years [---].
EDCS-08600294.summary: A funerary monument set up by Lucius Rutilius Felix for his father Lucius Rutilianus, recording the father's name and the son's filial devotion, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-08600295.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Arrio Crescenti fecit Antonia Rutilia coniugi bene merenti vixit annos XXXX\n
EDCS-08600295.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Antonia Rutilia made this for her well-deserving husband, Lucius Arrius Crescens, who lived 40 years.
EDCS-08600295.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Arrius Crescens, dedicated by his wife, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600296.text_edition: \n Philoponius Fortunatae virginiae coniugi filis ac nepotibus procreatis debitum Domino reddidit\n
EDCS-08600296.translation: Philoponius has paid his debt to his lord: Fortunata, his wife, and the children and grandchildren she bore him.
EDCS-08600296.summary: A funerary epitaph commemorating Fortunata, wife of Philoponius, and their descendants, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600297.text_edition: \n Spiritus sanctus hic quiescit Irene vixit nnos III menses \n
EDCS-08600297.translation: The Holy Spirit rests here. Irene lived 3 years, [some] months...
EDCS-08600297.summary: A funerary epitaph for Irene, a child who lived 3 years, likely from the Late Roman or Early Christian period.

EDCS-08600300.text_edition: \n Tiberius Iulius Fabia Capito missicius ex cohorte Aquitanorum III annorum LXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-08600300.translation: Tiberius Iulius Capito, son of Fabia, a mis(s)ic(ius) from the 3rd Cohort of Aquitanians, aged 65 years, is buried here.
EDCS-08600300.summary: A military epitaph for Tiberius Iulius Capito, a soldier from the 3rd Cohort of Aquitanians, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900412.text_edition: \n Milia passuum CXV domino nostro Imperatori Flavio Iulio Constanti beatissimo Caesari Flavius Titianus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Sardiniae devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-08900412.translation: 115 miles. Flavius Titianus, vir perfectissimus, praeses of the province of Sardinia, devoted to the divinity and majesty of our lord Emperor Flavius Julius Constantius, most blessed Caesar.
EDCS-08900412.summary: A milestone recording the construction of 115 miles by Flavius Titianus, governor of Sardinia, for Emperor Constantius, dated to the 4th century CE.

EDCS-08900943.text_edition: \n miles legionis XIIII geminae centuria Luci Aciri stipendiorum XVIIII vixit annos XXXXIII hic situs est\n
EDCS-08900943.translation: [...] a soldier of the 14th Legion Gemina, in the centuria of Lucius Acirius, with 19 years of service, lived 43 years, is buried here.
EDCS-08900943.summary: A military epitaph for an unnamed soldier of the 14th Legion Gemina, recording his service and age, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900944.text_edition: \n Marco Laevio Felici Calventiae Quinti filiae Marcellae Marcus Laevius Marcellus parentibus optimis vivus fecit in fronte pedes XXII retro pedes XLIII\n
EDCS-08900944.translation: Marcus Laevius Marcellus made this tomb while alive for his most excellent parents, Marcus Laevius Felicis and Marcella, daughter of Quintus Calventius. Frontage 22 feet, depth 43 feet.
EDCS-08900944.summary: A funerary monument built by Marcus Laevius Marcellus for his parents, with dimensions provided, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900978.text_edition: \n Impperatoribus Caessaribus domino nostro Flavio Valerio Constantino Aurelio Galerio Maximiano pissimis felicissimis Augustis Caesari domino nostro Galerio Valerio Maximiniano Augustorum filio tribunicia potestate consuli I sic restituente et curante Os procuratore \n
EDCS-08900978.translation: To our Emperors and Lords, Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Aurelius Galerius Maximianus, most pious, most fortunate Augusti. To Caesar our lord Galerius Valerius Maximinus, son of the Augusti, invested with tribunician power, consul. Restored and overseen by [---], procurator.
EDCS-08900978.summary: A dedication to multiple Roman emperors, including Constantius, Galerius Maximianus, and Maximinus, likely relating to public works, dated to the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-08900985.text_edition: \n Vincentia Cassiano conpari bene merenti in pace qui discessit Nonis Februariis qui vixit annos LXXV menses duo dies X\n
EDCS-08900985.translation: Vincentia for Cassianus, her well-deserving companion, who departed in peace on the Nones of February, having lived 75 years, 2 months, 10 days.
EDCS-08900985.summary: A funerary epitaph for Cassianus, dedicated by Vincentia, recording his death date and age, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900986.text_edition: \n Limenius senior qui bixit sic annos plus minus LXXX quiescet sic in pace IIII Idus Iunias\n
EDCS-08900986.translation: Limenius senior, who lived approximately 80 years, rests in peace on the 4th Ides of June.
EDCS-08900986.summary: A funerary epitaph for Limenius senior, recording his approximate age and date of death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900999.text_edition: \n AISA T insistente Flavio M fabricae PT\n
EDCS-08900999.translation: Under the supervision of Flavius... of the workshop...
EDCS-08900999.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to record a workshop or construction project, mentioning Flavius and possibly indicating the context of imperial administration or construction.

EDCS-09001402.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a property marker or a dedication indicating ownership by Quintus Memmius Karus.

EDCS-09001406.text_edition: \n Opus doliare ex praediis Statoniensibus Augusti domini nostri ex figlinis Marci Adi Sp\n
EDCS-09001406.translation: Brickwork from the estates of Statonius, from the kilns of Marcus Adi Speratus, property of our lord Commodus.
EDCS-09001406.summary: This inscription records brickwork from specific estates and kilns, identifying the owner as Marcus Adi Speratus and dedicating it to the emperor Commodus, likely dating to his reign (177-192 AD).

EDCS-09001407.summary: This is a fragment, possibly recording the name of Planius Victorinus and his parentage or affiliations, the rest is illegible.

EDCS-09001408.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae A Titus Aelius Augusti libertus Victor procurator regionis e fecit\n
EDCS-09001408.translation: To the divine shades. Flavia Amoeba. Titus Aelius Victor, freedman of Augustus, procurator of the Turritana riverbank, made [this].
EDCS-09001408.summary: This funerary inscription honors Flavia Amoeba, erected by Titus Aelius Victor, who was a freedman of the emperor and held the position of procurator of the Turritana riverbank, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09001418.text_edition: \n Titus Flavius Augusti libertus Maximus fecit Poticus servus\n
EDCS-09001418.translation: Titus Flavius Maximus, freedman of the emperor, made this; also Poticus, the slave.
EDCS-09001418.summary: An inscription recording the construction of a monument by Titus Flavius Maximus, a freedman, and his slave Poticus.

EDCS-09200306.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Iusto vixit annis XVIII diebus XXVIIII Titus Flavius Augusti libertus Martialis filio piissimo et Flaviae Primitivae filiae et Flaviae Auxesi coniugi bene merentibus fecit\n
EDCS-09200306.translation: To the divine shades. Titus Flavius Justus lived 18 years and 29 days. Titus Flavius Martialis, freedman of Augustus, [erected this] for his most dutiful son, and for Flavia Primitiua, his daughter, and for Flavia Auxesi, his wife, who deserved well.
EDCS-09200306.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Titus Flavius Martialis, a freedman of the emperor, for his son Titus Flavius Justus (who died at 18 years old), his daughter Flavia Primitiua, and his wife Flavia Auxesi, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09200307.text_edition: \n Iucunda Caturonis liberta annorum L hic sita est posuerunt filias sic Iulia et Spana Gratus libertus\n
EDCS-09200307.translation: Iucunda, freedwoman of Caturo, 50 years old, lies here. Her daughters Julia and Spana [set this up]. Gratus, freedman.
EDCS-09200307.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Iucunda, a freedwoman of Caturo who lived to 50 years old, and notes that it was set up by her daughters Julia and Spana, with Gratus, a freedman, also mentioned.

EDCS-09300954.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Valerius Genialis vixit anno I mensibus VIIII diebus XVII filio dulcissimo parentes bene merenti fecer(unt)\n
EDCS-09300954.translation: To the divine shades. Gaius Valerius Genialis lived 1 year, 9 months, and 17 days. His parents made [this] for their most sweet son, who deserved well.
EDCS-09300954.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates the short life of Gaius Valerius Genialis, who lived just over a year, and was erected by his parents.

EDCS-09300955.summary: This is a heavily fragmented inscription that mentions Caesar Gallienus, suggesting a dedication or official record from his reign (253-268 AD).

EDCS-09300957.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantino maximo Pio Felici Invicto ac perpetuo semper Augusto Titus Semptimius sic Ianuarius vir clarissimus praeses provinciae Sardiniae devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-09300957.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Maximus Pius Felix Invictus and perpetual Augustus. Titus Semptimius Ianurius, clarissimus vir, praeses of the province of Sardinia, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-09300957.summary: This dedication honors Emperor Constantine the Great by Titus Semptimius Ianurius, the governor (praeses) of Sardinia, likely dating to Constantine's sole rule or his co-rule with his sons (after 317 AD).

EDCS-09400442.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus Marco Arruntio Rogato filio piissimo et Arruntio Gallo fecit Lucius Arruntius Teres\n
EDCS-09400442.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Arruntius Teres made this for his most devoted son, Marcus Arruntius Rogatus, and for Arruntius Gallus.
EDCS-09400442.summary: A funerary monument set up by Lucius Arruntius Teres for his son Marcus Arruntius Rogatus and for Arruntius Gallus.

EDCS-09400446.text_edition: \n Milia passuum CLXIII Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II pater patriae proconsul consul viam quae adducet sic a Karalibus Olviae sic suae vetustate corruptam restituit curante ETV VSVE\n
EDCS-09400446.translation: 163 miles. Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, father of the country, proconsul, consul, restored the road, which leads from Karalibus to Olbia, ruined by age. This was done under the supervision of [---]ius, an excellent man.
EDCS-09400446.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a road from Karalibus to Olbia by Emperor Philippus, completed during his consulship.

EDCS-09401083.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luciliae Caesarum nostrorum servae vixit annis plus minus XXXI Fructus Caesarum nostrorum servus coniugi carissimae bene merenti fecit sibi posterisque\n
EDCS-09401083.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Fructus, a slave of our Caesars, made this for his dearest wife Lucilia, a slave of our Caesars, who lived for more or less 31 years, for her well-deserved memory, and for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-09401083.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Fructus to his wife Lucilia, both slaves of the imperial household.

EDCS-09401432.text_edition: \n Milia passuum CXXII Imperatori Caesari Caio Iulio Vero Maximino Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli designato imperatori II patri patriae proconsuli biam sic quae ducit a Karalibus Olbiae vetustate corrumptam sic restituit curante abiano praefecto provinciae Sardiniae procuratore suo viro egregio\n
EDCS-09401432.translation: 122 miles. For Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinius Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul-designate, imperator for the second time, father of the country, proconsul. He restored the road, which leads from Karalibus to Olbia, ruined by age. This was done under the supervision of Gaius Fabius (?) Fabianus, prefect of the province of Sardinia, his procurator, an excellent man.
EDCS-09401432.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a road from Karalibus to Olbia by Emperor Maximinius, overseen by the governor of Sardinia, Gaius Fabius (?) Fabianus.

EDCS-09700161.text_edition: \n Balari fines Ema praefectus provinciae passus DLIIII\n
EDCS-09700161.translation: Balari. The prefect of the province ordered a boundary to be placed. 554 paces.
EDCS-09700161.summary: A boundary marker set by a provincial prefect concerning the land of the Balari tribe, measuring 554 paces.

EDCS-09700162.text_edition: \n ------ al]ae Gallorum civium Romanorum miliariae tribuno Hispanorum tironum Latobicorum equitatae II Breucorum peditatae honoribus in patria functo Aurelinus Victor Montanus cola Silvanus untia Saturnina Augusti Antonini Pii\n
EDCS-09700162.translation: Of the ala Gallorum milliaria civium Romanorum, a tribune; of the Hispani equites tirones; of the Breuci pedites. Honored in his homeland. Aurelianus Victor Montanus; from Silvanus; [---]untia Saturnina; [---] Antoninus Pius.
EDCS-09700162.summary: A fragmented inscription likely listing military units and individuals, possibly related to a tomb or honorific monument, possibly connected to Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-09700163.text_edition: \n Sardo Patri Alexander Augusti servus regionarius dono dedit\n
EDCS-09700163.translation: To Sardo Patri. Alexander, a slave of Augustus, a regional official, dedicated this gift.
EDCS-09700163.summary: An offering made by Alexander, an imperial slave and regional official, to Sardo Patri.

EDCS-09700579.text_edition: \n Lyde Tiberi Germanici vixit annos XX Secundio conservos sic <f=E>ecit \n
EDCS-09700579.translation: Lyde, daughter of Tiberius Germanicus, lived 20 years. Secundius made her a freedwoman.
EDCS-09700579.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lyde, daughter of Tiberius Germanicus, who died at age 20, with her manumission by Secundius noted.

EDCS-09700581.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Corneliae quae vixit nnos Hilarus coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-09700581.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Cornelia(?), who lived [---] years. Hilarus made this for his well-deserved wife, for himself and [---] of her husband.
EDCS-09700581.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Hilarus to his wife Cornelia(?), likely also including provisions for himself and her deceased husband.

EDCS-11600199.translation: To the Nymphs, a vow for the safety of Quintus Baebius Modestus, admitted among the counselor friends by the Emperors Antoninus and Commodus Augusti. Servatus, freedman of the Augusti, procurator of the metals and estates, and his assistant, procurator of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-11600199.summary: A votive inscription to the Nymphs for the safety of Quintus Baebius Modestus, an official appointed by Emperors Antoninus and Commodus, with Servatus identified as a procurator.

EDCS-11600200.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Asadiso Osurbali, who died at the age of two.

EDCS-11600202.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Julius lived...
EDCS-11600202.summary: This fragmentary inscription is a funerary text dedicated to the underworld gods for a person named Julius.

EDCS-11600205.translation: Gaius Valerius, son of Anti, made this. His mother lived 35 years.
EDCS-11600205.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of a mother, possibly erected by her son Gaius Valerius.

EDCS-11600207.summary: This is a tombstone for Valerius, who died at the age of forty.

EDCS-11600208.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a woman named Atica.

EDCS-11600209.translation: Carius, son of Quintus, lived 16 years...
EDCS-11600209.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the life of Carius, who died at the age of sixteen.

EDCS-12100289.translation: To Gaius Coelius Magnus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, who served as junior decurion twice, as aedile twice, as judge, as priest of the Augusti, and as pontiff of the sacred rites, and as patron of the city, by decree of the most distinguished council.
EDCS-12100289.summary: This is a public dedication honoring Gaius Coelius Magnus for his distinguished civic and religious career in his city.

EDCS-12100294.translation: To Aemilia Urbana, who lived five years and sixteen days. Her mother, a most unhappy and incomparable daughter...
EDCS-12100294.summary: This is a moving funerary inscription from a mother to her deceased young daughter, Aemilia Urbana.

EDCS-12100295.translation: ...Alia made this for her well-loved husband... lived...
EDCS-12100295.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription possibly recording a wife, Alia, dedicating a monument to her husband.

EDCS-12100296.translation: Antonia Pull[a?], daughter of Lucius, and Annicus[?]. Lucius Antonius Flaccus, son of Lucius, lived... Lucius Antonius, son of Lucius, his freedman, made this for himself and his family. Father, behold I am here for my children...
EDCS-12100296.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription likely for multiple family members, including a father and his freedman, potentially intended for a family tomb.

EDCS-12100297.translation: Pompeius Felix Senecio Docimus and Quinta, his very dear mother, made this for themselves and for Gargilia Gemella, daughter of Lucius, his wife and lady.
EDCS-12100297.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument made by Pompeius Felix Senecio Docimus and Quinta for themselves, a wife, and a mother, likely dating to the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-12100298.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Julius Optatus, his good spouse made this well for him.
EDCS-12100298.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by a spouse for Gaius Julius Optatus, their good husband, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12100299.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Pompeius Marcianus lived for 23 years and six months. Aulonia Restituta made this well for her deserving son.
EDCS-12100299.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by Aulonia Restituta for her deserving son, Lucius Pompeius Marcianus, who died at age 23, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12100300.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Pompeius Mustulus Pontianus made this well for his deserving brother Publius Pompeius Dativus.
EDCS-12100300.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by Pompeius Mustulus Pontianus for his deserving brother, Publius Pompeius Dativus, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12100303.translation: [...]anus, [...]as, [...] slave(?), cared for this to be made for the deserving one.
EDCS-12100303.summary: This fragmentary inscription records someone (perhaps a slave) arranging for a funerary monument for a deserving person, the details of which are lost.

EDCS-12100320.translation: Marcus Scribonius Nicomachus made this for himself, for his wife Helpina, and for his family, during his lifetime.
EDCS-12100320.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument dedicated by Marcus Scribonius Nicomachus for himself, his wife Helpina, and his family, indicating it was built during his lifetime, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12100328.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Silvanus Tantilianus lived for 50 years. Felicitas, his dearest wife, made this.
EDCS-12100328.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by Felicitas for her husband Silvanus Tantilianus, who lived to 50 years old, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12100332.translation: For Quintus Minucius Pius, son of Quintus, a threefold official in the judiciary, chosen for the third time as flamen Augustalis, first chosen by decree of the decurions, and first chosen as perpetual flamen Augustalis by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-12100332.summary: This inscription honors Quintus Minucius Pius for his numerous civic and religious offices, including multiple appointments as a flamen Augustalis and judiciary official, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12100333.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Cara Marcellina, sufficient for herself, lived for 60 years, 9 months, and 20 days. She was widowed for 9 years, 11 months, and 10 days. Aurelia Victoria made this for her incomparable patron, deserving of honor.
EDCS-12100333.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by Aurelia Victoria for her incomparable patron, Aelia Cara Marcellina, who lived to 60 and was widowed for 9 years, likely dating to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12100336.translation: Aulus Vitellius Urbanus, the Augustalis magistracy, gave this as a gift.
EDCS-12100336.summary: This inscription records Aulus Vitellius Urbanus donating as a gift in his role as an Augustalis magistrate, likely dating to the late 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12100337.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100337.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Vespasiani filio Domitiano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II imperatori III patri patriae cons(uli) VIIII designato X Sextus Laecanius Labeo procurator Augusti praefectus provinciae Sardiniae plateas et c itinera municipi Caralitani sternenda et cloacas faciendas et tegendas pecunia publica et privata curavit\n
EDCS-12100337.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, pontiff, with tribunician power for the second time, imperator for the third time, father of the fatherland, consul for the ninth time, consul-designate for the tenth. Sextus Laecanius Labeo, procurator of the Emperor, prefect of the province of Sardinia, undertook at public and private expense the paving of streets and fields and the construction and covering of sewers for the municipality of Cagliari.
EDCS-12100337.summary: This inscription records the paving of streets and construction of sewers in Cagliari, funded by Sextus Laecanius Labeo, under the reign of Emperor Domitian.

EDCS-12100339.text_edition: \n ti filio Quirina I --- trib(uno) mil(itum) leg(ionis) I Italicae tribuno militum legionis quaestori praetori pr patrono coloniae Karalitanorum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-12100339.translation: [Epitaph for] Tiberius, son of Tiberius, of the Quirina tribe, [named] Italicus, tribune of soldiers of the [rank] ... legion, quaestor, praetor, [honored by the citizens] of Cagliari, [using] public funds.
EDCS-12100339.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the career of a man named Italicus, possibly of senatorial rank, who served in the military and held magistracies, with his career being funded by public money in Cagliari.

EDCS-12100342.text_edition: \n Benniae ca Flavia a Neriti Oe cum Lucio Bennio Luci filio Quirina Felice Miniciano XVviro sacris faciundis nepote\n
EDCS-12100342.translation: Bennia [name] Flavia [name] Neriti [name] with Lucius Bennius Felix, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, Miniciano, member of the Quindecimvirs for the performance of sacred rites, her nephew.
EDCS-12100342.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or family record, mentioning several individuals including Benniae, Flavia, Neriti, and Lucius Bennius Felix, a member of the Quindecimvirs.

EDCS-12100344.text_edition: \n Bettius Crescens domo Roma vixit nnos expeditionibus interfuit Daciae Armeniae Parthiae Iudaeae se vivo fecit\n
EDCS-12100344.translation: Lucius Tettius Crescens, from Rome, lived [number] years. He participated in expeditions to Dacia twice, Armenia, Parthia, and Judaea. He made this for himself during his lifetime.
EDCS-12100344.summary: This is a self-made epitaph for Lucius Tettius Crescens of Rome, recording his military service in various eastern campaigns and his lifespan.

EDCS-12100345.translation: Marcus Plotius Rufus, son of Siliso.
EDCS-12100345.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription or name marker for Marcus Plotius Rufus.

EDCS-12100346.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Valeria Salvia, wife of Gaius Iulius Trophimus.
EDCS-12100346.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Gaius Iulius Trophimus to his deceased wife, Antonia Naevia.

EDCS-12100347.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Apertia [name], daughter of [name] ... lived [number] years.
EDCS-12100347.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary dedication to Apertia, daughter of [name], who lived to a certain age.

EDCS-12100349.translation: Musa made this for Tiberius Claudius Agathangelus, patron.
EDCS-12100349.summary: This inscription records that Musa created this monument for her patron, Tiberius Claudius Agathangelus.

EDCS-12100350.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Claudia Sameramis made this for her well-deserving husband, Tiberius Claudius Marcellus.
EDCS-12100350.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Claudia Sameramis to her deserving husband, Tiberius Claudius Marcellus.

EDCS-12100351.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Faustina lived 35 years and 3 months. Probus made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-12100351.summary: This funerary dedication records the lifespan of Faustina and is made by her husband, Probus.

EDCS-12100352.translation: To Gabinia Leda, dearest wife, Gaius Gabinius Felix made (this monument) for himself and for his descendants.
EDCS-12100352.summary: This epitaph records a monument erected by Gaius Gabinius Felix for his wife Gabinia Leda and their descendants.

EDCS-12100353.translation: To the departed spirits. Damophilus made (this) for Gelasinus, his well-deserving pupil.
EDCS-12100353.summary: This epitaph records a monument dedicated by Damophilus to his pupil Gelasinus.

EDCS-12100354.translation: To the departed spirits. For Herennia Procula, who lived 21 years, her mother Valeria Secunda made (this) for her most dutiful daughter. // To the departed spirits. For Vaterius Luciferous, Secunda (made this) for her well-deserving spouse.
EDCS-12100354.summary: This epitaph records two separate individuals, Herennia Procula and Vaterius Luciferous, likely from the same burial area, with one part dedicated by a mother and the other by a spouse.

EDCS-12100355.translation: To the departed spirits. For Lucius Pompeius Optimus, freedman of Gaius.
EDCS-12100355.summary: This epitaph records the deceased Lucius Pompeius Optimus, a freedman.

EDCS-12100356.translation: To the departed spirits. For Primitibus, slave of Pompeius, who lived 26 years. His mother (made this) for her well-deserving son.
EDCS-12100356.summary: This epitaph records a monument erected by the mother of Primitibus, a slave, who died at the age of 26.

EDCS-12100357.translation: To the departed spirits. For Olimpias, who lived 21 years. Victor made (this) for his well-deserving spouse.
EDCS-12100357.summary: This epitaph records a monument erected by Victor for his wife Olimpias, who died at the age of 21.

EDCS-12100358.translation: To the departed spirits. For Vera Cassia, who lived 19 years. Fusca made (this) for her well-deserving (person).
EDCS-12100358.summary: This epitaph records a monument erected by Fusca for Vera Cassia, who died at the age of 19.

EDCS-12100364.translation: Ulpia Matrona made (this) for her husband Hilarus, who lived 38 years.
EDCS-12100364.summary: This epitaph records a monument erected by Ulpia Matrona for her husband Hilarus, who died at the age of 38.

EDCS-12100368.translation: [...] 53 years, 7 months, 17 days. Her mother (made this) for her well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-12100368.summary: This incomplete epitaph records a daughter who lived 53 years, 7 months, and 17 days, with the monument erected by her mother.

EDCS-12100385.translation: To the departed spirits. For Aelia Bonavia, who lived 6 years, 4 months, 12 days. Aelia Nereis made (this) for her pupil.
EDCS-12100385.summary: This epitaph records a monument erected by Aelia Nereis for her pupil Aelia Bonavia, who died at the age of 6 years, 4 months, and 12 days.

EDCS-12100387.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Arrius Callistus lived 20 years. Arria Chia and Crispinilla, his patrons, made this for him who is being buried, to a well-deserving person.
EDCS-12100387.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Arrius Callistus, set up by his patronesses, Arria Chia and Crispinilla.

EDCS-12100389.translation: To Eutychius, an innocent boy, most sweet son. His father Eutychianus and mother Felicissima made this against their vow. He lived 21 years, 6 months, and 14 days.
EDCS-12100389.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for the child Eutychius, set up by his grieving parents, Eutychianus and Felicissima.

EDCS-12100393.translation: To Hilarinus, my husband. Salvia Prima.
EDCS-12100393.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Hilarinus, dedicated by his wife Salvia Prima.

EDCS-12100394.translation: To Ireneus, a man of good memory, a good man of rare example, who lived 46 years, 8 months, 18 days, and 5 hours. Perpetua made this for her incomparable husband, and Ireneus for his father, against their vow.
EDCS-12100394.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ireneus, set up by his wife Perpetua and son Ireneus.

EDCS-12100395.translation: To the good memory of a good, innocent, and incomparable man, Munatius Ireneus, who lived in Christ 46 years, 8 months, 18 days, and 5 hours. Perpetua, his most virginal wife, and Ireneus, his dearest father, made this against their vow.
EDCS-12100395.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Munatius Ireneus, set up by his wife Perpetua and son Ireneus, mentioning his Christian faith.

EDCS-12100404.translation: To good memory. Restituta rested in peace on the 20th day, having lived approximately 29 years. [She] lived approximately [XX?] years and lived in peace [on the Xth day of November].
EDCS-12100404.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Restituta, recording her death and approximate age, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-12100410.translation: To the spirits of the departed. [For _______]ubius Paulus, who lived 67 years and [X months].
EDCS-12100410.summary: This is a funerary epitaph fragment dedicated to the deceased [_______]ubius Paulus.

EDCS-12100419.translation: [For Pomp]onius Entimus, who lived 51 years, 1 month, and 10 days, and for Antonia, who lived 9 years, 5 months, and 11 days. Hermes, their freedman, [made this] for his most devoted patrons.
EDCS-12100419.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Pomponius Entimus and Antonia, set up by their freedman Hermes.

EDCS-12100439.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Aulus Caspidius Secundus, who lived [XX?]II years, a well-deserving father.
EDCS-12100439.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aulus Caspidius Secundus, set up by his son.

EDCS-12100440.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Lucifero, who lived 27 years. To Aelius Tosuna, who lived 10 years, 1 month, and 22 days. His mother made this for [each] well-deserving [child].
EDCS-12100440.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucifero and Aelius Tosuna, set up by their mother.

EDCS-12100446.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Julius Pelagius lived 15 years, 3 months, and 5 days. His mother had this set up.
EDCS-12100446.summary: A funerary epitaph for Julius Pelagius, recording his age and indicating his mother as the dedicator.

EDCS-12100451.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Fortunata M. lived 10 years. Her father made this for his well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-12100451.summary: A funerary epitaph for Fortunata M., recording her age and noting her father as the dedicator.

EDCS-12100452.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Valerius Gellianus lived 22 years. Sempronia Donata made this for her son.
EDCS-12100452.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Valerius Gellianus, recording his age and identifying his mother as the dedicator.

EDCS-12100456.translation: Of good memory. Purpuria, who lived approximately 35 years, rested in peace.
EDCS-12100456.summary: A funerary epitaph for Purpuria, recording her approximate age and mentioning her peaceful rest.

EDCS-12100457.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Novia Hygia lived 18 years. Victoria, her mother, made this for her well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-12100457.summary: A funerary epitaph for Novia Hygia, recording her age and identifying her mother as the dedicator.

EDCS-12100459.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Julia Maricneti, of incomparable beauty, loved by all, who was taken from life too soon. She lived 26 years and 3 months. Her husband and parents made this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-12100459.summary: A funerary epitaph for Julia Maricneti, lamenting her early death, recording her age, and listing her husband and parents as dedicators.

EDCS-12100460.translation: For Fabia Flora, who lived 9 years. For Valeria Flora, who lived 10 years. Flavius Florus made this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-12100460.summary: A funerary epitaph commemorating two young girls, Fabia Flora and Valeria Flora, with Flavius Florus as the dedicator.

EDCS-12100461.text_edition: \n Antonia Urri filia vixit annos XXXVI\n
EDCS-12100461.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antonia Urri, recording her age.

EDCS-12100462.translation: [Name] Barecis son lived 51 years. Tilia his wife made this for her husband.
EDCS-12100462.summary: A funerary epitaph for [Name] Barecis, recording his age and identifying his wife as the dedicator.

EDCS-12100463.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Debos PAT[name] [father] lived 42 years. His heirs made this for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-12100463.summary: A funerary epitaph for Debos PAT[name] [father], recording his age and indicating his heirs as the dedicators.

EDCS-12100469.translation: Verpas the third, who served in the legion.
EDCS-12100469.summary: This is a military inscription recording the name and service of a soldier, Verpas, approximately dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100472.translation: To the August Nymphs, and to Asclepius.
EDCS-12100472.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to the Nymphs and Asclepius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100482.translation: To the shades of the departed. Belsa Cariti lived 101 years. Her husband made this for her, well-deserving. Benitur[.]t lived 80 years. The heirs [made this].
EDCS-12100482.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Belsa Cariti and an unknown individual, recording their ages and noting that their spouses or heirs erected the monument, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100483.translation: To the shades of the departed. V[.]i[.]a lived 65 years and [.] months.
EDCS-12100483.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone inscription recording the name and lifespan of an individual, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100484.translation: To the shades of the departed. Germana, daughter of [.]ericoris, lived 48 years. Her husband made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-12100484.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Germana, her parentage, age, and that her husband erected the monument, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100493.translation: Titus Aretius Faricus, son of Cornelius C[.], lived 50 years. His sons made this.
EDCS-12100493.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name, parentage, age, and that his sons erected the monument for Titus Aretius Faricus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100494.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone inscription recording the name and approximate age of an individual, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100495.translation: Tarcuinus, son of Fili, of the Neroneian tribe, lived 53 years. Lucinius Aure[.] and the heirs made this for him, well-deserving.
EDCS-12100495.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name, tribal affiliation, age, and that his heirs erected the monument for Tarcuinus Fili, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100499.translation: To the shades of the departed. [.]lo Nercai[.] lived 71 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-12100499.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name and age of an individual, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100501.translation: To the shades of the departed. For Lucius Cornelius Felix, son, aged 6 years. Caius Iani[.] two.
EDCS-12100501.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Lucius Cornelius Felix, aged 6, and also mentions two individuals named Caius Iani[.], likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100503.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Acareltorte, who lived 69 years, her parents made this for their well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-12100503.summary: A funerary epitaph for Acareltorte, likely dedicated by her parents, with the deceased's age provided.

EDCS-12100504.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cornelius Memor, a soldier (?) aged 90, is buried here. Marcus Fabius Faustus erected this.
EDCS-12100504.summary: A funerary epitaph for Cornelius Memor, identified as a soldier, with his age and the dedicator Marcus Fabius Faustus.

EDCS-12100505.translation: Lucius Lucretius, son of Minucius Severus, aged 7 years, died on the Kalends of September.
EDCS-12100505.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Lucretius, son of Minucius Severus, recording his age and date of death.

EDCS-12100506.translation: Secunda, freedwoman of Secundus, lived 35 years, is buried here. Iucundus, her brother, erected this.
EDCS-12100506.summary: A funerary epitaph for Secunda, a freedwoman, recording her age and the dedicator, her brother Iucundus.

EDCS-12100508.text_edition: \n Decumus Cirneti filius Ciniensis cohortis Aquitanorum annorum XXXIII stupendiorum sic XV hic situs est\n
EDCS-12100508.translation: Decumus, son of Cirnetus, of the CNI cohort of Aquitanians, aged 32, with 15 years of service, is buried here.
EDCS-12100508.summary: A military funerary epitaph for Decumus, son of Cirnetus, of the CNI cohort of Aquitanians, noting his age and years of service.

EDCS-12100510.translation: Ferentius, son of Milonius, lived 40 years, is buried here. His son took care to have this made.
EDCS-12100510.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ferentius, son of Milonius, recording his age and stating that his son arranged for the monument.

EDCS-12100511.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Aemilius Pacatus, who lived 9 months and 21 days. Lucius Aemilius Honoratus (father) and Cassia Severa (mother) made this for their well-deserving son.
EDCS-12100511.summary: A funerary epitaph for the infant Lucius Aemilius Pacatus, dedicated by his parents, specifying his short lifespan.

EDCS-12100512.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aristia, who lived 2 years and 4 days. Felix made this for her well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-12100512.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aristia, dedicated by her father Felix, recording her very young age.

EDCS-12100513.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Valeria, most sweet, aged 37 years and 7 days. Sossius made this for her well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-12100513.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valeria, dedicated by Sossius (likely her father), recording her age and referring to her as 'most sweet'.

EDCS-12100515.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Buried here in a tomb, born of the lineage of Valerius Anto..., I lived twice seven years, bearing my father's name. Rogatus, born young, Fate willed to take me quickly.
EDCS-12100515.summary: A funerary epitaph for Rogatus (son of Valerius Anto-), who died at age 14, lamenting his short life and early death.

EDCS-12100521.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Secunda lived 70 years, 10 months, and 9 days. Her incomparable daughter made this for her well-deserving mother.
EDCS-12100521.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespan of Secunda and mentioning her daughter as the dedicator, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100523.translation: For the health, safety, and victory of our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Quintillus Felix Invictus, chief pontiff, holder of tribunician power.
EDCS-12100523.summary: A fragmentary inscription dedicated to Emperor Quintillus, likely part of a public monument or honorific inscription, dating to his reign (270 AD).

EDCS-12100525.text_edition: \n Cnaeus Cornelius Cladus Isidi votum solvit\n
EDCS-12100525.translation: Gnaeus Cornelius Cladus paid his vow to Isis.
EDCS-12100525.summary: A votive inscription by Gnaeus Cornelius Cladus fulfilling a vow to the goddess Isis, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100526.text_edition: \n genium villae ae a Publi liberta ora omnium instituit\n
EDCS-12100526.translation: The genius of the villa... P(ublius)' freedwoman... established... for all.
EDCS-12100526.summary: A fragmentary inscription possibly dedicating something to the genius of a villa, mentioning a freedwoman and a patron, likely from a Roman villa context.

EDCS-12100527.text_edition: \n Fortissimo principi Galerio Valerio Maximiano nobilissimo Caesari Valerius Domitianus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Sardiniae devotus numini maiestatique eorum referentibus Lucio Aemilio Rustico et Valerio Rutilio iterum IIviris quinquennalibus decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-12100527.translation: To the most valiant prince, Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesar. Valerius Domitianus, a man of perfect standing, governor of the province of Sardinia, devoted to their divinity, with Lucius Aemilius Rusticus and Valerius Rutilius, twice quinquennial duumvirs, referring the matter, with public funds by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-12100527.summary: An honorific inscription for Emperor Galerius Maximianus, set up by the governor of Sardinia and local magistrates, dating to the Tetrarchy (late 3rd/early 4th century AD).

EDCS-12100532.translation: ... months...lius, son of Aulus, from Falerna, from Telesia, tribune... to his excellent wife.
EDCS-12100532.summary: A fragmented inscription likely a funerary dedication to an 'excellent wife' by a man namedlius, who held a military or administrative position, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100533.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Agria V... daughter of Sextus Julius Su... lived... 17 days. For remembrance... she made this to be set up.
EDCS-12100533.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for Agria V..., possibly erected by a relative, with the purpose of remembrance, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100535.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Quintus Allius Memori, who lived 30 years. Quintus Allius Telesphorus, his father, made this for his most devoted son.
EDCS-12100535.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Allius Memori, who died at 30, dedicated by his father, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100536.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To... Annius Maximus... his wife... Ladi, who lived 30 years.
EDCS-12100536.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for a woman named Ladi, who lived 30 years, and mentions Annius Maximus, likely her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100538.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ateius Victorinus lived about 60 years and three months. His foster-son made this for his deserving patron.
EDCS-12100538.summary: A funerary inscription for Ateius Victorinus, who lived approximately 63 years, dedicated by his foster-son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100541.translation: To the shades of the departed. Clodia Hygia lived 45 years. Clodius Asellianus and Clodia Euphrosyne made this for their well-deserving mother.
EDCS-12100541.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to Clodia Hygia by her children, Clodius Asellianus and Clodia Euphrosyne, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100542.translation: To the shades of the departed. Crysis lived 25 years. Her deserving husband made this.
EDCS-12100542.summary: This is a funerary monument for Crysis, erected by her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100543.translation: To the shades of the departed. Felicissimus lived 7 years, 2 months, and 10 days. His father made this for his incomparable son.
EDCS-12100543.summary: This is a funerary monument for Felicissimus, erected by his father, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100544.translation: To the shades of the departed. [---] Felix lived 59 years and 3 months. Proculus, his most dutiful son, made this for his most sweet, dutiful father, well-deserving.
EDCS-12100544.summary: This is a funerary monument for Felix, erected by his son Proculus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100545.translation: To the shades of the departed. Gaius Germanus Valens lived 47 years, two months, and 15 days. His wife made this for her most indulgent husband, well-deserving.
EDCS-12100545.summary: This is a funerary monument for Gaius Germanus Valens, erected by his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100546.translation: To the shades of the departed. Heracula lived 36 years, 4 months, and 17 days. Saturnina made this for her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-12100546.summary: This is a funerary monument for Heracula, erected by her wife Saturnina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100547.translation: To the shades of the departed. Irena, his pupil, lived 3 years. Her patron made this for his most unfortunate, well-deserving pupil.
EDCS-12100547.summary: This is a funerary monument for Irena, an alumna, erected by her patron, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100548.translation: To the shades of the departed. Iulius Paratus lived 62 years, 4 months, and 4 days. Iulius Hermogenes made this for his well-deserving brother.
EDCS-12100548.summary: This is a funerary monument for Iulius Paratus, erected by his brother Iulius Hermogenes, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100549.translation: To the shades of the departed. [---]ia Saturnina lived 47 years, 2 months, and 10 days. Her husband made this, well-deserving.
EDCS-12100549.summary: This is a funerary monument for [---]ia Saturnina, erected by her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100550.translation: To the shades of the departed. Liciniae Valentine lived 2 years and 1 month. Her parents made this for their well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-12100550.summary: This is a funerary monument for Licinia Valentine, erected by her parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100551.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Luria Privata lived for 33 years and 2 months; Symphorus made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-12100551.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Luria Privata, erected by her husband Symphorus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100552.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcianus lived for 61 years, 3 months, and 5 days; his son made this for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-12100552.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Marcianus, erected by his son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100553.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Martialis, slave of our lord Caius, lived for 62 years; Veneria made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-12100553.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Martialis, a slave, erected by his wife Veneria, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100554.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Quintia Numitoria lived for 22 years, 11 months, and 9 days. Silius Tabernarius for his well-deserving wife, and her parents, made this.
EDCS-12100554.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Quintia Numitoria, erected by her husband Silius Tabernarius and her parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100555.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Docimus made this for his most dutiful mother, Otacilia Itageni.
EDCS-12100555.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Otacilia Itageni, erected by her son Aelius Docimus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100558.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Rutilius Ostesis lived for 57 years, 2 months, and 12 days; Amanutius made this for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-12100558.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Rutilius Ostesis, erected by his son Amanutius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100559.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Sittius Egnatianus lived for 50 years and 4 months; Prima made this for her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-12100559.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Gaius Sittius Egnatianus, erected by his wife Prima, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100560.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tertiolus lived for 14 years, 4 months, and 7 days; his parents made this for their dearest son.
EDCS-12100560.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Tertiolus, erected by his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100562.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria Messalina lived for 72 years, 5 months, and 15 days; her father made this for his well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-12100562.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Valeria Messalina, erected by her father, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100564.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Bitalius lived for 13 years and 2 months; his parents made this for their incomparable son.
EDCS-12100564.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Bitalius, erected by his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100565.summary: This inscription records the name of Publius Vitruvius Coetus, likely on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-12100566.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Ulpius Theopompus, son of Marcus, lived 15 years and 6 months.
EDCS-12100566.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Marcus Ulpius Theopompus, who died at the age of 15 years and 6 months.

EDCS-12100568.summary: This inscription, beginning with the name Zoilus, is likely part of a funerary monument or dedication.
