EDCS-12100587.translation: To the departed spirits. Preiecta lived 20 years, 3 months, and 3 days [...].
EDCS-12100587.summary: This incomplete funerary inscription records the life span of Preiecta, who died at the age of 20 years, 3 months, and 3 days.

EDCS-12100591.translation: [...], the faithful Mukanus, the fisherman, in peace.
EDCS-12100591.summary: This fragment, possibly from a tombstone, mentions Mukanus, described as faithful and a fisherman, existing in peace.

EDCS-12100594.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Annia Haeliae sic Victoriae matri Longonensi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-12100594.translation: To the departed spirits. Aelia Annia Heliae Victoriae, to her mother Longonensis, who deserved well, she erected [this].
EDCS-12100594.summary: This funerary inscription records Aelia Annia Heliae Victoriae erecting a monument for her well-deserving mother, Longonensis.

EDCS-12100596.translation: To Gaius Cassius Blaesianus, of the Palatina tribe, decurion of the cohort of the Ligures, chief of the cavalry, for himself, his family, descendants, freedmen and freedwomen. Tiberius Claudius Actes Eutychus, his freedman, erected [this] for his best friend, according to his will.
EDCS-12100596.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a monument by Tiberius Claudius Actes Eutychus for his friend Gaius Cassius Blaesianus, a prominent figure in the cohort of Ligures and cavalry.

EDCS-12100598.translation: To the departed spirits. Fortunata, slave, [...], 45 years [...]. Crescentilla and Crescentianus [...], to their most beloved mother.
EDCS-12100598.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Fortunata, a slave who lived 45 years, and is dedicated by her children Crescentilla and Crescentianus.

EDCS-12100599.translation: [...] his own / [...] it celebrated / [...] body / [...] sailed / [...] father / [...] worthy / [...] for himself / [...].
EDCS-12100599.summary: This heavily damaged inscription likely records a funerary dedication or a statement about a deceased individual, mentioning sailing and family.

EDCS-12100612.translation: Benenatus lived in peace for 22 years.
EDCS-12100612.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Benenatus, who lived 22 years in peace.

EDCS-12100614.translation: To Caesar Augustus, commander, son of the divine Augustus.
EDCS-12100614.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Caesar Augustus, likely from the early imperial period, though its full context is lost.

EDCS-12100616.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Antonianus lived 9 years, 10 months. His father made this.
EDCS-12100616.summary: This is a tombstone recording the life and death of a child named Antonianus, who died at the age of 9 years and 10 months, with the monument erected by his father.

EDCS-12100617.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Atimetianus lived 55 years, 2 months, 10 days. His heirs and wife made this for their well-deserving husband.
EDCS-12100617.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life span of Aurelius Atimetianus as 55 years, 2 months, and 10 days, with the monument erected by his heirs and wife.

EDCS-12100618.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Clodius Festus lived 45 years. His son Lucius Clodius Saturninus made this for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-12100618.summary: This epitaph commemorates Lucius Clodius Festus, who lived 45 years, and was erected by his son, Lucius Clodius Saturninus.

EDCS-12100620.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Deutera, who lived 15 years. Paezusa and Hermogenes, her parents, for their most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-12100620.summary: This funerary inscription honors Deutera, who died at age 15, and was erected by her parents, Paezusa and Hermogenes.

EDCS-12100621.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Herennius Saturus, who lived 90 years. His son Lucius Herennius Faustillus made this for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-12100621.summary: This epitaph records the life of Lucius Herennius Saturus, who lived 90 years, with the monument erected by his son, Lucius Herennius Faustillus.

EDCS-12100624.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Lucrinius Peptitus lived 68 years. His sons erected this for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-12100624.summary: This funerary inscription marks the life of Quintus Lucrinius Peptitus, who lived 68 years, and was erected by his sons.

EDCS-12100627.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Trofim(a) lived 23 years, 8 months, 6 days. Genus [made this].
EDCS-12100627.summary: This epitaph records the life of Trofim(a) as 23 years, 8 months, and 6 days, with the monument likely erected by someone named Genus.

EDCS-12100628.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Valerius Victorinus lived 14 years. Lucius Valerius Creius made this for his excellent brother.
EDCS-12100628.summary: This funerary monument records the life of Lucius Valerius Victorinus, who died at age 14, and was erected by his brother, Lucius Valerius Creius.

EDCS-12100641.translation: For good memory. Felix, who lived approximately [unknown duration], died in December.
EDCS-12100641.summary: This inscription is a memorial for Felix, recording his death in December and providing space for his lifespan, though the exact duration is lost.

EDCS-12100642.translation: Here lies Iannarius, of good memory, who lived more or less 65 years, rested in peace.
EDCS-12100642.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and resting place of Iannarius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100644.translation: Of good memory, Luxuria, lived more or less 30 years, on the Ides of January.
EDCS-12100644.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the name and death date of Luxuria, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100646.translation: To eternal memory, Marcus Decianus Vigio, of old age, lived 75 years, 6 months, 16 days, 6 hours. Died January 1st.
EDCS-12100646.summary: A funerary epitaph detailing the lifespan and death date of Marcus Decianus Vigio, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100650.summary: A fragmentary epitaph beginning with a dedication to memory, likely funerary, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100653.text_edition: \n Milia passuum XI domino nostro Imperatori Lucio Domitio Alexandro Pio Felici Invicto Augusto procurante Papio Pacatiano viro perfectissimo praeside provinciae Sardiniae\n
EDCS-12100653.translation: 11 miles by the order of our lord Emperor Lucius Domitius Alexander Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, proconsul, by Papius Pacatianus, vir perfectissimus, governor of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-12100653.summary: A milestone indicating the distance to Rome, dedicated to Emperor Alexander Severus and commissioned by the governor of Sardinia, Papius Pacatianus, likely in the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-12100654.translation: 16 miles. Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus, son of the deified Nerva, holding tribunician power, consul, pontifex maximus.
EDCS-12100654.summary: A fragmentary milestone or dedication recording an imperial title of Emperor Trajan, likely from the early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12100656.translation: 6 miles. Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, son of the deified Marcus, nephew of the deified Pius, great-nephew of the deified Hadrian, great-great-nephew of the deified Trajan and deified Nerva, holding tribunician power 15 times, consul 3 times, father of the fatherland, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, son of Severus Augustus, nephew of the deified Marcus, great-nephew of the deified Pius, great-great-nephew of the deified Hadrian and deified Trajan, holding tribunician power 10 times, consul 3 times, father of the fatherland, and [Publius Septimius Geta, most noble Caesar].
EDCS-12100656.summary: A milestone detailing the extensive titulature of Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, with a mention of Geta, likely from the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-12100657.text_edition: \n Milia passuum LXXI Impperatoribus ddominis nnostris sanctissimis et piissimis Caius Vivius Trebonianus Gallus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus et Caius Vivius Afinius Veldumianus Belusianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus curante Marco Septimio Heraclito procuratore suo egregio viro\n
EDCS-12100657.translation: 71 miles. By our most holy and most pious lords Emperors Caius Vivius Trebonianus Gallus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and Caius Vivius Afinius Valerianus Belesianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, under the supervision of Marcus Septimius Heraclitus, his distinguished egregor vir procurator.
EDCS-12100657.summary: A milestone indicating distance and dedicated to Emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Valerian II, overseen by the procurator Marcus Septimius Heraclitus, mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-12100659.translation: 77 miles from the Tower. [Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power 6 times, father of the fatherland, imperator 11 times, consul designated for the 4th time, by Lucius Aurelius Patroclus, prefect of Sardinia, ordered(?)]
EDCS-12100659.summary: A fragmentary milestone or dedication potentially commissioned by Emperor Claudius, mentioning his titles and the prefect of Sardinia, Lucius Aurelius Patroclus, likely from the mid-1st century AD.

EDCS-12100660.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus.
EDCS-12100660.summary: A fragmentary dedication to Emperor Diocletian, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-12100661.translation: Seventy miles. Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Pius Felix [name and titles unknown], pontiff, maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, [remainder lost].
EDCS-12100661.summary: This inscription likely records the completion of a road project, possibly in the reign of Marcus Aurelius, mentioning a tribunician power of II.

EDCS-12100664.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aemilius Aemilianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate proconsul viam quae ducit a Turre Karales vetustate corruptam restituit curante Marco Calpurnio Caeliano procuratore suo egregio viro\n
EDCS-12100664.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aemilius Aemilianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontiff maximus, with tribunician power, proconsul, restored the road that leads from Turris (Cagliari) which was ruined by age, under the care of his procurator, the distinguished man Marcus Calpurnius Caelianus.
EDCS-12100664.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road near Cagliari by Emperor Aemilianus, overseen by his procurator Marcus Calpurnius Caelianus, likely around 253 AD.

EDCS-12300001.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caeciliae Apolloniae vixit nnos LXXVIII Caecilia Tyche liberta patronae bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-12300001.translation: To the departed spirits. Caecilia Apollonia lived for 78 years. Caecilia Tyche, her freedwoman, made this for her well-deserving patroness. To Caecilia [name lost]ssa, lived for 50 years. Caecilia Tyche made this for her patroness, well-deserving. [lacuna] to her husband.
EDCS-12300001.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caecilia Apollonia and Caecilia [name lost]ssa, set up by the freedwoman Caecilia Tyche for her patrons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12300213.translation: Servius Galba, Emperor Caesar Augustus, pontiff maximus, consul designate for the second time, granted to the veterans who served in the 1st Adjutrix Legion an honorable discharge and citizenship, to themselves, their children and descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or if they were unmarried, with those they would later marry, one wife each, on the 21st day before the Kalends of January. With Gaius Bellicus Natalis and Publius Cornelius Scipio Asiaticus as consuls. [This is] tablet 2, page 5, location 18. From Ursari, son of Tornalis, son of Sardo. Described and confirmed from the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the Capitol, near the altar of the Julian family, on the right side. Servius Galba, Emperor Caesar Augustus, pontiff maximus, tribunician power, consul designate for the second time, granted to the veterans who served in the 1st Adjutrix Legion an honorable discharge and citizenship, whose names are subscribed, to themselves, their children and descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or if they were unmarried, with those they would later marry, one wife each, on the 21st day before the Kalends of January. With Gaius Bellicus Natalis and Publius Cornelius Scipio Asiaticus as consuls. [This is] tablet 2, page 5, location 18. From Ursari, son of Tornalis, son of Sardo. Described and confirmed from the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the Capitol, near the altar of the Julian family, on the right side. [Names of witnesses, including veterans from Legion I Adjutrix].
EDCS-12300213.summary: This inscription, a copy of a bronze tablet displayed in Rome, records the grant of honorable discharge, citizenship, and marriage rights by Emperor Servius Galba to veterans of the Legio I Adjutrix in 69 AD.

EDCS-12300283.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300283.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontiff maximus, with tribunician power for the 18th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to those who served in the fleet at Misenum, which is under Calpurnius Seneca, having completed twenty-six years of service and been honorably discharged, whose names are subscribed, to themselves, their children and descendants, and to the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or if they were unmarried, to those they would later marry, one wife each, on the 15th day before the Kalends of October. With Publius Licinius Pansa and Lucius Attius Macro as consuls. From a common soldier, Decimus Numitorius, son of Agisinus Taramonni, of Fifen(sis), from Sardinia, and his son Tarpalar. Described and confirmed. Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontiff maximus, with tribunician power for the 18th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country, granted to those who served in the fleet at Misenum, which is under Calpurnius Seneca, having completed twenty-six years of service and been honorably discharged, whose names are subscribed, to themselves, their children and descendants, citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted, or if they were unmarried, with those they would later marry, one wife each, on the 15th day before the Kalends of October. With Publius Licinius Pansa and Lucius Attius Macro as consuls. From a common soldier, Decimus Numitorius, son of Agisinus Taramonni Fifensis from Sardinia, and his son Tarpalar. Described and confirmed from the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near Minerva. [Names of witnesses].
EDCS-12300283.summary: This inscription, a copy of a bronze tablet displayed in Rome, records Emperor Hadrian granting citizenship and marriage rights to sailors of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum who had completed 26 years of service, in 136 AD.

EDCS-12800216.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerio Frontoni qui vixit nnum I menses V dies XX Valerius Fronto miles classis praetoriae Misenensis IIII Minerva pater Volumnia Flora mater issimo fecerunt\n
EDCS-12800216.translation: To the departed spirits. Valerius Fronton, lived for 1 year, 5 months, 20 days. Valerius Fronton, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, [details about his family or funerary monument], father, Volumnia(?) Flora, mother, [remainder lost], made this. [Possibly for a young child, given the name Fronton and short lifespan].
EDCS-12800216.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a young boy named Valerius Fronton, son of a soldier from the Misenum fleet, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14100369.translation: To good memory. Fortuna, who lived approximately 40 years, rested in peace on the 4th day before the Kalends of August.
EDCS-14100369.summary: This inscription records the death and resting place of Fortuna, who lived about 40 years, in a Christian context given the 'in pace' notation.

EDCS-14100375.translation: To good memory. Flavius Rogatianus lived for 52 years and 15 days, departed on the 15th day before the Kalends of December.
EDCS-14100375.summary: This inscription records the life span and death date of Flavius Rogatianus, likely a Christian burial from the Late Roman or Early Byzantine period.

EDCS-14700005.translation: To his good lady wife, Septimia Musa, who lived 47 years, 5 months, 15 days, rest in Christ in peace.
EDCS-14700005.summary: This is a Christian funerary epitaph for Septimia Musa, recording her age and expressing hope for peace in Christ.

EDCS-16600350.summary: This is a very brief inscription identifying Actes as a freedwoman of the emperor Augustus, possibly marking a tomb or property.

EDCS-20401557.translation: To Lucius Valerius Silvanus, deceased at 55 years of age.
EDCS-20401557.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Valerius Silvanus, who died at the age of 55.

EDCS-20402884.translation: Sacred to Ceres. Claudia Acte, freedwoman of Augustus (dedicates).
EDCS-20402884.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Ceres, likely by Claudia Acte, a freedwoman of the emperor.

EDCS-22100632.translation: To Himilco, son of Idnibalis, son of Himilco. Himilco, the son, erected the statue for having undertaken the construction of this temple by decree of the senate. // (Punic text: Himilkot son of Idnib'al son of Himilkot, ruler over Mytba, chief of Hylky, for building the temple that was set up for the Lady, the goddess Tinnit, that was built by Himilko.)
EDCS-22100632.summary: This bilingual inscription records the erection of a temple and a statue by Himilco, honoring his father and grandfather, with a Punic text providing additional details and names.

EDCS-22100633.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-22100633.translation: Marcus Primigenius, freedman of Marcus, master of the Augustan Lares, restored the temple of Isis and Serapis, with its images and ornaments and the surrounding area, in honor of the decurions Marcus Porcius Felix and Impetratus, his sons, who were designated as ones with aedilitian power.
EDCS-22100633.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple of Isis and Serapis by Marcus Primigenius, master of the Augustan Lares, in honor of two prominent local officials.

EDCS-22100635.translation: Gaius Asinius Tucurianus, proconsul, paved the street that had not been paved.
EDCS-22100635.summary: This inscription records the paving of a street by the proconsul Gaius Asinius Tucurianus.

EDCS-22100637.translation: By decree of the decurions, acting on his testament, Sulcitani erected (this monument) for Lucius Cornelius Quirinus Marcellus, father of Lucius Cornelius Laurus, who was a quaestor, flamen of the Augustan cult, twice pontiff for performing public sacred rites, patron of the town, who was co-opted and enrolled among the five decurias and among the priests of the province of Sardinia, because of his merits in the state.
EDCS-22100637.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Cornelius Quirinus Marcellus, detailing his numerous public and religious offices in Sardinia, and records his posthumous commemoration by his town based on his will.

EDCS-22100639.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aemilia Marcia placed this for Lucius Peducaeus Apollinaris, her dearest husband, for herself and her descendants.
EDCS-22100639.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by Aemilia Marcia for her husband, Lucius Peducaeus Apollinaris.

EDCS-22100640.translation: For Sextus Avienus Calliclus and Aviena Philumena.
EDCS-22100640.summary: This is a dedication to Sextus Avienus Calliclus and Aviena Philumena, possibly a funerary monument.

EDCS-22100641.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cornelius Aemilianus lived 3 years, 8 months, 6 days.
EDCS-22100641.summary: This is a tombstone for Cornelius Aemilianus, recording his age at death.

EDCS-22100642.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Flavius Polycarpus, who lived 26 years, 4 months, 1 day. His unfortunate husband Valeria Quadratilla (placed this) for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-22100642.summary: This funerary dedication records the life and death of Flavius Polycarpus and was set up by his wife, Valeria Quadratilla.

EDCS-22100643.translation: Licinia Urbana, on her own initiative, took care to have this made for her son Sextus Iulius Campirie[---], son of Sextus, of the Voltinian tribe.
EDCS-22100643.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Licinia Urbana to her son, Sextus Iulius Campirie, and was likely erected in the Imperial period.

EDCS-22100644.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Calpurnius Stablarius made this tomb for his wife, Polibia Saturnina, who lived 29 years, a well-deserving woman.
EDCS-22100644.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Calpurnius Stablarius for his wife, Polibia Saturnina, who died at the age of 29, probably in the Imperial period.

EDCS-22100645.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Spatalus, freedman of Augustus, made this for his well-deserving stepson Scribonius Felix, who lived 16 years and 11 months.
EDCS-22100645.summary: This tombstone records the death of Scribonius Felix, aged 16 years and 11 months, and was erected by his stepfather, Spatalus, a freedman of the emperor.

EDCS-22100654.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Zosime, wife of Gaius Iulius Aponianus, who lived 49 years and served 28 years in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, had this made for him, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22100654.summary: This funerary inscription honors Gaius Iulius Aponianus, who lived 49 years and served in the Misenum fleet, and was erected by his wife Zosime.

EDCS-22100655.translation: Claudia Proposis, freedwoman of Augustus, made this for her well-deserving companion Niso, companion of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus.
EDCS-22100655.summary: This monument was erected by Claudia Proposis, a freedwoman of the emperor, for her deceased partner Niso, who was a companion of Emperor Claudius.

EDCS-22100656.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Farsonius Occiarius, a soldier with 20 years of service, who lived 40 years.
EDCS-22100656.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Farsonius Occiarius, a soldier with 20 years of service who died at the age of 40.

EDCS-22100657.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Primitius and Gemellina made this for their most beloved daughter Urbana, who lived 18 years, 8 months, and 5 days, a well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-22100657.summary: This tombstone was erected by parents Primitius and Gemellina for their daughter Urbana, who died at 18 years, 8 months, and 5 days.

EDCS-22100659.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-22100659.translation: To Juno, sacred. For Favonia Vera, daughter of Marcus, who donated this house in Karalis to the Norensian people. Marcus Favonius Callistus, the first Augustalis and perpetual Augustalis by decree of the decurions, [made this] in honor of his most dutiful daughter for her munificence, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22100659.summary: This inscription records the donation of a house by Favonia Vera to the people of Noreia and the subsequent erection of a shrine to Juno by her father, Marcus Favonius Callistus, a prominent local official.

EDCS-22100661.translation: Gaius Mucius Scaevola, proconsul, made his own ornaments/decorations with his own money.
EDCS-22100661.summary: This inscription records that Gaius Mucius Scaevola, a proconsul, financed public decorations at his own expense.

EDCS-22100662.translation: Statorianus, freedman of Augustus, made this for his most dutiful wife Aelia Philete, who lived 32 years.
EDCS-22100662.summary: This funerary inscription was made by Statorianus, an imperial freedman, for his devoted wife Aelia Philete, who died at the age of 32.

EDCS-22100664.translation: To the departed spirits. Valerius Felix, who lived for 60 years. His sons Valerius Felix, Valerius Venustus, and [---]ius Felix made this for their well-deserving father. To the departed spirits. Saturnuna, who lived for 19 years. Felix made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-22100664.summary: This is a double funerary monument from Roman Italy, recording the death of Valerius Felix and Saturnuna, with the latter being made by her husband Felix.

EDCS-22100668.translation: Lucifer rests in peace on the fourth day before the Kalends of December.
EDCS-22100668.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription from Roman North Africa, recording the death of Lucifer on December 2nd.

EDCS-22100670.translation: Sacred to Aesculapius Augustus. Lucius Julius Mario, master of the Augustales and attendant of the consuls, erected this at his own expense.
EDCS-22100670.summary: This is a dedication to the god Aesculapius from Roman Italy, made by Lucius Julius Mario, a local magistrate, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100675.translation: A gift to Liber, from the will of Marcus Erenius Faustus.
EDCS-22100675.summary: This is a dedication to the god Liber from Roman Italy, recorded as a testamentary gift from Marcus Erenius Faustus.

EDCS-22100676.text_edition: \n Deae Sanctae A T Serbulus sic votum solvit votum libens animo\n
EDCS-22100676.translation: To the Sacred Goddess Atecina of Turobriga. Serbulus willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-22100676.summary: This is a votive inscription from Roman Spain, where Serbulus dedicates a vow to the local goddess Atecina.

EDCS-22100679.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, holding tribunician power for the second time. [---] of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus. [---]
EDCS-22100679.summary: This is a fragment of an imperial dedication, likely from Roman Asia Minor, honouring Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius and Lucius Septimius Severus.

EDCS-22100683.translation: To the departed spirits. For Atilia Pomptilla, daughter of Lucius, his dearest mother, and for Lucius Cassius Philippus, her father, his holy parents. Lucius Atilius Felix [---] and Lucius Atilius Eutychus, freedmen, made this for themselves and their descendants. [---] for the good, well.
EDCS-22100683.summary: This is a funerary inscription from Roman Italy, recording the deceased Atilia Pomptilla and Lucius Cassius Philippus, with the monument erected by their freedmen and sons.

EDCS-22100699.translation: To Lucius Baebius Aurelius Iuncinus, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe, procurator of inheritances, procurator of the Emperor, prefect of the province of Sardinia, prefect of the transport service for 200,000 sesterces, prefect of the transport service for 100,000 sesterces, procurator for the grain supply at Ostia for 60,000 sesterces, procurator of the library for 60,000 sesterces, from the people of Laurentum and Lavinium. Quintus Montanius Pollio, decurion of the mounted attendants, his attendant, erected this to a distinguished and most rare official.
EDCS-22100699.summary: This is a funerary monument from Roman Italy, honouring Lucius Baebius Aurelius Iuncinus, a high-ranking imperial official with extensive experience in administration and finance, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100701.translation: To the health of our Lords, Claudius Iustinus, prudent moderator, placed this.
EDCS-22100701.summary: This is a dedication from Roman territory, honouring Claudius Iustinus as a 'prudent moderator', possibly related to imperial administration.

EDCS-22100702.translation: To Marcus Cosconius Frontonus, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, prefect of the fabrica, elected from the consul, prefect of the I Cohors Antiochensium, tribune of soldiers of the I Italica Legion, procurator of the Emperors for the twentieth tax on inheritances through Pontus and Bithynia and Pontus Mediterraneus and Paphlagonia, procurator of the Emperors for the twentieth tax on inheritances through Asia, Lycia, Phrygia, Galatia, and the Cyclades islands, subprefect of the city's grain supply, procurator of the Emperors for the tax on Gallic iron mines, and prefect of the province of Sardinia, a most excellent and rare governor. Publius Sempronius Victor, optio of the praetorian guard, erected this to his outstanding and most rare governor.
EDCS-22100702.summary: This is a funerary monument from Roman Asia Minor, honouring Marcus Cosconius Frontonus, a highly decorated imperial official with a distinguished career in military and administrative posts, erected by his subordinate Publius Sempronius Victor.

EDCS-22100703.translation: To Marcus Cosconius Frontonus, son of Marcus, of the Pollian tribe, prefect of the engineers, promoted from the consul, prefect of the Ist cohort of Antiochenes, military tribune of the I Italica legion, procurator of the Emperors for the twentieth tax on inheritances throughout Pontus and Bithynia, and Pontus Mediterranean and Paphlagonia, procurator of the Emperors, likewise for the twentieth tax on inheritances throughout Asia, Lycia, Phrygia, Galatia, and the Cyclades islands, subprefect of the grain supply of the city, procurator of the Emperors for the tax on Gallic mines, procurator of the Emperors and prefect of the province of Sardinia, the most excellent and most holy Lucretius, chief clerk of the province of Sardinia, [dedicated this].
EDCS-22100703.summary: This epitaph records the extensive career of Marcus Cosconius Frontonus, a Roman official, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE, with a dedication by his clerk.

EDCS-22100704.translation: To Quintus Gabinius Barbarus, a distinguished man, from the records of the praetorian prefects, prefect of the vehicles throughout the Flaminian way, procurator of the province of Sicily, procurator of inheritances, procurator of our Emperors, prefect of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-22100704.summary: This inscription lists the career of Quintus Gabinius Barbarus, a Roman official, with several procuratorial and prefectural appointments, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100705.translation: To Ruffia Marcellae, a most distinguished woman, his inimitable wife; Sabinianus, her husband, made this against his wishes. To Aurelia Rogata, his dearest daughter, an inimitable girl; Aurelius Rogatus, a distinguished man, and Cessia Sabina, her parents, made this against their wishes.
EDCS-22100705.summary: This epitaph records a double dedication by parents for their deceased wife and daughter, emphasizing their exceptional qualities and the parents' grief, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100706.translation: [Dedication to] Rufus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, prefect of the cohort, subcurator of the Aemilian Way, tribune of the XIV Gemina and XX Victrix legions, procurator of Plotina Augusta, procurator of Caesar Hadrianus, superintendent of the bridge, pontiff of the Caralitani(?), and a quinquennial duovir with judicial power. Titus Cutius [dedicated this].
EDCS-22100706.summary: This inscription lists the career of Rufus, a Roman official with military and administrative roles, including procuratorial appointments and a priesthood, likely dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22100707.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Diadumeno Augusti servo dispensatori Epaphroditiano vixit nnos XXX Docimus Theon Apolausus vicarii domino bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-22100707.translation: To the departed spirits. To Diadumenus, servant of Augustus, his steward Epaphroditianus, who lived 30 years. Docimus, Theon, and Apolausus, the deputies, made this for their well-deserving master.
EDCS-22100707.summary: This tombstone records the death of Diadumenus, a freedman and steward of an imperial servant, with a dedication by his deputies, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100708.translation: Valerianus, a Dalmation citizen, from the office of the praetorian prefects, who lived 51 years and 4 months, [died as a] neophyte.
EDCS-22100708.summary: This tombstone records the death of Valerianus, a soldier from Dalmatia, who served in the praetorian prefecture and died as a neophyte, likely dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100709.translation: To the departed spirits. To [---]poni, servant, [tabulary(?) of Augustus], who lived [---] years. Tecusa [dedicated this] for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-22100709.summary: This tombstone is dedicated to a servant of Augustus, likely an administrator, with a partial record of his life and a dedication by his wife, dating possibly to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100710.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Arrius Laetus, soldier of the cohort of Sardinians, who lived 17 years, 3 months, and 13 days. Antonia Ianuar(ia), his devoted mother, made this.
EDCS-22100710.summary: This tombstone records the early death of Gaius Arrius Laetus, a soldier in the cohort of Sardinians, with a dedication by his mother, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100711.translation: Marcus Epidius Quadratus, soldier from the Misenian fleet, of the century of Gnaeus Valerius Priscus, served for 3 years, lived 26 years. He lies here.
EDCS-22100711.summary: This tombstone records the life and service of Marcus Epidius Quadratus, a soldier in the Roman fleet at Misenum, who died young, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22100712.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Fabricius Ianuarius, chief pilot. Fabricia Primigenia made this for her well-deserving patron.
EDCS-22100712.summary: This tombstone is dedicated to Gaius Fabricius Ianuarius, a chief pilot, by his patroness Fabricia Primigenia, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100713.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Julius Venustus, soldier of the First Cohort of Sardinians; he served for [3] years and 3 months. He lived for 35 years. Julia Venusta [erected this] for her well-deserving son.
EDCS-22100713.summary: This is a tombstone for Julius Venustus, a soldier who served in the First Cohort of Sardinians and died at the age of 35, erected by his mother.

EDCS-22100714.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Mettenius Mercator, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, of the century of Caesilius Hispanus, a Thracian by birth. He served for 19 years and lived for 40 years. Lucius Mettenius, his son, and Lucius Mettenius Mercurius, his freedman, made this for their well-deserving father and patron.
EDCS-22100714.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Mettenius Mercator, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who served for 19 years and died at the age of 40, erected by his son and freedman.

EDCS-22100715.translation: Rufus Valentinus, son of Tabusius, aged 30, with 11 years of service in the Cohort of Aquitanians, lies here. His brother Spedius arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-22100715.summary: This funerary inscription records Rufus Valentinus, a soldier of the Cohort of Aquitanians, aged 30, who served for 11 years, and was buried at his brother's arrangement.

EDCS-22100716.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Rusticelius Felix, who lived for 50 years, a good man. His wife made this for her well-deserving husband. // To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Helvius Rogatus, who lived for 40 years. His son made this for his well-deserving father. // [...] served for 5 years [...] and lived for 59 years, a soldier of the legion [...]
EDCS-22100716.summary: This inscription appears to be a palimpsest or a composite monument with multiple funerary notices, including one for Rusticelius Felix, one for Marcus Helvius Rogatus, and a partial one for a legionary soldier.

EDCS-22100717.translation: [Lucius] Alfitenus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe [...] weighing the earth on private land.
EDCS-22100717.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording Lucius Alfitenus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, likely related to a land or property matter.

EDCS-22100718.translation: For Quintus Gabinius Receptus, son of Aulus, of the Quirina tribe, aedile with perpetual quinquennial authority, flamen of the deified emperors, by consent of the province, pontiff of sacred rites.
EDCS-22100718.summary: This inscription honors Quintus Gabinius Receptus, a prominent local official and priest who held multiple significant civic and religious roles.

EDCS-22200431.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Tiberius Julius Speciosus, magistrate. Tiberius Julius Speciosus dedicated [this] for his father.
EDCS-22200431.summary: This tombstone is for Tiberius Julius Speciosus, a magistrate, erected by his son who bore the same name.

EDCS-22200432.translation: For Julia Vateria, daughter of Vaterius, as flaminica, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-22200432.summary: This inscription records Julia Vateria, daughter of Vaterius, honored as flaminica by a decree of the local council funded by public money.

EDCS-22200433.translation: [...] Caius Quinctius, son of Caius, of the Quirina tribe, [and] [aedile with] curule aedile power. He made [this] for Vateria, daughter of Lucius, and for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-22200433.summary: This inscription records Caius Quinctius, an official, who dedicated a monument for Vateria, daughter of Lucius, and for himself and his descendants.

EDCS-22200434.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Titia Flavia Blandina, perpetual flaminica, by decree of the decurions, with money collected from contributions. The vicus Martis and Aesculapi [erected this].
EDCS-22200434.summary: This tombstone honors Titia Flavia Blandina, a perpetual flaminica, erected by the residents of the Vicus Martis and Aesculapi through a collective fundraising effort.

EDCS-22200442.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Primus, a slave, erected this for his most loving master, Lucius Julius Ponticlus, a negotiator from Gaul.
EDCS-22200442.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Julius Ponticlus, set up by his freedman, Primus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200443.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius Iulianus, a magister clavicularius, lived for 30 years; his father made this.
EDCS-22200443.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valerius Iulianus, who died at age 30, with the inscription mentioning his occupation and his father as the dedicator.

EDCS-22200444.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Aelius Probinus, freedman of the emperor, lived for 50 years and 6 months; Ovicila set up the burial place for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-22200444.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Aelius Probinus, a freedman of the emperor, with his wife Ovicila as the dedicator.

EDCS-22200445.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Ilaritas made this for her well-deserving husband, Gaius Aemilius Succesus. Her son made this for Ilarita, his mother.
EDCS-22200445.summary: A funerary epitaph with two dedications: a wife for her husband and a son for his mother, both deceased.

EDCS-22200446.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Of Antonius, a slave of the emperor, who lived for 49 years.
EDCS-22200446.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antonius, a slave of the emperor, recording his age at death.

EDCS-22200448.translation: Marcus Antonius Apelles made this for Venusta, his fellow freedwoman, who lived for 11 years.
EDCS-22200448.summary: A funerary epitaph for Venusta, a fellow freedwoman, erected by Marcus Antonius Apelles, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200449.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Antonius Calvisius lived for 75 years. Antonius Calvisianus made this for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-22200449.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antonius Calvisius, who died at age 75, erected by his son, Antonius Calvisianus.

EDCS-22200451.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Antonius Felix made this for his most sweet sons, Antonius Veihilianus, who lived for 4 years, and Antonius Fusciianus, who lived for 1 year.
EDCS-22200451.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Quintus Antonius Felix for his two young sons, Antonius Veihilianus and Antonius Fusciianus.

EDCS-22200452.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valeria Mystica dedicated this for her son, Marcus Antonius Saturninus Pisciculus.
EDCS-22200452.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Antonius Saturninus Pisciculus, erected by his mother, Valeria Mystica.

EDCS-22200453.translation: Antonia Felicitas is placed here, aged [3?] years; [her mother?] Rusia...
EDCS-22200453.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph for Antonia Felicitas, indicating her age and mentioning her mother.

EDCS-22200454.translation: To Antonia Licinia, who lived 18 years, 4 months, and 22 days. Licinia Aegle made (this) for her dearest daughter, well-deserving.
EDCS-22200454.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Antonia Licinia, recording her age at death and dedication by her mother.

EDCS-22200455.translation: To the departed spirits. Antonia Musterida, who lived 31 years, 9 months, and 2 days. Vitellius Sardus made (this) for his good wife, well-deserving.
EDCS-22200455.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Antonia Musterida, recording her age at death and dedication by her husband.

EDCS-22200456.translation: To... Ruficinus [?], Sextus Appuleius [made this] for his sweetest wife.
EDCS-22200456.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph, likely recording the dedication of a monument by Sextus Appuleius to his wife.

EDCS-22200457.translation: To the departed spirits. Aquilia Pompeia, who lived 40 years. To the departed spirits. Junius Felix placed (this) for his well-deserving [wife]. // To the departed spirits. Publius Serbilius, who lived 50 years. Aquilia Pompeia made (this) for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-22200457.summary: This epitaph consists of two parts, recording the death of Aquilia Pompeia and the death of Publius Serbilius, with Aquilia Pompeia acting as the dedicator in the second part.

EDCS-22200458.translation: To the departed spirits. Asella, who lived 22 years. Asellus made (this) for his sweetest daughter. // To the departed spirits. Asellus, who lived 77 years and 30 days. His son made (this) for him, well-deserving.
EDCS-22200458.summary: This epitaph records the deaths of two individuals named Asella and Asellus, with dedications from a father to a daughter and from a son to a father.

EDCS-22200459.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Aurelius Graptus, a good man, who lived about 50 years. Eusebia Todote, his wife, made (this) for him, well-deserving.
EDCS-22200459.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Lucius Aurelius Graptus, noting his good character and approximate age, and is dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-22200460.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelia Felicitas, daughter of Marcus. Flavius Ingenuus made (this) for his sweetest wife.
EDCS-22200460.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Aurelia Felicitas and dedicated by her husband, Flavius Ingenuus.

EDCS-22200461.translation: To the departed spirits. Axtinia Sofia, who lived 26 years. Hylas, her husband, made (this) for his dearest wife, well-deserving.
EDCS-22200461.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Axtinia Sofia at the age of 26 and is dedicated by her husband.

EDCS-22200462.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Babullius Urbanus [son of?] Babullius Maro [?].
EDCS-22200462.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph mentioning Quintus Babullius Urbanus and possibly his father, Babullius Maro.

EDCS-22200463.translation: To the departed spirits. Buttis, who lived 28 years and 8 days. Her husband made (this) for her, well-deserving.
EDCS-22200463.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Buttis at the age of 28 years and 8 days and is dedicated by her husband.

EDCS-22200464.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caesonia Soteri, who lived 60 years; her son Iucundus made this for her, a well-deserving woman.
EDCS-22200464.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caesonia Soteri, set up by her son Iucundus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200465.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Calvisius Euticianus lived 5 years and 7 months. To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Saturnina lived 34 years, 8 months, and 16 days; Calbisius Primitibus made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-22200465.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lives of Quintus Calvisius Euticianus and Aelia Saturnina, set up by her husband Calbisius Primitibus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200466.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. His mother made this for Karalitanus, a well-deserving son, who lived 6 years.
EDCS-22200466.summary: A funerary epitaph for Karalitanus, set up by his mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200467.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. The name [was] Caterbarius; he lived 10 years, 8 months, and 23 days.
EDCS-22200467.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caterbarius, recording his lifespan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200468.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tiberius Claudius [---] lived 40 years. Claudius Primitivus made this for his well-deserving patron.
EDCS-22200468.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius, set up by his freedman Claudius Primitivus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200469.translation: Tiberius Claudius Gemellus, son of Spurius, lived 9 years, 4 months, and 15 days. Tiberius Claudius Actes, his freedman, and Claudia Ianuaria, his mother, made this.
EDCS-22200469.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Gemellus, set up by his freedman and mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200470.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Clodius Clodianus lived 73 years. [---] made this.
EDCS-22200470.summary: A funerary epitaph for Clodius Clodianus, with the dedicant missing, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200471.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Clodius Saturninus lived 11 years. Gaius Antonius Sosius made this for his well-deserving stepson.
EDCS-22200471.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Clodius Saturninus, set up by his stepfather Gaius Antonius Sosius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200472.translation: Cornelius Estutus and Cornelia Salsula, loving each other in life and marriage and equal in harmony until death. Estutus lived 36 years. Salsula lived 27 years. Cornelius Saturninus [made this] for his son-in-law and daughter.
EDCS-22200472.summary: A funerary epitaph for the couple Estutus and Salsula, recording their ages and erected by Cornelius Saturninus for his daughter and son-in-law, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200473.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Cornelius Iustianus lived 50 years. Iulia Granilla made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-22200473.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Cornelius Iustianus, set up by his wife Iulia Granilla, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200474.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Cornificia Gemina, and for Aristia Euthyca, daughter of Publius Gabinius Faustus.
EDCS-22200474.summary: This funerary inscription honors at least two women, Cornificia Gemina and Aristia Euthyca, likely related to the dedicator Publius Gabinius Faustus.

EDCS-22200475.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Dorotia Simplicissima, who lived 28 years.
EDCS-22200475.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Dorotia Simplicissima, recording her name and lifespan of 28 years.

EDCS-22200476.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Hennius Simphorus, who lived 65 years and 6 months. His son set this up for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-22200476.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Marcus Hennius Simphorus (65 years, 6 months) and states it was erected by his son.

EDCS-22200477.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Epaphra, aged 72. Charis, his granddaughter, placed this for her grandfather while he was alive.
EDCS-22200477.summary: This funerary inscription records the age of Epaphra (72) and notes it was set up for him by his granddaughter Charis during his lifetime.

EDCS-22200478.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tyche made this for Erotus, slave of Domitia Domitiana, her most beloved husband.
EDCS-22200478.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Tyche to her husband Erotus, who was the slave of Domitia Domitiana.

EDCS-22200479.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Pulchriana, daughter of Marcus, made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-22200479.summary: This funerary inscription records that Pulchriana, daughter of Marcus, dedicated it to her well-deserving husband.

EDCS-22200754.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Eucarpia, his most dutiful mother, who lived 26 years. Marcus Herennius Aristophanes made this for his well-deserving mother.
EDCS-22200754.summary: This funerary inscription honors Eucarpia, the mother of Marcus Herennius Aristophanes, recording her age as 26 years.

EDCS-22200755.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Quintus Favonius, their son, and for Pompeia, his most dutiful mother.
EDCS-22200755.summary: This funerary inscription honors both a son, Quintus Favonius, and his mother, Pompeia.

EDCS-22200756.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Faustina, his rarest, incomparable, and sweetest wife, who lived 16 years and 7 months, he made this for her well-deserving spirit. To the spirits of the departed. For Prisca, her sweetest daughter, who lived 11 years, 6 months, and 17 days, her parents Cornelianus and Tantilia. To the spirits of the departed, sacred. For Tantilia Caesarum, who lived 41 years. Her husband Cornelianus made this for her well-deserving spirit. Cornelianus Caesarum, who lived 80 years, well-deserving.
EDCS-22200756.summary: This complex funerary monument records three individuals: Faustina, her daughter Prisca, and her husband Cornelianus, with separate dedications and lifespans.

EDCS-22200758.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Fortunatus made this for Messia, his most excellent mother-in-law.
EDCS-22200758.summary: This funerary inscription records that Fortunatus dedicated the monument to his excellent mother-in-law, Messia.

EDCS-22200759.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Epicteis, wife of [G]argilius [G]ianus, for 36 years. To [---]VEGS[---], to whom Julianus, son, made this for his father, 40 years. To [---]AFEPO[---], to whom Sex(tus) Ver(---) Iulianus made this for his very deserving wife.
EDCS-22200759.summary: This is a funerary monument with three inscriptions, likely a columbarium or a shared tomb, commemorating at least three individuals and their family members.

EDCS-22200760.translation: master of horses, for 6 months. Geminius Vitalis, his firstborn son, made this memorial for himself while alive, in which Geminius Ursinius and Seia Ursa, his grandmother, are also placed, and for freedmen and freedwomen of both sexes and their descendants.
EDCS-22200760.summary: This is a funerary inscription detailing the construction of a tomb by Geminius Vitalis for himself and his family, including his son and grandmother, with provisions for freedpersons and their descendants.

EDCS-22200761.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Herennius Castus lived for 16 years and two months. He met his fate.
EDCS-22200761.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Lucius Herennius Castus, who died at the age of 16 years and 2 months.

EDCS-22200762.translation: Marcus Herennius Faustus, to his mother.
EDCS-22200762.summary: This is a dedication by Marcus Herennius Faustus to his mother, likely on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-22200763.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Herennius Felix lived for 35 years. Lucius Herennius Felix, his father, made this for his most dutiful son.
EDCS-22200763.summary: This is a funerary inscription where a father, Lucius Herennius Felix, dedicates a monument to his deceased son, also named Lucius Herennius Felix, who died at 35.

EDCS-22200764.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Herennius Fuscus, who lived 65 years. Iunia Thallusa made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-22200764.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Iunia Thallusa dedicating a tomb to her husband, Lucius Herennius Fuscus, who lived 65 years.

EDCS-22200765.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Herennius Annianus lived for 18 years and two months. Herennia Saturnia, his mother, made this for her most dutiful, well-deserving son.
EDCS-22200765.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Quintus Herennius Annianus at the age of 18 years and 2 months, with his mother Herennia Saturnia dedicating the monument.

EDCS-22200768.translation: Ianuarius Honorali, for his dutiful mother, made this for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-22200768.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Ianuarius Honorali dedicating a monument for his mother and for himself and his descendants.

EDCS-22200769.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ianuarius lived for 16 years and 10 months. Postumius Sertinianus, his foster-father, and Ianuaria, his mother, made this for him, well-deserving.
EDCS-22200769.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Ianuarius, who died at 16 years and 10 months, with a dedication by his foster-father and mother.

EDCS-22200770.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tiberius Iulius Euplus made this for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-22200770.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Tiberius Iulius Euplus dedicates a monument for himself and his posterity.

EDCS-22200771.translation: Sextus Julius Phyricus made this monument for Grama, his stepdaughter, who lived 19 years.
EDCS-22200771.summary: A funerary monument erected by Sextus Julius Phyricus for his stepdaughter Grama, who died at age 19.

EDCS-22200772.translation: To Gaius Julius Saturninus, son of Gaius, who lived 5 years and 4 months. Gaius Julius Agathas made this for his son and for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-22200772.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Gaius Julius Agathas to his son Gaius Julius Saturninus and for himself and his descendants, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200773.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Julia Fortunata, also called Epangielosa, who lived 14 years. Gaius Lollius Crescentianus made this for an excellent, well-deserving woman.
EDCS-22200773.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Gaius Lollius Crescentianus to his wife Julia Fortunata, also known as Epangielosa, who died at age 14.

EDCS-22200774.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. [Name missing] Iulianus, dearest, dutiful infant, lived 5 years. A dutiful father made this.
EDCS-22200774.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young child named Iulianus, erected by his devoted father, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200775.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Numerius Iunius Capitolinus, who lived 12 years. Numerius Iunius Fatta made this.
EDCS-22200775.summary: A funerary epitaph for Numerius Iunius Capitolinus, erected by Numerius Iunius Fatta, recording the deceased's age of 12.

EDCS-22200776.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Iunius Felix lived 50 years. His son set this up with his own money, for a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22200776.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iunius Felix, erected by his son who paid for it, noting the father's age of 50.

EDCS-22200779.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Licinius Aufidianus lived 30 years. His daughter made this for her well-deserving father.
EDCS-22200779.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a daughter to her father, Lucius Licinius Aufidianus, who died at age 30.

EDCS-22200780.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Licinius Felix lived 22 years. His son Marcus Licinius Victor made this. To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Licinius Victor lived 70 years. His son set this up for a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22200780.summary: A complex epitaph recording the deaths of both Marcus Licinius Felix (age 22) and his father Marcus Licinius Victor (age 70), both set up by the son Victor.

EDCS-22200781.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Licinia Pompeia, who lived 13 years. Sulpicia Insulana, mother, made this for her most dutiful daughter. To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Blossius Faustinus, son, lived [age missing] years and 4 months. Sulpicia Insulana made this for her most dutiful son.
EDCS-22200781.summary: A funerary monument with two parts, one for Sulpicia Insulana's daughter Licinia Pompeia (age 13) and another for her son Lucius Blossius Faustinus, both erected by the mother.

EDCS-22200782.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Lucretius Felix, who lived 20 years and [space missing] months. May the earth rest lightly upon you.
EDCS-22200782.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucretius Felix, who died at age 20 years and an incomplete number of months, with a wish for a light earth covering him.

EDCS-22200783.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucretia Fortunata made this for her brother Lucretius Fortunatus, who lived 15 years, 5 months. She made this for him, well-deserving. To the spirits of the departed. For Lucretia Priaeris, who lived 44 years, for her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-22200783.summary: This funerary monument records the deaths of Lucretius Fortunatus and Lucretia Priaeris, with familial dedications.

EDCS-22200784.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcella Patulca, slave of Eutychianus, who lived 40 years. Silvanus made this for his dearest, well-deserving wife.
EDCS-22200784.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Marcella Patulca, a slave, and is dedicated by her husband Silvanus.

EDCS-22200786.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Montana, his partner. [He/She] made this.
EDCS-22200786.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication to Montana by her partner.

EDCS-22200787.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gaius Octavius Iulianus, son of Octavius, who lived 2 years, [illegible months], 14 days.
EDCS-22200787.summary: This funerary inscription records the short life of Gaius Octavius Iulianus, son of Octavius.

EDCS-22200788.translation: For their well-deserving partner Marcus Octavius Oceanus. Octavia Prima and her son Crescens made this.
EDCS-22200788.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by Octavia Prima and her son Crescens to Marcus Octavius Oceanus, their partner.

EDCS-22200789.translation: To the spirits of the departed. In memory of Octavia Heuresis. Iulia Heuresis made this for her most beloved mother.
EDCS-22200789.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by Iulia Heuresis to her mother Octavia Heuresis.

EDCS-22200790.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Paccia Ingenua, who lived 10 years, 5 months, 22 days, for her dearest daughter. Paccius Coillius and his mother Antonia T[illegible]pia made this for themselves and their descendants.
EDCS-22200790.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Paccia Ingenua, dedicated by her daughter, and also serves as a dedication for Paccius Coillius and his mother.

EDCS-22200791.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Papirius, son of Spurius, who lived 32 years. Manlia Ingenua, his mother, [made this].
EDCS-22200791.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Marcus Papirius, son of Spurius, and is dedicated by his mother Manlia Ingenua.

EDCS-22200793.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Petronius Fortunatus, who lived 39 years, 7 months, 6 days. Syra made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-22200793.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Petronius Fortunatus and is dedicated by his wife Syra.

EDCS-22200794.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Pisidius Montanus, who lived 31 years, 3 months, 9 days. Licinia Crescentilla made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-22200794.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Marcus Pisidius Montanus and is dedicated by his wife Licinia Crescentilla.

EDCS-22200795.translation: To the departed spirits. Pompeius Felix lived for 17 years and 57 days. His parents made this for their dearest son.
EDCS-22200795.summary: A tombstone dedicated to a young man named Pompeius Felix, erected by his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200796.translation: To the departed spirits. Pomponia Crescentilla, daughter of Caius, made this for her excellent father, Pomponius Crescentio, son of Caius.
EDCS-22200796.summary: A tombstone set up by a daughter for her father, indicating a family relationship and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200798.translation: To the departed spirits. Hermes made this for Restuta, his well-deserving mother. She lived for 60 years.
EDCS-22200798.summary: A tombstone erected by Hermes for his mother Restuta, who lived to 60 years old, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200799.translation: To the departed spirits. Victorina made this for Restutus, her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-22200799.summary: A tombstone dedicated by Victorina to her husband Restutus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200800.translation: Gaius Rubellius Clytius for his dearest wives Marcia Helladis, daughter of Lucius, and Cassia Crassilla, daughter of Caius, and for his descendants. You who read this inscription, remember that you are mortal.
EDCS-22200800.summary: A funerary monument erected by Gaius Rubellius Clytius for his two wives and descendants, with a philosophical admonition, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200801.translation: To the departed spirits. Rufina lived for 23 years. Aurelius Fortunatus made this for his well-deserving wife. [---] for well-deserving. [---]
EDCS-22200801.summary: A tombstone for Rufina, who died at 23, erected by her husband Aurelius Fortunatus, with partially preserved additional dedications, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200803.translation: To the departed spirits. Scribonius Crispino lived for 60 years. Flavia Parthenope made this for her well-deserving husband. To the departed spirits. Flavia Parthenope lived for approximately 75 years. Flavia Hilaritas, her freedwoman, made this for her excellent patroness, well-deserving. To the departed spirits. Gaius Cominius Euplus lived for 30 years. His wife made this for him, well-deserving. To the departed spirits. Flavia Euodia lived for approximately 100 years. Titus Flavius Antonianus and Flavia Parthenope made this for their well-deserving mother.
EDCS-22200803.summary: A complex funerary monument commemorating at least four individuals, including Scribonius Crispino, Flavia Parthenope, Flavia Euodia, and Gaius Cominius Euplus, with dedications from spouses, a freedwoman, and children, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22300375.translation: To the departed spirits. Sergius Antoninus made this for his well-deserving patroness, Sergia Melitina, for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-22300375.summary: A tombstone set up by Sergius Antoninus for his patroness Sergia Melitina, also intended for his own family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22300376.translation: In memory. Silvanus lived for 35 years and 9 months. His wife(?) made this for him, well-deserving.
EDCS-22300376.summary: A funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Silvanus and a dedication by his wife, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22300377.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Stertinius Aelianus lived for 35 years, 8 months, and 11 days. Stertiniana Fortunata made this for her dutiful father. To the departed spirits. Iulia Primitiva lived for 26 years, 8 months, and 22 days. Gaius Stertinius Bachyllas made this for his well-deserving daughter. To the departed spirits. Iulia Primitiva lived for 40 years. Gaius Stertinius Bachyllas made this for his well-deserving wife. To the departed spirits. Gaius Stertinius Bacilas lived for 60 years and 8 months. Julius Bacilas made this for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-22300377.summary: A multi-part funerary monument honoring at least four individuals from the Stertinius family, including Gaius Stertinius Aelianus, Iulia Primitiva (twice), and Gaius Stertinius Bacilas, with dedications from children and a spouse, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22300378.translation: To the departed spirits. Stertinius Abitus lived 56 years. His sons made this for their deserving father.
EDCS-22300378.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Stertinius Abitus, with the monument erected by his sons.

EDCS-22300379.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Sutorius Cornelianus made this for his most devoted father, Lucius Sutorius Nicephorus.
EDCS-22300379.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Lucius Sutorius Nicephorus, with the monument erected by his son.

EDCS-22300380.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Sutorius Nicephorus made this for his deserving wife, Sutoria Athenais.
EDCS-22300380.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Sutoria Athenais, with the monument erected by her husband.

EDCS-22300381.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Sutorius Cornelianus made this for his deserving Sutoria Euporis.
EDCS-22300381.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Sutoria Euporis, likely with the monument erected by Lucius Sutorius Cornelianus.

EDCS-22300382.translation: To the departed spirits. [Monument erected by?] Caius Terentius Callisto for his deserving Caius Terentius Diocles.
EDCS-22300382.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Terentius Diocles, with partially legible restorations indicating the dedicator and recipient.

EDCS-22300383.translation: [Monument erected by?] Trosius Felix for himself and his family.
EDCS-22300383.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the construction of a monument by Trosius Felix for himself and his family.

EDCS-22300385.translation: Quintus Valerius Athictus and Antonia Recepta [made this] for their deserving father, Quintus Valerius Castinus, and his wife.
EDCS-22300385.summary: A funerary epitaph likely erected by children for their parents, Quintus Valerius Castinus and Antonia Recepta.

EDCS-22300386.translation: To the departed spirits. His mother made this for Marcus Valerius Benerianus, who lived 17 years. To the departed spirits. Clodia Beneria lived 80 years.
EDCS-22300386.summary: Two funerary epitaphs side-by-side: one for Marcus Valerius Benerianus erected by his mother, and another for Clodia Beneria.

EDCS-22300387.translation: To the departed spirits. His brother made this for Caius Valerius Victoricus, who lived 40 years and 2 months.
EDCS-22300387.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Caius Valerius Victoricus, with the monument erected by his brother.

EDCS-22300388.translation: To the departed spirits. Her husband made this for Valeria, his deserving wife, who lived 40 years.
EDCS-22300388.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Valeria, with the monument erected by her husband.

EDCS-22300389.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valeria Casta lived 36 years. Valeria Restituta, her sister, and Valerius Atimetus made this for their well-deserving wife and mother.
EDCS-22300389.summary: This is a tombstone for Valeria Casta, set up by her sister and atimetus, likely her husband, dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-22300390.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valeriae Variae lived 23 years, 10 months, and 7 days. Ducenius Fastus made this for his incomparable wife.
EDCS-22300390.summary: This is a tombstone for Valeria Varia, erected by her husband Ducenius Fastus, dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-22300391.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Verna, slave of Aulus Helvidius, lived 40 years. Vera made this for her well-deserving comrade.
EDCS-22300391.summary: This is a tombstone for Verna, a slave, erected by Vera, identified as her 'contubernalis' (a term for a female partner or comrade), dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-22300392.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Vetur(ius?) [---]rinus Max[---] made this for his well-deserving wife. She lived 45 years. C[---] also made this for her well-deserving.
EDCS-22300392.summary: This is a tombstone for an unnamed woman, erected by her husband Vetur(ius?) [---]rinus Max[---] and another individual, dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-22300393.translation: Titus Vinius Beryllus made a temple for his own Security during his lifetime, for himself and his descendants. // Of Titus Vinius Beryllus, son of Titus. // Also of Titus Vinius Beryllus, the father.
EDCS-22300393.summary: This is a dedication of a temple to the goddess Securitas, built by Titus Vinius Beryllus for himself, his descendants, and his father, dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-22300394.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Urbanus lived 60 years. His daughter made this for her deserving father.
EDCS-22300394.summary: This is a tombstone for Urbanus, erected by his daughter, dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-22300395.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aulo Papilio [---]ere lived 3[--] years. [---]enta[---] [---] it for her husband [---]
EDCS-22300395.summary: This is a tombstone fragment likely for a woman named Aulo Papilio [---]ere, erected by an unnamed person for her husband, dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-22300396.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Aleitenius Diadumenus, who lived 10[--] years. His son [---]aresia [---]m[---]
EDCS-22300396.summary: This is a tombstone fragment for Lucius Aleitenius Diadumenus, erected by his son, dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-22300407.translation: For Sronius Ingenuus and Primigenius Antonius and Artemis(i)us.
EDCS-22300407.summary: This is a dedication or funerary text mentioning three individuals, likely patrons or recipients of a benefaction, dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-22300417.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely a tombstone, mentioning an individual whose name ends in -tius, dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-22300418.translation: To Habedea of good memory, who lived about 20 years, rested in peace, having been laid to rest.
EDCS-22300418.summary: This inscription records the death of Habedea, who lived approximately 20 years, likely from a funerary monument.

EDCS-22300424.translation: After thirty years, here Apici rests in honor. Behold, the one name, our lord and the council ordered.
EDCS-22300424.summary: This fragmented inscription likely records the burial and honor of someone named Apici after a period of time, possibly in a tomb or a public place.

EDCS-22300427.translation: In this tomb rests Bonifatius, bishop, of holy memory, who lived about 60 years and sat on the cathedra for 7 years and 4 months. He rested in peace on the 16th day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-22300427.summary: This inscription is a tomb epitaph for Bonifatius, a bishop, detailing his age, tenure, and date of death.

EDCS-22300431.translation: To Fortunatus of good memory, who lived about 40 years. He rested in peace on the Nones of November.
EDCS-22300431.summary: This is a tomb epitaph for Fortunatus, stating his approximate age and date of death.

EDCS-22300438.summary: This very fragmented inscription likely records a dedication or memorial, possibly a funerary one, mentioning a woman named Maria and a maker.

EDCS-22300442.translation: To Proiectus of good memory, who lived 23 years. He departed on the 7th day before the Kalends of February in peace.
EDCS-22300442.summary: This tomb epitaph records the death of Proiectus, specifying his age and date of passing.

EDCS-22300444.translation: To good memory. Renobata, who lived about 25 years, reposed in peace on the 6th day before the Nones of May.
EDCS-22300444.summary: A funerary inscription for Renobata, who died around the age of 25, with a specific date of repose.

EDCS-22300454.translation: To Theod[...], of good memory, who lived [about...] 55 months. [She] rested in peace on the day before the Ides.
EDCS-22300454.summary: This incomplete tomb epitaph records the death of someone named Theod[...], detailing a lifespan in months and the date of death.

EDCS-22400025.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Lucius Iulius Castricus, Roman knight, principal of the city.
EDCS-22400025.summary: This is a funerary dedication (Dis Manibus) for Lucius Iulius Castricus, a Roman knight and city official.

EDCS-22400026.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Burce lived 33 years. Agbadeus made [this] for his most dutiful son. // To the Spirits of the Underworld. Benustilla lived 1 year and 2 months. Agbadeus made [this] for his granddaughter. // To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Benusta of most dutiful memory, who lived 22 years, Agbadeus made [this].
EDCS-22400026.summary: This inscription is a collection of three funerary dedications by Agbadeus for his son, granddaughter, and daughter, detailing their lifespans.

EDCS-22400027.translation: To Gaius Atius, son of Gaius, aged 65, well-deserving.
EDCS-22400027.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Atius, who died at the age of 65.

EDCS-22400028.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. In memory of Claudia Atticilla, a most noble and innocent, most dutiful, outstanding, and chaste, and incomparable girl, who lived 21 years, 5 months, 12 days, and 10 hours. Felix, her husband, and Atticus, to her well-deserving.
EDCS-22400028.summary: This funerary epitaph records the premature death of Claudia Atticilla, detailing her age and virtues, and was set up by her husband and possibly another relative.

EDCS-22400029.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Thaidi coniugi bene merenti vixit annos X fecit CoLI Hilarus\n
EDCS-22400029.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Thais, his well-deserving wife, who lived 10 years [lost]. Cornelius Hilarus made this.
EDCS-22400029.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates Thais, wife of Cornelius Hilarus, who died at the age of 10.

EDCS-22400031.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Enia Isias, mother, who lived 32 years, they made this for her well-deserving.
EDCS-22400031.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Enia Isias at the age of 32, likely erected by her children.

EDCS-22400032.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Iulia Iucundula lived 11 years. Donatus, her uncle, made this.
EDCS-22400032.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Iulia Iucundula at the age of 11, erected by her uncle Donatus.

EDCS-22400033.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Matius Resus made this tomb for his well-deserving sister, Julia Ticena, who lived 45 years. A dutiful brother placed it.
EDCS-22400033.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Julia Ticena at the age of 45, erected by her brother Matius Resus.

EDCS-22400034.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Rutilia Xanthippe. Rutilius Veratianus [set this up] for his dearest adopted daughter.
EDCS-22400034.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates Rutilia Xanthippe, dedicated by Rutilius Veratianus, likely her adoptive father.

EDCS-22400036.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Victoria Caesilla, his daughter, Victorinus made this, well-deserving. // To the spirits of the underworld. To Cornelius Agil[i]s, Victorinus, [a servant] of our Emperor, [set this up] well-deserving.
EDCS-22400036.summary: This monument contains two separate funerary epitaphs, one for Victorinus' daughter Victoria Caesilla and another for Cornelius Agilis, possibly a fellow soldier or associate.

EDCS-22400039.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Cocceius Martialis [dedicated this] to his natural father, Martialis, a servant of our Emperor, well-deserving.
EDCS-22400039.summary: This funerary epitaph records the dedication by Marcus Cocceius Martialis to his father, Martialis, who was a servant of the emperor.

EDCS-22400040.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Of Gnaeus Scribonius, son of Gaius, freedman. ... lived 64 years. // To Claudius Pudentus, lived 60 years. [His] wife. // To the spirits of the underworld. ...
EDCS-22400040.summary: This fragmented funerary monument appears to commemorate at least two individuals, Gnaeus Scribonius and Claudius Pudentus, with ages and familial relationships partially preserved.

EDCS-22400041.translation: To the departed spirits. To Claudia Chrysippiana, dearest daughter, who lived 42 years and 5 months. Tiberius Claudius Chrysippus, unhappy father.
EDCS-22400041.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Claudia Chrysippiana, dedicated by her father, Tiberius Claudius Chrysippus.

EDCS-22400044.translation: To the departed spirits. For Caesilius, son of Lucius, who lived 50 years. Aemilia Veneria made [this] for a well-deserving wife.
EDCS-22400044.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Caesilius, son of Lucius, and was made by his wife, Aemilia Veneria.

EDCS-22400045.translation: For Herennia Helvidia, daughter of Marcus. Claudius Proculus [and the] council of Karalitaeans.
EDCS-22400045.summary: This inscription records Herennia Helvidia, daughter of Marcus, with dedications from Claudius Proculus and the council of Karalitaeans.

EDCS-22400046.translation: To the departed spirits. Gallicanus lived 55 years and 7 months and 27 days. [His] sons made [this] for [him].
EDCS-22400046.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Gallicanus, who lived 55 years, 7 months, and 27 days, dedicated by his sons.

EDCS-22400048.translation: To the departed spirits. Faustilla, who lived 60 years. Licinius Caen's slave made [this] for a well-deserving wife.
EDCS-22400048.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Faustilla, who lived 60 years, dedicated by her husband, Licinius Caen's slave.

EDCS-22400049.translation: To the departed spirits. Licinia Iulia, who lived 35 years. Aelius Fonteius made [this] for a well-deserving wife.
EDCS-22400049.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Licinia Iulia, who lived 35 years, dedicated by her husband, Aelius Fonteius.

EDCS-22400052.translation: [For the] most holy [woman], who lived 37 years, 5 months, and 20 days. Gemellus, a shoemaker, made [this] for a most devoted, well-deserving wife.
EDCS-22400052.summary: This funerary inscription records a woman, referred to as most holy and devoted, who lived 37 years, 5 months, and 20 days, dedicated by her husband Gemellus, a shoemaker.

EDCS-22400058.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulius M[---]sufe, who lived 17 years. His daughter made [this] for a well-deserving [son]. To the departed spirits. Iulius Faustillus, who lived 66 years. His wife [made this] for a well-deserving husband.
