EDCS-46600174.translation: Marsyas, from Neapolis, may you make Decimus Ostilius Donatus wretched, mute, and deaf, just as you respond to a human.
EDCS-46600174.summary: A curse tablet invoking the mythical figure Marsyas, asking for the affliction of Decimus Ostilius Donatus with misfortune, muteness, and deafness.

EDCS-48200132.translation: From the workshop of Quintus Sempronius
EDCS-48200132.summary: This inscription indicates the origin or ownership of an object, stating it comes from the workshop of Quintus Sempronius. The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48300185.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely recording the ownership or dedication of an object or property, belonging to Biricus Agathopus. The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48600037.translation: Claudius Capito's freedwoman Fauxtila lived 35 years. Claudius [?] is buried here.
EDCS-48600037.summary: This funerary inscription records the death and age of Fauxtila, freedwoman of Claudius Capito, and mentions the burial of Claudius [?]. It dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48600038.translation: For the health, safety, return, and victory of the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, consul, and of Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar's son, of the Divine House, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, Minerva, Hope, and Health.
EDCS-48600038.summary: This dedication records prayers for the well-being and success of Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Gaius Valerius Maximus, offered to major Roman deities. It dates to the reign of Maximinus Thrax (235-238 AD).

EDCS-48600053.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or dedication of an object or property, belonging to Lucius Rasinius Pisanus. The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48600060.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or dedication of an object or property, belonging to Sextus Murrius Festus. The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48600062.summary: This inscription records the ownership or dedication by Vilius Natalis, likely on a private monument.

EDCS-48600070.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus.
EDCS-48600070.summary: This inscription records something belonging to or decreed in the name of Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600071.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus. 225.
EDCS-48600071.summary: This inscription records a quantity of 225 associated with Emperor Hadrian, possibly related to tribute or a measurement from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600072.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus. Tax 11.
EDCS-48600072.summary: This inscription records a tax assessment of 11 units associated with Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600073.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus. Tax 23.
EDCS-48600073.summary: This inscription records a tax assessment of 23 units associated with Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600074.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus. Tax 29.
EDCS-48600074.summary: This inscription records a tax assessment of 29 units associated with Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600075.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus.
EDCS-48600075.summary: This inscription records something belonging to or decreed in the name of Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600076.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus. Tax 29.

EDCS-48600077.summary: This inscription records a tax assessment of 26 units associated with Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600078.translation: From the census of Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus, 28.
EDCS-48600078.summary: This inscription appears to record a census figure related to Emperor Hadrian, dating to his reign.

EDCS-48600079.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. German(a), mother, and Honorat(us), father, made this for their son, who lived 9 years, a well-deserving child.
EDCS-48600079.summary: A tombstone erected by parents for their young son, Honaritanus, who died at age nine.

EDCS-48600080.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucus(ti)on and his wife Montan(a) made this for their father, Silvanus the tenant farmer, who lived 65 years, 3 months, and 20 days, a well-deserving father.
EDCS-48600080.summary: A tombstone erected by a son and his wife for their father, Silvanus, a tenant farmer, who lived to be 65.

EDCS-51400894.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Coelius Abdi[t]inus made this for his well-deserving son.
EDCS-51400894.summary: A tombstone fragment indicating a father set it up for his son, with the father likely being Gaius Coelius Abditinus.

EDCS-52000074.translation: From Tower LXXVIII. Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 6th time, father of the fatherland, emperor for the 11th time, consul designate for the 4th time, ordered [this] for Lucius Aurelius Patroclus, prefect of Sardinia.
EDCS-52000074.summary: An official inscription indicating Emperor Claudius ordered a construction or dedication at Tower 78 for Lucius Aurelius Patroclus, the prefect of Sardinia, likely dating to Claudius's reign (41-54 AD).

EDCS-54300016.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Opsilius Conius lived 30 years; his wife made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-54300016.summary: A tombstone erected by a wife for her husband, Opsilius Conius, who died at age thirty.

EDCS-54300021.translation: [...]. Lucius Poponius Celer lived 20 years. FE[...
EDCS-54300021.summary: A tombstone fragment recording the lifespan of Lucius Poponius Celer, who died at age twenty.

EDCS-55702042.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Sulla Annonis, most dutiful, lived [X] years.
EDCS-55702042.summary: A tombstone fragment honoring Sulla Annonis, described as most dutiful, with her age missing.

EDCS-55702043.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aino Libialis, son (?), dutiful, lived 63 years.
EDCS-55702043.summary: A tombstone fragment for Aino Libialis, described as dutiful, who lived to be 63.

EDCS-55702044.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. [N]ius Cracius [X], son [X]
EDCS-55702044.summary: A fragmentary tombstone inscription that likely names a deceased individual, possibly a son.

EDCS-55702045.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valeria M... lived for ... years and ... months. Monument.
EDCS-55702045.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription recording the name of the deceased, Valeria M, and the duration of her life, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-55702047.summary: A funerary dedication to Aurelia Concordia Chresime, possibly a freedwoman or slave, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-55702048.translation: Of Sedecamis, son of Aaron(?), lived 128(?) years. [Meaning of H() unclear].
EDCS-55702048.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name Sedecamis, his possible patronymic, and an extraordinary lifespan of 128 years, likely Roman imperial period.

EDCS-58100148.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning the name Sextus Murrius Festus, possibly a funerary dedication or ownership mark, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-58100151.translation: [He/She lived] 44 years. His brother made this for his well-deserving sister.
EDCS-58100151.summary: A funerary inscription stating the deceased lived 44 years and was commemorated by their brother for a deserving sister, Roman imperial period.

EDCS-58200012.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Asellus, their father. His daughters Montana and Silvana made this for him, his deserving father.
EDCS-58200012.summary: A funerary inscription by two daughters, Montana and Silvana, for their deceased father Asellus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-58700040.translation: Born on the Kalends of April. Baptized on the fourth day before the Nones of April. Here lies Theusebius, the innocent, newly initiated, virgin. He was dear to me, pious, my son, behold, buried. Theusebi, holy, always associated with chastity. He spent twelve months and thirty days plus more in the light of God. Stainless from sin, he was happily laid to rest as a gift. Completed on his birthday and reborn by Christ, Theusebius entered the eternal abode of life, and with the Lord granting a crown, he bears a virgin's state. Rufinus and Severa, parents, for their dearest son. They made this for him well-deserving, in the Lord. He rests in peace, laid to rest on the sixth day before the Nones of May. Here first, my son, here first I dedicated this place as a virgin for a virgin.
EDCS-58700040.summary: A complex Christian funerary inscription by parents Rufinus and Severa for their son Theusebius, detailing his birth and baptism dates, his virtuous life as a virgin, and his death, likely from the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-58700045.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-58700045.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of his country, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to foot soldiers and cavalrymen who served in two cohorts named I Gemina Sardorum et Corsorum and II Gemina Ligurum et Corsorum, who are in Sardinia, after completing twenty-five or more years of service, and were honorably discharged, whose names are inscribed below, to them, their children, and their descendants; and also to those who were unmarried, with the wives whom they would have at the time citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those whom they would later marry, each one woman. On the third day before the Nones of May, when Titus Didius Secundus and Lucius Publilius Celsus were consuls. For Hannibal son of Tabilatis of the Nur() Alb() tribe, a foot soldier of the II Gemina Ligurum et Corsorum cohort, commanded by Lucius Terentius Serenus, and for his wife Iurina daughter of Tammuga, Sordia, and for their son Sabinus, and for their son Saturninus, and for their daughter Tisarena, and for their daughter Bolgitta, and for their daughter Bonasson. This document was transcribed and verified from a bronze tablet which is fixed on a wall in Rome behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near the Minerva.
EDCS-58700045.summary: An imperial rescript, likely issued under Trajan (98-117 AD), granting Roman citizenship and marriage rights to auxiliary soldiers who completed long service in Sardinia, recorded on a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-59500348.translation: For Flavia Publicia, the chief Vestal Virgin, exempt from taxes, in the Portus district, with a fish emblem, belonging to Eudromus of Porphyris.
EDCS-59500348.summary: An epitaph for Flavia Publicia, a high-ranking Vestal Virgin, likely erected by Eudromus, indicating her status and the location of her burial, dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-60000045.summary: A simple funerary dedication to Junia Rufina, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-61300027.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Oppius Restitutus.

EDCS-67400267.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Axiochus, a freedman and regimental slave, and his son Axius.

EDCS-67400268.summary: This inscription honors Egnatuleius Anastasius, a high-ranking official (Prefect of the Vigiles).

EDCS-68500234.summary: This inscription likely records ownership or dedication by Quintus Memmius Pudentes.

EDCS-68500239.summary: This inscription likely records ownership or dedication by Gaius Oppius Restitutus.

EDCS-68600060.summary: This inscription is a dedication or ownership marker by Gaius Marius Euporus.

EDCS-69200237.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Vitellius Felix lived 58 years. Vitellius Felix and Vitellius Ianuaris, his sons, made this for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-69200237.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Publius Vitellius Felix at age 58, dedicated by his sons.

EDCS-69200238.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Cassius Martialis lived 60 years. His sons made this for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-69200238.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Cassius Martialis at age 60, dedicated by his sons.

EDCS-69200241.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Cutia lived approximately 49 years. Her husband made this for her, the well-deserving.
EDCS-69200241.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Cutia at approximately 49 years of age, dedicated by her husband.

EDCS-69200243.translation: Of good memory. Benustus the reader, who lived 48 years, rested in peace on the 11th day before the Kalends of August.
EDCS-69200243.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Benustus, a reader, who died on July 22nd.

EDCS-70400037.translation: To the departed spirits. Honerata lived 19 years; her mother (set this up) not undeservedly.
EDCS-70400037.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Honerata, who died at age 19.

EDCS-70400038.text_edition: \n CEM patrono coloniae decreto decurionum pecunia publica referentibus Tiberio Arruntio Eutychiano II et Diodoro \n
EDCS-70400038.translation: To the patron of the colony, by decree of the decurions, at public expense, with Tiberius Arruntius Eutychianus II and Diodorus reporting.
EDCS-70400038.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a public monument to the patron of the colony, funded by the town council.

EDCS-70500038.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Hort[---], who lived...
EDCS-70500038.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription, likely for a person named Marcus Hort[---].

EDCS-70500047.translation: Gaius Memmius Dareus is buried here.
EDCS-70500047.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription marking the burial place of Gaius Memmius Dareus.

EDCS-70500067.translation: To the departed spirits. Citellia Cris[---]
EDCS-70500067.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription, beginning with the name Citellia Cris[---].

EDCS-70500069.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly funerary, mentioning a person named Stertinius Ce[---].

EDCS-70500071.translation: [---] pious and lived. V/[ere]cunda (her) daughter made (this) for (her) dutifully.
EDCS-70500071.summary: This is a funerary inscription where a daughter, V[ere]cunda, honors her pious mother.

EDCS-70500106.translation: To the departed spirits. Tadius Lygdamus, son of Titus, lived 33 years and 15 days. Tadius Quintanus, his brother, made (this) for (him) dearly beloved and deserving.
EDCS-70500106.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Tadius Quintanus for his brother, Tadius Lygdamus, who lived 33 years and 15 days.

EDCS-70500150.translation: Marcus Cispius, son of Lucius, praetor.
EDCS-70500150.summary: This inscription identifies Marcus Cispius, son of Lucius, as a praetor.

EDCS-70500165.translation: Marcellus the freedman lived for sixty-nine years, three months, and nine days.
EDCS-70500165.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Marcellus, a freedman, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-70500166.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Kassius Caiolus, the father, and Afinia Valeria, the mother, made this for their dearest daughter, well-deserving, for themselves and their descendants. She lived seventeen years. She is buried here. To the good, good things.
EDCS-70500166.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Afinia Cassia Barsana, set up by her parents, and likely dates to the Imperial period.

EDCS-70800372.summary: This is a heavily damaged inscription, possibly funerary, mentioning the name Domitia, and its original context is unclear.

EDCS-70900406.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Nispellius, son of Pipedio, lived for seventy-six and a half years.
EDCS-70900406.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Nispellius, son of Pipedio, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-70900408.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Terentius lived for [number] years.
EDCS-70900408.summary: This is a funerary epitaph fragmentarily recording the name Terentius and his lifespan, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-70900409.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Tarcisius, son of Tarincius, lived for 115 years. He lived for [number] years.
EDCS-70900409.summary: This funerary epitaph records an exceptionally long lifespan for Tarcisius, son of Tarincius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-70900475.translation: to Octavianus, our procurator, a distinguished man.
EDCS-70900475.summary: This inscription likely honored Octavianus, a procurator of distinguished status, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-70900476.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription intended to commemorate Publius Minucius, its precise purpose unknown but likely funerary.

EDCS-70900478.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aurelia Felicitas lived for [number] years, 2 months, and 9 days. [She/Someone] made [this].
EDCS-70900478.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Aurelia Felicitas and likely dates to the Imperial period.

EDCS-71000295.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. [fragmentary text referring to] the sweetest [person].
EDCS-71000295.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription, likely from the Imperial period, that refers to a deceased individual in endearing terms.

EDCS-71000298.summary: This funerary epitaph identifies Zoilus, a shipowner from Cyprus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71000328.translation: To my unforgettable and dearest daughter, aged twelve, who departed the world, her mother Chreste in memory.
EDCS-71000328.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by a mother to her twelve-year-old daughter, who died young, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71000533.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Osuna, daughter of Valerius, lived 20 years. Her husband made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-71000533.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Osuna, daughter of Valerius, who died at twenty, erected by her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71000583.translation: A difficult and corrupt text, possibly related to local deities or family relations.
EDCS-71000583.summary: This inscription is largely illegible and its meaning is obscure, possibly relating to local cults or familial matters.

EDCS-71000587.summary: This inscription is very brief and its meaning is unclear, possibly a name or a designation.

EDCS-71000660.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Hilarinus, who lived [...] years.
EDCS-71000660.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Hilarinus, recording his lifespan which is incomplete, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71000661.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Valerius Iustus, son of Caius, lived [...]
EDCS-71000661.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Valerius Iustus, son of Caius, recording his lifespan which is incomplete, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72900244.translation: Gaius Julius Primitivus of Arela made it. Gaius Julius Primitivus of Arela made it.
EDCS-72900244.summary: This inscription, likely a dedication or a marker, is repeated, identifying Gaius Julius Primitivus of Arela as the maker, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-74700006.translation: In the 13th year of his reign. Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, restored the road which leads from Caralis, ruined by age.
EDCS-74700006.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road from Caralis by Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, dated to his reign, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-74700007.translation: In the 13th year of his reign. Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, grandson of the deified Gordian, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul, restored the road which leads from Nora to Caralis, ruined by age, under the supervision of Marcus Minicius Clodianus, his excellent procurator.
EDCS-74700007.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road from Nora to Caralis by Emperor Gordian III, dated to his reign and supervised by the procurator Marcus Minicius Clodianus, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-74700008.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Traianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, proconsul, father of the country, consul, rebuilt the road from Nora to Karalibus, which had been damaged by age, under the administration of Septimius Heraclitus, his governor of the province of Sardinia and his outstanding procurator.
EDCS-74700008.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by Emperor Decius, administered by the governor of Sardinia, Septimius Heraclitus, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-74800025.translation: [...] for Marcus Aurelius Caro Pius Felix Invictus [...]
EDCS-74800025.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly honoring Marcus Aurelius Caro, with illegible portions and unclear context.

EDCS-74800483.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the beginning of a dedication or an honorific text for Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-76000032.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely indicating the owner or maker of an object, possibly belonging to Publius Fabatus.

EDCS-76000033.translation: From the kilns of Barbus, by Aurelius Heracla, father and son.
EDCS-76000033.summary: This inscription records the makers of pottery, Aurelius Heracla (father and son), from the kilns of Barbus.

EDCS-76000045.summary: This is a simple dedication or ownership inscription for Gaius Utius, son of Gaius.

EDCS-76600118.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valeria lived 15 years, 4 months. Her parents made this.
EDCS-76600118.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Valeria, who died at the age of 15 years and 4 months, erected by her parents.

EDCS-76600172.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius M[...]
EDCS-76600172.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Publius M[...].

EDCS-76600173.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Silvana [...]
EDCS-76600173.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Silvana.

EDCS-76600174.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Marcus Publius C[...], who lived 45 years.

EDCS-77700148.translation: In this place, dedicated by Sestius.
EDCS-77700148.summary: This is a dedication by Sestius, possibly marking a sacred space.

EDCS-79600257.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sextus Elanus Proculus, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, served as a vexillary on a trireme named Salamina, served for 9 years, and lived for 30 years.
EDCS-79600257.summary: This is a tombstone for Sextus Elanus Proculus, a naval soldier who served in the Misenum fleet, giving his service and life span.

EDCS-79600260.translation: To the spirits of the departed? To... Ritus? To... a well-deserving person.
EDCS-79600260.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription honoring a deceased individual.

EDCS-80200061.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Fint[3 S]ilva/niani lived 40 and X years.
EDCS-80200061.summary: This is a fragmented tombstone recording the age of Fint[3 S]ilviani at the time of death.

EDCS-82100035.translation: Luna, Mars, Venus, Amor // Rufus, Rufus, Rufus, Rufus, Rufus, Rufus, Rufus, Rufus // Ianuaria, Pyrrhus, Epidius, Hilarus(?), Secundus.
EDCS-82100035.summary: This inscription lists a series of names, likely representing deities, patrons, and clients or individuals associated with a specific group or location.

EDCS-82800049.translation: X miles from the Tower. Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, Imperator for the 13th time, Father of the Country, Consul for the 5th time, designated Consul for the 6th time, censor, rebuilt and restored. Under Sextus Subrius Dexter, procurator and prefect of Sardinia.
EDCS-82800049.summary: This milestone records the rebuilding and restoration of a road by Emperor Vespasian, noting the distance and the procurator of Sardinia, Sex. Subrius Dexter, likely dating to between 73 and 74 AD.

EDCS-82800054.translation: To the Nymphs. Valeria Modesta, freedwoman of Marcus Valerius Optatus, procurator Augusti and prefect of the province of Sardinia, gave this gift.
EDCS-82800054.summary: This is a dedication to the Nymphs by Valeria Modesta, a freedwoman of the procurator and prefect of Sardinia, Marcus Valerius Optatus.

EDCS-00380333.summary: A brief funerary inscription for a Jewish child who died at the age of 8.

EDCS-03000423.translation: To good memory. Gelianus reposed, faithful.
EDCS-03000423.summary: A funerary inscription praising the faithfulness of Gelianus.

EDCS-03000424.translation: To good memory. Marcelianus, who lived about seven years, reposed in peace on the 3rd day before the Nones of May.
EDCS-03000424.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcelianus, who died around the age of 7, with a specific date of repose.

EDCS-03300373.translation: To the departed spirits. Phrami [Cir]s, who lived A [---].
EDCS-03300373.summary: A damaged funerary inscription mentioning Phrami Cirs and an incomplete indication of lifespan.

EDCS-03300374.translation: Ten miles, with the safety of our lords Magnis Maximinus and Flavius Victor, always Augusti, with Salustius Exsuperius, most perfect man, governor of Sardinia, administering.
EDCS-03300374.summary: A milestone inscription indicating a distance of 10 miles and dating to the reign of Emperors Magnis Maximinus and Flavius Victor, under the governorship of Salustius Exsuperius.

EDCS-03300375.translation: Twenty-four miles, with the safety of our lords Magnis Maximinus and Flavius Victor, always Augusti, with Salustius Exsuperius, most perfect man, governor of Sardinia, administering.
EDCS-03300375.summary: A milestone inscription indicating a distance of 24 miles and dating to the reign of Emperors Magnis Maximinus and Flavius Victor, under the governorship of Salustius Exsuperius.

EDCS-03600029.translation: He was frustrated by his own prayers and those of his parents, and by the wretched, pitiful rites, he built this tomb to the Fates, enduring harsh words.
EDCS-03600029.summary: This is a funerary inscription expressing the grief and circumstances of the deceased's burial, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-03600030.translation: Cestia Antigona, freedwoman of Gaius, made this with a willing spirit for Gaius Cestius Signus, son of Gaius and freedman of Gaius.
EDCS-03600030.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a freedwoman for her patron or husband, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-03600031.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Flavia Assararia made this for her most dutiful and dearest son, Flavius Ingenuus, who lived 5 years.
EDCS-03600031.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a mother for her young son, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-03700346.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely a name on a tombstone or other monument, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04900437.translation: Of the association of Marcus, son of Gaius, and Marcus, sons of Marcus Pontilienus.
EDCS-04900437.summary: This inscription likely records the name of a business association or collegium, possibly related to trade or craft, from the Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-04900438.translation: Of the association of Marcus, son of Gaius, and Marcus, sons of Marcus Pontilienus.
EDCS-04900438.summary: This inscription likely records the name of a business association or collegium, possibly related to trade or craft, from the Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-04900439.translation: Of Quintus Appius, son of Gaius.
EDCS-04900439.summary: This is a gentilicial inscription, likely part of a longer text such as a boundary marker or tombstone, dating from the Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-04900442.translation: Of Gnaeus Atellius, son of Titus, son of Menenia.
EDCS-04900442.summary: This is a gentilicial inscription, possibly a name on a tombstone or other monument, from the Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-04900445.translation: Of Marcus Pinarius, son of Marcus.
EDCS-04900445.summary: This is a gentilicial inscription, likely part of a longer text such as a boundary marker or tombstone, dating from the Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-04900446.translation: Of the association of Marcus, son of Gaius, and Marcus, sons of Marcus Pontilienus.
EDCS-04900446.summary: This inscription likely records the name of a business association or collegium, possibly related to trade or craft, from the Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-04900447.translation: Of the society of Marcus Cai Pontilienus, sons of Marcus.
EDCS-04900447.summary: This inscription seems to record a society or group named after Marcus Cai Pontilienus.

EDCS-04900448.translation: Of Marcus Planius Russinus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-04900448.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or ownership mark by Marcus Planius Russinus.

EDCS-04900449.translation: Of Marcus Pontilienus, son of Marcus.
EDCS-04900449.summary: This inscription seems to record ownership or a dedication by Marcus Pontilienus.

EDCS-04900451.translation: Here lies Bonifatius the religious, who lived about 70 years, in peace, on the fifth day before the Ides of November, in the indictional year.
EDCS-04900451.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death and age of Bonifatius, dated by the indictional year and a specific day in November.

EDCS-04900452.translation: Metatus, servant of Saint Longinus the centurion, be absent from the lord, devil.
EDCS-04900452.summary: This inscription is a devotional or protective charm invoking Saint Longinus against the devil.

EDCS-04900453.translation: Here lies Deusdedit of good memory, defender of the holy Church of Cagliari, who lived about 42 years, rested in peace on the Ides of November in the tenth indictional year, Cagliari.
EDCS-04900453.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Deusdedit, a defender of the Church of Cagliari, providing his age and death date within the tenth indictional year.

EDCS-04900455.translation: Victor, bishop of holy memory, rested in peace on the fifth day before the Kalends of October, lived in peace about 70 years.
EDCS-04900455.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Bishop Victor, stating his age at death and the date of his repose.

EDCS-04900468.translation: To the divine spirits. Verrius Proculus, to his most excellent avunculus Lucius Valerius Tatianus, and to his most rare husband, Rutilia Ammia. He/She lived 70 years.
EDCS-04900468.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication from Verrius Proculus to his uncle Lucius Valerius Tatianus and his wife Rutilia Ammia, noting their lifespan.

EDCS-04900472.translation: Musa died in peace on the Kalends of June during the consulship of our lords Arcadius III and Honorius II, who lived unhappily 20 years and three months. To his devoted servant and fosterer, Thalassus, a courtier, made this for his cherished one.
EDCS-04900472.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Musa, detailing her age, date of death during the consulship of Arcadius and Honorius, and acknowledging Thalassus as her patron and maker of the monument.

EDCS-04900477.summary: This inscription is a greeting or dedication to Asclepiades of Helenopolis.

EDCS-04900478.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmented and possibly corrupt text, possibly related to a transaction or debt, with no clear context for dating or monument type.

EDCS-05000367.translation: Here lies Redemta of good memory, abbess of the monastery of Saint Lawrence, who lived for more or less 60 years, rested in peace on the day before the Kalends of January, in the 12th indiction.
EDCS-05000367.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Redemta, an abbess, providing her age and date of death within the 12th indiction, likely from a medieval monastery.

EDCS-05200288.translation: Have mercy on me, God, according to your great mercy, and according to the abundance of your compassion, blot out my iniquity. Wash me more from my injustice and from my sin. Here lies Gaudiosus of good memory, a devout man, optio and standard-bearer of the Sardean standard-bearers, who lived for more or less 24 years, rested in peace on the 16th day before the Kalends of August, in the first indiction. Oh, what pain you showed to your father!
EDCS-05200288.summary: This inscription combines a plea for divine mercy with a funerary epitaph for Gaudiosus, a standard-bearer, noting his age and date of death in the first indiction, likely from the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-05200290.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Pribatus made this monument for himself while alive, for himself, at the age of 80.
EDCS-05200290.summary: This is a funerary monument inscription set up by Pribatus for himself during his lifetime, indicating his age at death, likely Roman.

EDCS-05200291.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Sacred to Lucius Amurius Pudentianus, who lived for 53 years and 3 months. The heir set this up.
EDCS-05200291.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Amurius Pudentianus, stating his age at death and indicating that his heir erected the monument, likely Roman.

EDCS-05200296.translation: Sent by... to Saturnina, daughter of... who lived 24 years, and to his descendants.
EDCS-05200296.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Saturnina, stating her age at death and that the monument was intended for her and her descendants, likely Roman.

EDCS-05200299.translation: Burial of the holy memory of Stephen, bishop, who lived 46 years, rested in peace on the 4th day before the Kalends of March.
EDCS-05200299.summary: This inscription records the burial of Bishop Stephen, providing his age at death and date of rest, likely from the early Christian period.

EDCS-05200302.translation: Of good memory. Peter, who lived for more or less 22 years, departed in peace on the 3rd day before the Nones of November.
EDCS-05200302.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Peter, stating his approximate age at death and date of passing, likely Roman.

EDCS-05200307.translation: 30 miles. To our lord Flavius Julius Constantius, Pious, Fortunate, Victor.
EDCS-05200307.summary: This inscription records a distance of 30 miles and is dedicated to Emperor Constantius, likely a milestone marker from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-05200310.translation: From Karales they restored... to Marcus Antonius Septimius, their outstanding man, as procurator.
EDCS-05200310.summary: This inscription records a restoration effort originating from Karales (Cagliari) and honors Marcus Antonius Septimius, their procurator, likely Roman.

EDCS-06100299.translation: Of Marcus Planus Russinus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-06100299.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Marcus Planus Russinus, son of Lucius.

EDCS-06100300.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Gaius Utius, son of Gaius.

EDCS-06100302.translation: Of Gnaeus Bulio, freedman of Gnaeus.
EDCS-06100302.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Gnaeus Bulio, freedman of Gnaeus.

EDCS-06100304.translation: Here lies Asterius, of good memory, here lies Iobiana, of good memory, on the fourth day before the Nones of February.
EDCS-06100304.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Asterius and Iobiana on February 2nd.

EDCS-07000530.translation: Felix, who lived about 14 years, rested in peace on the Kalends.
EDCS-07000530.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Felix around the age of 14, on the first day of a month.

EDCS-07000532.translation: Sicius for Saturnina, his daughter, who lived 24 years, for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-07000532.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by Sicius for his daughter Saturnina, and for himself and his descendants.

EDCS-07000537.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Larensis, son of Victorinus, most unfortunate, who lived 4 years and 4 months, made this.
EDCS-07000537.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by Victorinus to his unfortunate son Larensis, who died at the age of 4 years and 4 months.

EDCS-07000538.translation: He made [this] for his most dutiful wife.
EDCS-07000538.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by a husband to his deceased wife.

EDCS-07000543.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Fulcinius Ingeniosus, by nationality Sicositanus, lived 26 years.
EDCS-07000543.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Titus Fulcinius Ingeniosus, a native of Sicos, at the age of 26.

EDCS-07000544.translation: To Quintus Fabius Montanus, who lived 7 years, thwarted and not opposed to the wishes of his parent, he, compelled by miserable affection, erected this sad tomb; fate decreed his death and the taunts of mortality.
EDCS-07000544.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by a parent to their son Quintus Fabius Montanus, who died at the age of 7, lamenting his untimely death.

EDCS-07000545.summary: This is a personal name, likely for an epitaph or a dedication.

EDCS-07000546.summary: This is a short inscription, possibly a good wish or a fragment of a longer text, for Bonifatia.

EDCS-07000548.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Tadius Sabinus lived for 1 year, 9 months. Gaius Tadius Nicephor made this for his son.
EDCS-07000548.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Tadius Sabinus, set up by his father Gaius Tadius Nicephor, and records his lifespan.

EDCS-07000550.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Coelius Junior made this for his well-deserving freedman and adopted son, who lived for 15 years.
EDCS-07000550.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a freedman and adopted son, dedicated by Coelius Junior, recording his age at death.

EDCS-07000553.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Pollux lived for 7 years, 3 months, 5 days. Pollius Savinus and Calpurnia Ostia made this for their well-deserving adopted son.
EDCS-07000553.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for an adopted son named Pollux, set up by his parents Pollia Savinus and Calpurnia Ostia, recording his short lifespan.

EDCS-07000559.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Silvana lived.
EDCS-07000559.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph mentioning the deceased Silvana.

EDCS-07400354.summary: This inscription indicates a location in the Roman province of Mauretania Caesariensis, specifically the city of Tubusuctu.

EDCS-07400356.summary: This inscription refers to Olybrius, a man of high social standing, possibly indicating a dedication or a mention in an official context.

EDCS-08500315.translation: Of Lucius Planus, son of Lucius Russinus
EDCS-08500315.summary: This is a patronymic indicating the parentage of Lucius Planus, likely part of a longer inscription such as an epitaph or dedication.

EDCS-08900411.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Julia Celele, his wife. Lucius Hostilius Felix, her husband, made this for her, his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-08900411.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Julia Celele, dedicated by her husband Lucius Hostilius Felix, honoring her as a well-deserving wife.

EDCS-08900982.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Cnaeus Aelius Gaius, treasurer of the estates.
EDCS-08900982.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Cnaeus Aelius Gaius, a treasurer of estates.

EDCS-08900983.translation: For Euticius, of wondrous innocence and piety, who served well, who lived for more or less 55 years, rests in peace.
EDCS-08900983.summary: This funerary inscription honors Euticius, praising his innocence and piety, and noting his age and peaceful rest.

EDCS-08900984.translation: For Maximus, of good memory, who lived well for more or less 50 years. His son placed this tablet in peace with the Silvani.
EDCS-08900984.summary: This inscription records that the son of Maximus placed this tablet in his memory, noting his good life and peaceful rest.

EDCS-08900987.translation: Here lies Iscribonissa, of good memory, who lived for more or less 28 years, rests in peace on the 19th day of the month of October, in the 13th indiction.
EDCS-08900987.summary: This funerary inscription records the death and burial details of Iscribonissa, including her age, date of death, and indiction.

EDCS-08900988.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely funerary, dedicated to a beloved son.

EDCS-09001400.translation: For Epittitus, of good memory, may God refresh him in peace.
EDCS-09001400.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Epittitus, expressing a wish for his repose in peace.

EDCS-09001401.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or ownership marker, identifying Nestoris as the son of Nettius.

EDCS-09001410.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Musciana lived 21 years, 4 months, 21 days. Her father, Dol, her husband, F., made this tomb.
EDCS-09001410.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Aurelia Musciana and states that her father and husband erected her tomb.

EDCS-09001411.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Istumenius Victor lived 45 years and 5 days. His wife, I., to her husband.
EDCS-09001411.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Istumenius Victor and states that his wife erected it.

EDCS-09001412.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Porcia Staphilia, the dutiful. She lived 47 years, 3 months, 8 days. Her husband made it for his most rare wife.
EDCS-09001412.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Porcia Staphilia and states that her husband erected it for his beloved wife.

EDCS-09001414.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Gigas lived 36 years, 5 months, and 15 days. Aelia Verecunda made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-09001414.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Aurelius Gigas and commissioned by his wife, Aelia Verecunda.

EDCS-09001415.translation: I am the slave of Felix the archdeacon. Hold me so I do not escape.
EDCS-09001415.summary: An inscription concerning a slave named 'me' belonging to Felix the archdeacon.

EDCS-09001416.translation: To the departed spirits. Vitalis lived 25 years and 4 months. Philinus made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-09001416.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Vitalis and commissioned by his wife, Philinus.

EDCS-09400441.translation: To the departed spirits. For Lucius Cornelius Annalis, his father, well-deserving. Lucius Cornelius Felix and Annalis Cornelia Peregrina, his sons, made this for themselves and their descendants. It was fitting for a father of our ancestors to perform the rites, as fate permitted him to live long. Now where is the togate youth, where have the axes now gone? Where do you lie now, abandoned by bright light? The purple that covered you, alas, the earth covers you with a cruel death in only 9 months.
EDCS-09400441.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Cornelius Annalis, commissioned by his sons, lamenting his early death and reflecting on his status and life.

EDCS-09400444.translation: To the departed spirits. For Phoebe, patron and mother, well-deserving. Gorge made this for herself and her descendants.
EDCS-09400444.summary: A funerary epitaph for Phoebe, commissioned by Gorge who identifies Phoebe as both patron and mother.

EDCS-09401082.translation: For Lucius Remmius Exsuper. Claudia Pompeia, his wife, and Remmia made this for themselves and their descendants.
EDCS-09401082.summary: A funerary epitaph commissioned by the wife and possibly daughter, Claudia Pompeia and Remmia, for Lucius Remmius Exsuper.

EDCS-09401084.translation: For Danicia Consulta, who lived 11 years and 6 months. Marcus Danicius Buccula made this for his most sweet and most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-09401084.summary: A funerary epitaph for the young Danicia Consulta, commissioned by her father, Marcus Danicius Buccula.

EDCS-09401085.translation: To the departed spirits. For Licinia Galla. Caius Julius Proculus and Quintus Julius Herennianus made this for their well-deserving mother.
EDCS-09401085.summary: A funerary epitaph commissioned by two sons, Caius Julius Proculus and Quintus Julius Herennianus, for their mother, Licinia Galla.

EDCS-09401086.translation: To the departed spirits. For Marcus Valerius Rufus, father, and for Iulia Kara, wife. Caius Clodius Gallus made this for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-09401086.summary: A funerary epitaph commissioned by Caius Clodius Gallus for his father, Marcus Valerius Rufus, and his wife, Iulia Kara.

EDCS-09700571.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mulciber, likely from a tomb or shrine.

EDCS-09700576.translation: To the Holy Lares, Titus Flavius V___
EDCS-09700576.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the household gods (Lares) by Titus Flavius V___.

EDCS-09700586.translation: Here lies Muscula of good memory, who lived about 24 years; she rested in peace on the 8th day before the Kalends of December in the twelfth indiction.
EDCS-09700586.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Muscula, recording her age at death and the date of her repose, likely from the 4th century CE based on the indiction.

EDCS-09700587.translation: Paula of good memory lived about 90 years; she rested in peace.
EDCS-09700587.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Paula, recording her age at death and her peaceful rest, likely from the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-09700588.translation: Sabbatius is placed here on the 8th day before the Kalends of February, who lived about 30 years.
EDCS-09700588.summary: This tomb inscription records the placement of Sabbatius and his age at death.

EDCS-10701757.translation: Here lies Gaudiosa, a little infant, who lived about three years; she rested in peace. Shalom.
EDCS-10701757.summary: This is a tomb inscription for a very young child named Gaudiosa, indicating her age at death and peaceful rest, with a Hebrew greeting.

EDCS-11600198.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the beginning of a name or epitaph, possibly for Publius Vilius Vami[lius].

EDCS-12100302.summary: This is a simple inscription bearing the name Lucius Hortensius, possibly from a tomb or public dedication.

EDCS-12100310.translation: ___ 15 years and 3 months; here lies ___riensis of good memory, who [lived] about 30 years; son of Lucius [rested] in peace.
EDCS-12100310.summary: This is a fragmentary tomb inscription that mentions the lifespan of the deceased and their relationship to Lucius.

EDCS-12100311.translation: Here rests in peace Ur, who lived [number] years, buried on the 10th day of the 5th indiction.
EDCS-12100311.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of Ur with a date based on the indiction cycle.

EDCS-12100314.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that appears to be part of a religious or devotional text, possibly related to sin and a serpent.

EDCS-12100319.translation: Peace to Iuda, of the family of Arshi, who lived about 60 years.
EDCS-12100319.summary: This funerary inscription, with Hebrew text, records the life span of Iuda.

EDCS-12100326.translation: Faust made this for Lucius Quinctius Antiochus, freedman of Lucius.
EDCS-12100326.summary: This is a funerary or commemorative inscription indicating a dedication by Faust to Lucius Quinctius Antiochus, a freedman.

EDCS-12100341.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or honorific text mentioning Publius Aelius Per[...]

EDCS-12100343.translation: [...]athe / [...]temer / [...] son of Caius, of the Pomptina tribe / [...]met / [...]tetus / [...] of the Papirii tribe / [...] to his sister / [...]e
EDCS-12100343.summary: This is a highly fragmented funerary inscription that mentions family relationships and tribal affiliations, possibly for a tomb.

EDCS-12100361.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a dedication or memorial to Apronius Julianus.

EDCS-12100363.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely part of a larger dedication or funerary monument, mentioning the maker and their mother.

EDCS-12100382.translation: To the good memory of Iomisus the cleric, who lived 45 years, rested in peace on the 18th day before the Kalends of November.
EDCS-12100382.summary: This funerary inscription records the death date and age of Iomisus, a cleric.

EDCS-12100383.translation: To the good memory of Ionisus the cleric, who lived 45 years, buried on the 18th day before the Kalends of November. To the good memory of Dulcitia, who lived 70 years, buried on the 3rd day before the Ides of February, rested in peace.
EDCS-12100383.summary: This funerary inscription records the death dates and ages of two individuals, Ionisus the cleric and Dulcitia, likely from a Christian context.

EDCS-12100388.translation: Celius, who lived 14 days before the Kalends of [Month], rested in peace. Fortunatus, who lived [---].
EDCS-12100388.summary: This inscription records the death and age of Celius and Fortunatus, with Celius's death dated to 14 days before the Kalends of an unspecified month.

EDCS-12100390.translation: Here lies Faustina of good memory, who lived more or less 22 years, rested in peace on the 12th day before the Ides of October, in the 14th indiction.
EDCS-12100390.summary: This epitaph records the death of Faustina, who lived approximately 22 years, and is dated to the 12th day before the Ides of October in the 14th indiction.

EDCS-12100398.translation: Here lies Iohannacius of good memory, who lived more or less 33 years, rested in peace on the day before the Nones of July, in the first indiction.
EDCS-12100398.summary: This epitaph records the death of Iohannacius, who lived approximately 33 years, and is dated to the day before the Nones of July in the first indiction.

EDCS-12100399.translation: Here rests Iohannes, son of the late Iohannes, formerly of good memory, from the tribune, who lived more or less 26 years, rested in peace on the 25th day of September, in the tenth indiction.
EDCS-12100399.summary: This epitaph records the death of Iohannes, son of a former tribune, who lived approximately 26 years, and is dated to the 25th of September in the tenth indiction.

EDCS-12100400.translation: Here lies Laurentius of good memory, who lived more or less 40 years, rested in peace on the Ides of November. Here lies Agnes of good memory, who lived more or less 70 years, rested in peace on the 10th day before the Kalends of August. Dominica of good memory, who lived 11 years, rested in peace on the Kalends of July, in the fourth indiction.
EDCS-12100400.summary: This inscription records the deaths of Laurentius, Agnes, and Dominica, with their ages and dates of rest in peace, including indiction numbers for Dominica.

EDCS-12100401.translation: Here rests Menas, notary and subregional official of the holy Roman church and rector, of good memory, who lived more or less 40 years, rested in peace on the day before the Ides of February, in the first indiction.
EDCS-12100401.summary: This epitaph records the death of Menas, a notary, subregional official, and rector of the Roman church, who lived approximately 40 years and is dated to the day before the Ides of February in the first indiction.

EDCS-12100402.translation: Merula of good memory, who lived more or less 80 years, was laid to rest 5 days before the Kalends of August.
EDCS-12100402.summary: This inscription records the burial of Merula, who lived approximately 80 years, and is dated to 5 days before the Kalends of August.

EDCS-12100403.translation: Here lies Ianuarius of good memory, the sorrow of his parents, who lived more or less 25 years, rested in peace on the 3rd day before the Kalends of January. [---] his mother's [---] for [---] years eternally.
EDCS-12100403.summary: This epitaph records the death of Ianuarius, who lived approximately 25 years, and was laid to rest on the 3rd day before the Kalends of January, with a partial inscription to his mother.

EDCS-12100405.translation: Sabastus of good memory, who lived more or less 20 years, rested in peace on the 12th day before the Kalends of March. Here, Iuhannes of good memory, who lived more or less 10 years, rested in peace on the day before the Nones of July. Iulia of good memory, who lived more or less 70 years, rested in peace on the 5th day before the Kalends of December. Frontosus lived 13 years in peace.
EDCS-12100405.summary: This inscription records the deaths of Sabastus, Iuhannes, Iulia, and Frontosus, with their ages and dates of rest in peace.

EDCS-12100406.translation: Taurus rests, from a noble lineage, always borne towards the stars, he was the first to conquer the province, rested in peace on the day before [---] an example of piety, lived [---] years.
EDCS-12100406.summary: This inscription is a poetic epitaph for Taurus, praising his noble lineage and military achievement, and stating he rested in peace on the day before an unspecified date, having lived an unspecified number of years.

EDCS-12100407.translation: Here lies Thedote of good memory, who lived about 17 years and rested in peace on the 7th day before the Kalends of September in the second indiction.
EDCS-12100407.summary: This epitaph records the death of Thedote, who lived approximately 17 years, and provides the date of her repose.

EDCS-12100409.translation: Here lies Vitalis of good memory, who lived about 17 years and rested in peace on the 6th day before the Ides of January. Here lies Musa of good memory, who lived about 45 years and rested in peace on the 7th day before the Ides of April in the [..] indiction.
EDCS-12100409.summary: This epitaph records the deaths and ages of two individuals, Vitalis and Musa, and provides dates of repose.

EDCS-12100412.summary: This inscription records the name Ianuarius and his age at death, likely a tombstone fragment.

EDCS-12100441.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Sextus erected this for himself, well-deserving.
EDCS-12100441.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Sextus to himself, indicating he prepared his own tomb.

EDCS-12100442.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. His father erected this for his son, aged 2 years, well-deserving.
EDCS-12100442.summary: This funerary dedication records a father's remembrance of his son who died at the age of 2.

EDCS-12100465.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caius Avidius Speratus erected this for his dearest son, Caius Avidius Speratus, aged 14 years.
EDCS-12100465.summary: This funerary dedication records a father, Caius Avidius Speratus, commemorating his son of the same name who died at age 14.

EDCS-12100477.translation: To our Lords Magnentius and Flavius Victor, ever August Emperors.
EDCS-12100477.summary: This is a dedication to the emperors Magnentius and Flavius Victor, likely from the mid-4th century.

EDCS-12100481.summary: This is a simple inscription of a personal name, possibly a tombstone or a dedication.

EDCS-12100518.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Socaras, son of Nercus, aged 40 years. E.FECIT ITV MV V COI VX KARA.
EDCS-12100518.summary: This funerary dedication records the death of Socaras, son of Nercus, at the age of 40, with additional, partially legible text.

EDCS-12100519.translation: Boundary stone, fifth of the Uddadhaddaritani, of Numisius Eutychianus.
EDCS-12100519.summary: This inscription marks a boundary stone, identifying it as the fifth within the territory of the Uddadhaddaritani and naming Numisius Eutychianus.

EDCS-12100529.translation: He served as aedile twice and as a six-man of the Augustan college. He lived 45 years. His most devoted son [erected this].
EDCS-12100529.summary: This funerary epitaph records the public offices and age at death of a man, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100530.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the name and a magistracy of an individual, possibly from a municipal context.

EDCS-12100537.translation: Gaius Apronius, son of Gaius, [---]; Servilia, freedwoman of Gaius, Mo[---]; Gaius Apronius, son of Gaius, [---] Postumianus.
EDCS-12100537.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to list several individuals, possibly family members, with their patronymic or gentilician names, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100556.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Paccius Cornelianus made this for Felicis, his well-deserving kinsman, who lived 48 years, 6 months, and 27 days.
EDCS-12100556.summary: This funerary epitaph is a dedication by Lucius Paccius Cornelianus to his kinsman Felicis, providing the deceased's age at death, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100563.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Tryphon, freedman of Valerius, seeing to it, made this for Valerius Hermesianactus, his well-deserving husband.
EDCS-12100563.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Tryphon to his husband Valerius Hermesianactus, indicating a same-sex relationship and likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100574.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the name and age at death of an individual, likely a funerary epitaph from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12100585.translation: The faithful boy Victorinus departed in peace on the day of Mercury, 6 days before the Kalends of November, during the tenth consulship of Honorius and the sixth of Theodosius. He lived more or less 15 years.
EDCS-12100585.summary: This Christian funerary epitaph records the death date and age of a boy named Victorinus, and mentions the consuls Honorius and Theodosius, likely dating it to the early 5th century CE.

EDCS-12100586.translation: Here rests Martialis of most distinguished rank, of good memory, who lived more or less 57 years. He rested in peace on the day of the Nones of [---] in the twelfth indiction.
EDCS-12100586.summary: This Christian funerary epitaph records the death and age of a distinguished man named Martialis, likely dating to the later Roman Empire based on the mention of an indiction.

EDCS-12100590.translation: Here lies [---] of good memory, who lived more or less 38 years. Zoilus rested in peace on the day of the 17th day before the Kalends of July.
EDCS-12100590.summary: This Christian funerary epitaph records the death and age of an individual, whose name is partially lost, and provides a date, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-12100602.summary: This fragment likely identifies an individual as a freedman of an emperor, possibly Augustus, and is too incomplete to provide further context.

EDCS-12100605.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Claudius Sentius, who died as a child.

EDCS-12100619.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. His father and mother made this for Crescentius, their son, who deserved well, who lived 32 years, 1 month, 16 days, and 9 hours.
EDCS-12100619.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated by parents to their deceased son, Crescentius, noting his age at death.

EDCS-12100623.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius H. Aristius, Lucius H. Faustius, and Lucius H. Gemellus made this for their patron, Lucius H. Faustus, while he was alive; he lived 35 years.
EDCS-12100623.summary: This is a tombstone erected by freedmen for their living patron, Lucius H. Faustus, indicating his age at the time of inscription.

EDCS-12100626.translation: Marcus Scribonius Gallus made this for Metrodorus, his father, out of filial piety, and for his wife Petronia Fortunata, who deserved well, and for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-12100626.summary: This tombstone was erected by Marcus Scribonius Gallus for his father, his wife, and himself, highlighting filial piety.

EDCS-12100629.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the name and relationship of Valeria.

EDCS-12100632.translation: Mai lived 10 years. Her mother made this.
EDCS-12100632.summary: This is a tombstone stating that Mai lived for 10 years and was commemorated by her mother.

EDCS-12100635.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly part of a longer text related to a person named Papiria.

EDCS-12100643.translation: Here lies Johanna of good memory, chaste virgin, who lived more or less 35 years and rested on the seventh day before the Ides.
EDCS-12100643.summary: This tombstone records the death of Johanna, a virgin of good memory, who lived approximately 35 years.

EDCS-12100645.translation: always to be revered, the common people remained... the good soul Leont, 60 days, the eleventh day before the Kalends...
EDCS-12100645.summary: This is a damaged inscription that seems to commemorate someone named Leont, mentioning their age in days and a date.

EDCS-12100647.translation: Here lies Sabbatius of good memory, who lived 35 years and rested in peace on the twelfth day before the Kalends of February.
EDCS-12100647.summary: This tombstone records the death of Sabbatius of good memory, who lived 35 years and died on February 1st.

EDCS-12100649.translation: To good memory, Isportella, who lived more or less 7 years, rested in peace on the 10th day.
EDCS-12100649.summary: A funerary epitaph for a child named Isportella, dating to Late Antiquity.

EDCS-12100651.translation: To our lords, the Emperors Magnus Maximus and Flavius Victor, and the most noble Caesar V. OEX V ER O, governor of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-12100651.summary: An inscription dedicating something to the emperors Magnus Maximus and Flavius Victor and the governor of Sardinia, dating to the late 4th century CE.

EDCS-13302530.summary: A fragmentary inscription that likely records the names of owners or donors, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14100001.translation: Here lies in good memory, Antiochia, who lived more or less 55 years, rested in peace, was buried on the 17th day before the Kalends of February.
EDCS-14100001.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antiochia, who lived about 55 years, dating to Late Antiquity.

EDCS-14100370.translation: Here lies in good memory, Istefanus, archpriest of the holy church of Cagliari, who lived more or less 70 years.
EDCS-14100370.summary: A funerary epitaph for Istefanus, an archpriest of Cagliari, dating to Late Antiquity.

EDCS-14100371.translation: In good memory, S., bishop, lived 10 years.
EDCS-14100371.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for a bishop who lived for 10 years, dating to Late Antiquity.

EDCS-14100373.translation: In good memory, Honorius, rested in peace, of years.
EDCS-14100373.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Honorius, indicating he rested in peace, dating to Late Antiquity.

EDCS-14700002.translation: How broadly we remain in the embrace of the gods, we pray for the gifts of Mercury, of the martyr in good memory, rests in peace.
EDCS-14700002.summary: A fragmented epitaph that mentions Mercury and a martyr, suggesting a funerary or devotional context from Late Antiquity.

EDCS-14700003.translation: ...who is desired, with the sweetness of remembered children, parent is departed from life, and to the sorrow of life, but for the mother to be wept for, the firstborn is of 7 years and ... rested in peace on the 12th day of October, in the indictional year.
EDCS-14700003.summary: A fragmented epitaph possibly relating to a mother mourning her firstborn son who died at age 7, dating to Late Antiquity with an indiction year.

EDCS-14700004.translation: To the most deserving father, Dionysius, who lived 55 years, 2 months, and 10 days. Esychius and Valeria, dearest children, made this.
EDCS-14700004.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Dionysius by his children, Esychius and Valeria, dating to Late Antiquity.

EDCS-20401556.translation: Here lies Basilius, of good memory, who lived approximately fifty-five years and rested in peace on the eighteenth day of March, a Tuesday, in the second indiction.
EDCS-20401556.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life and death of Basilius, dated by indiction.

EDCS-20401558.summary: A very brief inscription likely identifying an individual, Primitivus.

EDCS-20401559.summary: An inscription recording the maker of a monument, Quintus Pompeius Atticus, from Arles.

EDCS-20401560.translation: Gaius Julius Primitius the craftsman made [this].
EDCS-20401560.summary: An inscription identifying Gaius Julius Primitius as the craftsman who made a monument.

EDCS-20401561.summary: A fragmentary inscription potentially related to the imperial family or a person named Germanicus.

EDCS-22100652.translation: The hall shines where the body of the blessed, holy Antiochus rested in the glory of virtue; the work of restoration was done by the ministering bishop of Christ, as a house should be, which Peter the prelate renewed with worship, splendor, and nobility of faith, dedicated on the twelfth day before the Kalends of February.
EDCS-22100652.summary: An inscription dedicating a restored building, likely a church, mentioning Bishop Peter and the resting place of St. Antiochus, dated by the Roman calendar.

EDCS-22100660.translation: With our lords Theodosius and the always Augusti Placidus Valentiniani safe, Flaviolus restored the formerly diverted waters and those denied to the homeland to the people with a pure stream, while Valerius Euhodius, the chief and foremost of the same city, was overseeing.
EDCS-22100660.summary: An inscription celebrating the restoration of water supply to the populace, mentioning Emperors Theodosius and Valentinian III, and a local official Valerius Euhodius.

EDCS-22100663.translation: To the departed spirits. C. Popeianus lived for 12 years. His father and mother made [this] for their most dutiful son, who deserved well.
EDCS-22100663.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young boy named C. Popeianus, made by his parents.

EDCS-22100669.translation: To the good and innocent spirit of Respectus, who lived for 1 year and 4 months. Rogatus the lector made [this] for his most dutiful son in Christ Jesus.
EDCS-22100669.summary: A funerary epitaph for a child named Respectus, made by his father Rogatus, identifying the deceased's age and the father's role as a lector.

EDCS-22100673.translation: To Hercules the Victorious, Lucius Cornelius Felix gave this offering.
EDCS-22100673.summary: A dedication of a votive offering to the god Hercules Victor by Lucius Cornelius Felix.

EDCS-22100674.translation: Larus Carpus, son of Cupitus, gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-22100674.summary: An inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Larus Carpus, son of Cupitus.

EDCS-22100678.translation: Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, consul for the third time.
EDCS-22100678.summary: A fragmentary inscription identifying Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, as a three-time consul, likely a political or commemorative text.

EDCS-22100698.translation: Wife of Lucius Aurelius Orestes, son of Lucius.
EDCS-22100698.summary: An incomplete inscription referring to the wife of Lucius Aurelius Orestes, son of Lucius.

EDCS-22200438.translation: Egnatius, son of Publius, the freedman.
EDCS-22200438.summary: A brief inscription identifying Egnatius, son of Publius, as a freedman (VF likely stands for 'vixit' or similar).

EDCS-22200441.summary: An inscription identifying Sextus Caecilius as a 'vascularius', a craftsman who made metal vessels.

EDCS-22200447.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Antonius Crescens made this monument for his beloved father, Gaius Antonius Aemilianus, who lived 40 years, a good and deserving man.
EDCS-22200447.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Antonius Aemilianus, erected by his son Gaius Antonius Crescens.

EDCS-22200450.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aemilia made this for her well-deserving wife, Gaia Antonia Crescenta, who lived 10 years.
EDCS-22200450.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaia Antonia Crescenta, who lived 10 years, erected by her wife Aemilia.

EDCS-22200757.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Flavia Pomponia lived 45 years and 2 months. Licinius Crescens made this for his most chaste wife, with whom he lived 20 years and 7 months, a well-deserving woman.
EDCS-22200757.summary: A funerary inscription for Flavia Pomponia, erected by her husband Licinius Crescens, detailing their ages and duration of marriage.

EDCS-22200766.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Herennius Saturninus lived 32 years. His mother, Iunia Thallusa, made this for her most dutiful son.
EDCS-22200766.summary: A funerary inscription for Herennius Saturninus, erected by his mother Iunia Thallusa.

EDCS-22200777.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Iunia Pedusea made this for her daughter, who lived 25 years.
EDCS-22200777.summary: A funerary inscription for an unnamed daughter, erected by her mother Iunia Pedusea.

EDCS-22200792.translation: To the Divine Spirits, of Petronera, daughter of Aulus, my incomparable T(h)at(h)ia.
EDCS-22200792.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the Divine Spirits for Petronera, daughter of Aulus, by her relative T(h)at(h)ia.

EDCS-22200797.translation: aged 22, C(a)idius, from the 7th Praetorian cohort, with 8 years of service.
EDCS-22200797.summary: This funerary epitaph records the age and service of C(a)idius, a soldier of the 7th Praetorian cohort.

EDCS-22200802.translation: To Rutilia, daughter of Caius, Ametghes.
EDCS-22200802.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Rutilia, daughter of Caius, by Ametghes.

EDCS-22300421.translation: Here lies Amabilis, servant of God, of holy memory, who lived about 80 years, rested on the 15th day before the Kalends of April.
EDCS-22300421.summary: This epitaph records the death of Amabilis, a servant of God, on March 18th (approximately) and his age of about 80.

EDCS-22300422.translation: Here lies Andreas, of good memory, who lived about 12 years, rested in peace on the 6th day before the Kalends of January.
EDCS-22300422.summary: This epitaph records the death of Andreas, who lived about 12 years, resting in peace on December 27th.

EDCS-22300425.translation: Arthemius rested in peace. Miccina, who lived, rested in peace on the day.
EDCS-22300425.summary: This fragmented epitaph records the rest in peace of Arthemius and Miccina.

EDCS-22300426.translation: Here lies Vernaculius, of good memory, who lived about 20 years, rested in peace on the 16th day before the Kalends of December, in the 10th indiction.
EDCS-22300426.summary: This epitaph records the death of Vernaculius, who lived about 20 years, resting in peace on November 16th, during the 10th indiction.

EDCS-22300429.translation: Here lies Florus, of good memory, who lived about 70 years, rested in peace on the 9th day before the Kalends of February. Here lies Pulcheria, of good memory, who lived about 65 years, rested in peace on the Kalends of January.
EDCS-22300429.summary: This epitaph records the death of two individuals, Florus (about 70 years old) and Pulcheria (about 65 years old), who rested in peace on January 24th and January 1st, respectively.

EDCS-22300430.translation: Here lies Fortuna, of good memory, who lived about 27 years and 3 months, rested in peace on the day of the Nones, a worthy life she lived, her husband closed her eyes.
EDCS-22300430.summary: This epitaph records the death of Fortuna, who lived about 27 years and 3 months, resting in peace around August 5th.

EDCS-22300433.translation: Here lies H(o)norius, who lived about [---] years, rested in peace on the day before the Nones of [---], in the fifth indiction.
EDCS-22300433.summary: This epitaph records the death of H(o)norius, with an incomplete age, resting in peace around September 4th during the fifth indiction.

EDCS-22300434.translation: Here lie of good memory John and Redemptus and Matrona, they rest in peace on the 14th day before the Kalends of April, in the ninth and eleventh indiction.
EDCS-22300434.summary: This inscription records the burial of three individuals, John, Redemptus, and Matrona, with specific dates and indiction cycles, likely from a late antique or early medieval context.

EDCS-22300436.translation: Here lies of good memory Maria, who lived more or less 50 years, she rested in peace on the 7th day before the Kalends of May.
EDCS-22300436.summary: This epitaph records the death of Maria, noting her approximate age and the date of her repose, likely from a late antique or early medieval burial.

EDCS-22300437.translation: Here lies of good memory Maria, who lived more or less 20 years, and of good memory Honorius, who lived more or less 30 years, they rested in peace on the 6th day before the Kalends of June.
EDCS-22300437.summary: This inscription commemorates two individuals, Maria and Honorius, providing their approximate lifespans and the date of their peaceful rest, suggesting a shared burial or commemoration.

EDCS-22300439.translation: Here lies of good memory Paschasius, who lived more or less 40 years, he rested in peace on the 9th day before the Kalends of December.
EDCS-22300439.summary: This epitaph records the death of Paschasius, including his approximate age and the date of his repose, typical of late antique or early medieval funerary inscriptions.

EDCS-22300440.translation: Here lies of good memory Paschasius, who lived more or less 13 years, he rested in peace on the 8th day before the Kalends of July, in the eleventh indiction.
EDCS-22300440.summary: This inscription details the death of a young individual named Paschasius, noting his approximate age and the date of his repose within a specific indiction cycle.

EDCS-22300441.translation: Of good memory Paulinus lived more or less 51 years, he rested in peace; of good memory Firmina, who lived more or less 51 years, she rested in peace; of good memory Felicianus, who lived 2 years and 16 days.
EDCS-22300441.summary: This inscription commemorates three individuals: Paulinus, Firmina, and Felicianus, noting their lifespans and burial, likely from a family tomb.

EDCS-22300443.translation: Of good memory Quodvultdeus, who lived more or less 50 years, he rested in peace on the Kalends of August; of good memory Thecla, who lived more or less 60 years, she rested in peace on the 13th day before the Kalends of August.
EDCS-22300443.summary: This epitaph records the deaths of Quodvultdeus and Thecla, providing their approximate ages and the dates of their peaceful repose, possibly indicating a joint commemoration or burial.

EDCS-22300449.translation: Here lies of good memory Sergius, who lived more or less 70 years, he rested in peace on the 6th day before the Ides of January.
EDCS-22300449.summary: This inscription commemorates Sergius, detailing his approximate age and the date of his peaceful repose, characteristic of late antique funerary records.

EDCS-22300450.translation: Of good memory Servulus, who lived 40 years, he died on the 6th day before the Kalends of January, he rests in peace.
EDCS-22300450.summary: This epitaph records the death of Servulus, specifying his age at death and the date of his repose, likely from a late antique or early medieval burial context.

EDCS-22300451.translation: Here my dear wife, Emerita, always joined with holy modesty, is covered by this pious tomb; faithful to God, she bore the years, twice twelve and more, unharmed by evil, and enjoying a blessed light, she retained sanctity, granted by the Lord as a crown. Laurentius against his vow.
EDCS-22300451.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Emerita, commissioned by her husband Laurentius, praising her piety, fidelity, and virtuous life, likely dating from the late Roman period.

EDCS-22300452.translation: Here lies Stephana, of good memory, a chaste, consecrated virgin, who lived for 13 years and rested in peace on the Kalends of August.
EDCS-22300452.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Stephana, a virgin, detailing her age at death and the date of her repose, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-22300456.translation: Bictor, of good memory, who lived approximately 70 years, rested in peace on the day before the Kalends of August.
EDCS-22300456.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Bictor, providing his approximate age at death and the date of his repose, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-22300457.translation: A good person, of good memory.
EDCS-22300457.summary: This is a simple epitaph for an unnamed individual, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-22300460.translation: Here lies Exsitiosus, of good memory, who lived approximately 25 years, laid to rest on the tenth day before the Kalends of February.
EDCS-22300460.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Exsitiosus, detailing his approximate age at death and the date of his burial, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-22300472.translation: Fidelis was born and departed in peace, approximately 40 years old. B
EDCS-22300472.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Fidelis, indicating her approximate age at death and the date of her repose, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-22400030.translation: To the departed spirits of Egnatia.
EDCS-22400030.summary: This is a funerary inscription invoking the spirits of the deceased, Egnatia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22400035.translation: To the departed spirits of Secundinus, Gaius, the master scribe.
EDCS-22400035.summary: This is a funerary inscription invoking the spirits of the deceased, Secundinus, a scribe, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22400042.translation: To the departed spirits of Petronia. Her mother Egnatia Ingenua set this up.
EDCS-22400042.summary: This is a funerary inscription from Egnatia Ingenua dedicating a monument to her mother Petronia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22400051.translation: To the departed spirits of Fortunata, who lived 60 years. Her daughter Felia made this for her deserving mother.
EDCS-22400051.summary: This is a funerary inscription from Felia dedicating a monument to her mother Fortunata, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22400053.translation: 20 days laid to rest in peace on the Nones of August. Iohannes of good memory, who lived [crossed out and overwritten]. Cresconius of good memory, who lived approximately 50 years, rested in peace on the eighth day before the Kalends of May, and [was] laid to rest on the ninth day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-22400053.summary: This is a complex tomb inscription recording the burial of at least two individuals, Iohannes and Cresconius, with detailed dates and ages, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-22400056.translation: To the departed spirits. Allenia Urbana made this for her well-deserving husband, who lived 25 years and 7 months.
EDCS-22400056.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife to her deceased husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22400057.translation: To the departed spirits. Lutatia Feifensis made this tomb for Marcus Alenius Sardus, who lived 50 years, well-deserving.
EDCS-22400057.summary: A funerary dedication by Lutatia Feifensis for her deceased husband Marcus Alenius Sardus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22400059.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Memius Saturninus made this for his most excellent wife Valeriae Amoccadae, and for himself and his descendants.
