EDCS-22400059.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius Memius Saturninus for his wife Valeriae Amoccadae and their descendants, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22400067.translation: Iunius So, 31 years old, they made this.
EDCS-22400067.summary: A funerary dedication for Iunius So, whose age is given, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500036.translation: Cleon, a slave of the salt-tax collectors, willingly gave this offering to Aesculapius Merre, well-deserving. A votive altar of bronze was set up by Cleon, supervisor of the salt taxes, by command of Iadnah, son of 'L'šmn, a bronze altar, as vowed by 'A'klyn, son of 'Šḥsgm, from the kingdom of 'Šm'. He heard the voice of the healer in the month of 'Špṭm, from Ḥmlkt and W'bd'šmn, son of Ḥmlnk.
EDCS-22500036.summary: A bilingual inscription in Greek and Aramaic recording a votive offering to Aesculapius by Cleon, a slave and supervisor of salt taxes, likely from the Roman period given the presence of Latin titles and Aramaic script.

EDCS-22500037.translation: Caius Stertinius Felix willingly paid his vow to the god Aesculapius.
EDCS-22500037.summary: A votive dedication by Caius Stertinius Felix to the god Aesculapius, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500038.translation: For the divine will of the god Hercules. The men of Martenses, through Iulius Princeps and the brothers Flavius Pompeius, [dedicated this].
EDCS-22500038.summary: A dedication to Hercules by the people of Martenses, mediated by Iulius Princeps and the Flavii Pompeii brothers, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500046.translation: To good memory. Ingenua lived 43 years; she rests in peace on the 16th day before the Kalends of March.
EDCS-22500046.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ingenua, recording her age and date of death, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500070.translation: [...], procurator? of the emperor, [restored text] / [restored text] praetor
EDCS-22500070.summary: A fragmentary inscription possibly mentioning an imperial procurator and a praetor, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500078.translation: [...] lived 11 months and 12 days. His sons made this for their well-deserving father, for his dutifulness.
EDCS-22500078.summary: A funerary dedication by sons to their father, recording his lifespan and their filial piety, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500081.translation: Agapitus and Felicitas offered this gift.
EDCS-22500081.summary: This inscription records a gift offered by Agapitus and Felicitas, likely a votive or dedicatory offering.

EDCS-22500096.translation: to Lucius, son of A[---] TIV[---].
EDCS-22500096.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording a dedication or a memorial to Lucius, son of A[---].

EDCS-22500104.translation: The first boundary stone of the Giddilitani is in the Port, the boundary stone of Euthicianus is Olla.
EDCS-22500104.summary: This inscription marks boundary stones belonging to the Giddilitani and Euthicianus, likely related to property or territorial divisions.

EDCS-22500107.translation: To the departed spirits. Priscus Ursinus lived for [---] years.
EDCS-22500107.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Priscus Ursinus, indicating his age at death.

EDCS-22500110.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Cassius Etruscilianus lived for 63 years.
EDCS-22500110.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Lucius Cassius Etruscilianus, stating his age at death as 63.

EDCS-22500117.translation: To the departed spirits. Tecusa [wife?] of Memmius Sar[---].
EDCS-22500117.summary: This is a funerary inscription possibly commemorating Tecusa, related to Memmius Sar[---].

EDCS-22500119.translation: Fulvius lived more or less well for 22 years.
EDCS-22500119.summary: This inscription records the lifespan of Fulvius as 22 years, with a somewhat unusual phrasing.

EDCS-22500136.translation: To Quietia, daughter of Sextus Severa, his dearest wife, Quintus Julius Zosimianus [dedicated this].
EDCS-22500136.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Quintus Julius Zosimianus to his wife, Quietia Iulia Severa.

EDCS-22500139.translation: Petronia Helena, freedwoman of Sextus, made [this] at her own expense.
EDCS-22500139.summary: This is an epitaph funded by Petronia Helena, a freedwoman of Sextus, indicating she commissioned her own monument.

EDCS-22500141.translation: To Gaius Vehilius Rufus, son of Gaius, of the Collina tribe.
EDCS-22500141.summary: This inscription is likely a dedication or honorific monument for Gaius Vehilius Rufus, mentioning his tribal affiliation.

EDCS-22500145.translation: To the memory of Theodora, a faithful girl of wondrous innocence and integrity, who lived 19 years, was married for 4 years, 3 months, and 19 days, and departed in Christ, laid to rest on the 6th day before the Kalends of May; to her dearest husband.
EDCS-22500145.summary: This epitaph commemorates a young girl named Theodora, detailing her life span and marital duration, and was likely erected by her husband.

EDCS-22500146.translation: Here lies Silbius, minister of the holy church; he awaits the aid of Christ to live again in his own flesh and to see the joys of the new light, with Him reigning. He lived 33 years, laid to rest in peace on the Nones of April.
EDCS-22500146.summary: This epitaph for Silbius, a church minister, expresses a Christian hope for resurrection and eternal life, and notes his burial date.

EDCS-22500168.translation: To Larius Secundus, of worthy merit, a good man, father of orphans and the needy, a most religious and highly accomplished patron of all sincerity and discipline. He lived more or less 70 years. His wife Paulina and son Ianuarius made this for him, a deserving man, in peace.
EDCS-22500168.summary: This epitaph honors Larius Secundus, a respected man known for his generosity and integrity, and was erected by his wife and son.

EDCS-22500171.translation: Dedicated by Flavius Amachius, a most accomplished man, president of the province of Sardinia, during the reign of our triumphant lord Augustus.
EDCS-22500171.summary: This inscription records the dedication of something, possibly a building or monument, by Flavius Amachius, the provincial governor of Sardinia, under an emperor referred to as Augustus.

EDCS-22500779.translation: A damaged inscription mentioning 'vetustate corruptam' (corrupted by age) and possibly relating to numbers or measurements, with illegible portions.
EDCS-22500779.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, partially illegible due to age, that likely recorded something that was restored, possibly related to measurements or a location.

EDCS-22500785.translation: Usellum from Neapolis restored this, corrupted by age.
EDCS-22500785.summary: This inscription states that Usellum, originating from Neapolis, restored something that had been damaged by age.

EDCS-22500803.translation: Under our lord Valentinian Augustus, holding tribunician power, father of the fatherland, he restored the road leading from Karales to Olbia, which had been corrupted by age, under the supervision of his procurator, Flavius Maximnus.
EDCS-22500803.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road between Karales and Olbia under Emperor Valentinian, supervised by the procurator Flavius Maximnus.

EDCS-22900536.summary: This is a maker's mark indicating that an object originated from the workshop of a person named Iunior.

EDCS-22900537.translation: From the workshop of Lucius Hortensius.
EDCS-22900537.summary: This is a maker's mark indicating that an object originated from the workshop of Lucius Hortensius.

EDCS-22900554.summary: This inscription appears to be a name, possibly indicating ownership or the subject of a monument.

EDCS-22900557.summary: This is a personal name, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Caius Helius Ianuarius.

EDCS-22900559.summary: This is a personal name, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Lucius Caecilius Sib[---].

EDCS-22900566.summary: This is a personal name, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Lucius Arrius Saturninus.

EDCS-22900569.summary: This is a personal name, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Gaius Cornelius Ursus.

EDCS-22900598.summary: This is a personal name, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Quintus Memmius Karus.

EDCS-22900625.summary: This is a personal name, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Gaius Vehilius Demosthenes.

EDCS-22900633.summary: This is a personal name, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Gaius Arrius Saturninus.

EDCS-22900637.summary: This is a dedication, likely commemorating a military or political achievement for saving Roman citizens.

EDCS-22900638.summary: This is a personal name, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Marcus Novius Germanus.

EDCS-22900640.summary: This is a personal name, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Lucius Munatius Threptus.

EDCS-22900651.summary: This inscription records the ownership or attribution of Gaius Julius Philippus, likely on a monument or object.

EDCS-22900658.summary: This inscription indicates that an object or monument comes from the workshop of Germanus, a craftsman.

EDCS-22900671.summary: This inscription records the ownership or attribution of Gaius Clodius Successus, likely on a monument or object.

EDCS-22900672.summary: This inscription records the ownership or attribution of Gaius Clodius Successus, likely on a monument or object.

EDCS-22900682.translation: Pottery work of our Caesar's Wolf
EDCS-22900682.summary: This inscription describes a pottery item made by 'Wolf', who is associated with 'Caesar', suggesting a workshop or a specific craftsman.

EDCS-22900686.summary: This inscription records the ownership or attribution of Tiberius Claudius Homilius, likely on a monument or object.

EDCS-22900689.translation: Pottery of Earinius Lucilla, wife of Verus, an agent.
EDCS-22900689.summary: This inscription records pottery belonging to Earinius Lucilla, the wife of Verus, who was an agent, likely indicating ownership or production.

EDCS-22900692.translation: From the potteries of Lucilla Quartionis
EDCS-22900692.summary: This inscription indicates that an object or monument comes from the potteries of Lucilla Quartionis, a producer of pottery.

EDCS-22900704.summary: This inscription records that Mel Phasidis Rodo made the object or monument, indicating the creator.

EDCS-22901720.summary: This inscription indicates that an object or monument originates from the estate of Apollonius, suggesting property or origin.

EDCS-22902036.translation: From the pottery workshops of Avidius Quietus.
EDCS-22902036.summary: This is a potter's mark indicating the origin of the pottery from the workshops of Avidius Quietus.

EDCS-22902039.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or a tombstone for individuals named Patrobius and Venusta.

EDCS-22902047.translation: To the honor of the deified Emperor.
EDCS-22902047.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly honoring an emperor, with 'deified' implied.

EDCS-23000002.translation: Apollonius, a slave, made this for Petronia Galla.
EDCS-23000002.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that the slave Apollonius made this monument for Petronia Galla.

EDCS-23000019.summary: This is a maker's mark or dedication stating that Mercurialis was the maker.

EDCS-23000049.summary: This is likely an ownership mark or a funerary dedication for Gaius Caecilius Clementis.

EDCS-23000072.summary: This is likely an ownership mark or a funerary dedication for Lucius Petronius Euscus.

EDCS-23000091.summary: This is a potter's mark indicating the origin of the pottery from the workshop of Pontianus.

EDCS-23000098.summary: This is likely a funerary inscription recording that Pamphilus made this for Rusticelia.

EDCS-23000204.summary: This is likely an ownership mark or a funerary dedication for Gaius Caecilius Clementis.

EDCS-23001497.summary: This inscription records the name of Lucius Antonius, possibly part of a larger inscription on a monument.

EDCS-23001510.summary: This inscription records the name Marcus Pontius Stephanus, likely a dedication or ownership mark.

EDCS-23001515.summary: This inscription records the name Valerius Apolaustus, possibly part of a funerary or dedication text.

EDCS-23001516.summary: This inscription indicates that a work originated from the workshop of Victor, likely related to a manufactured object.

EDCS-23001521.translation: Lucius Rustius Repentinus made it; Lucius Rustius Lupio made it.
EDCS-23001521.summary: This inscription records that both Lucius Rustius Repentinus and Lucius Rustius Lupio were makers, likely of a monument or object.

EDCS-23001550.translation: Lucius Eburius, son of Lucius, son of Lucius...
EDCS-23001550.summary: This inscription records the filiation of Lucius Eburius, indicating his father and grandfather, likely on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-23001561.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Matrius Merces, possibly part of a dedication or ownership record.

EDCS-23001569.summary: This inscription indicates that a work originated from the workshop of Pontianus, likely related to a manufactured object.

EDCS-23001589.summary: This inscription indicates that a work originated from the workshop of Victor, likely related to a manufactured object.

EDCS-23001590.summary: This inscription records the name Volusius Hermetis, possibly part of a dedication or funerary text.

EDCS-23001857.summary: This inscription records the ownership of a workshop belonging to Belicus.

EDCS-23001858.summary: This inscription records possession or ownership by Caius Caelius Clementis.

EDCS-23001865.translation: From the estates of Marcus Nomentanus.
EDCS-23001865.summary: This inscription records produce or goods originating from the estates of Marcus Nomentanus.

EDCS-23001871.summary: This inscription records the origin of goods or ownership of a workshop belonging to Victor.

EDCS-23101821.translation: From the workshop of Marcus Caelius.
EDCS-23101821.summary: This inscription records the origin of goods or ownership of a workshop belonging to Marcus Caelius.

EDCS-23101877.summary: This inscription records possession or ownership by Caius Messius Priscus.

EDCS-23101883.summary: This inscription records possession or ownership by Lucius Umbricius Mancia.

EDCS-23101884.translation: Of Lucius Versius, son of Pater.
EDCS-23101884.summary: This inscription records possession or ownership by Lucius Versius, son of Pater.

EDCS-23101888.summary: This inscription records possession or ownership by Cnaeus Ateius Plocamius.

EDCS-23101889.summary: This inscription records possession or ownership by Cnaeus Ateius Plocamius.

EDCS-23101890.summary: This is a personal name, likely an owner or dedicant of a monument, possibly from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-23200500.translation: Of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix.
EDCS-23200500.summary: This inscription refers to the Roman Emperor Caracalla, indicating a monument dedicated during his reign (198-217 AD).

EDCS-23300016.translation: Of Gaius Vallus Scipio, son of Gaius.
EDCS-23300016.summary: This inscription records the name of Gaius Vallus Scipio, son of Gaius, likely a funerary or dedicatory inscription from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23300042.summary: This inscription records the name of Claudia Galla, possibly a funerary dedication or reference to ownership from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23300048.summary: This inscription records the name of Lucius Aienus Dexter, likely a personal name found on a tomb or dedication from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23300062.summary: This inscription records the name of Marcus Aurelius Ionicus, likely a personal name found on a tomb or dedication from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23300063.translation: Of Publius Scantius Mopsus, son of Publius Scantius Mopsus.
EDCS-23300063.summary: This inscription records the name Publius Scantius Mopsus, repeating the name, likely a funerary or ownership marker from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23300064.translation: Servandus, freedman of Quintus, and Gaius, freedman of Lucius.
EDCS-23300064.summary: This inscription names two freedmen, Servandus (freedman of Quintus) and Gaius (freedman of Lucius), likely found on a tomb or property marker from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23300084.summary: This inscription records the name of Publius Spurius Justus, likely a personal name on a funerary monument or dedication from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23300097.translation: Three pounds of Cataplus the freedman, and Limen the son, gave as a gift to freedmen and their household.
EDCS-23300097.summary: This inscription records a gift of three pounds of something (likely an unspecified commodity or precious metal) from Cataplus, a freedman, and his son Limen, to their freedmen and household, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-23300098.translation: Cataplus, freedman of V librae, and his son Limen gave this gift to their freedmen and household.
EDCS-23300098.summary: This inscription records a gift made by Cataplus and his son Limen to their freedmen and household.

EDCS-23300099.translation: Of Diphilus, actor for Marcus Marius Auctus.
EDCS-23300099.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary dedication or ownership mark related to Diphilus, an actor.

EDCS-23300101.translation: To the household gods of Galilla.
EDCS-23300101.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Lararium, the household gods, of a person named Galilla.

EDCS-23300102.summary: This inscription is a simple genitive naming Euticianus, son of Euticianus, likely for a tomb or property marker.

EDCS-23300116.summary: This inscription is a genitive identifying Tiberius Julius Ianuarius, possibly for a tomb or ownership.

EDCS-23300117.summary: This inscription is a genitive identifying Gnaeus Lucretius Varus, possibly for a tomb or ownership.

EDCS-23300122.translation: Of Marcianus, servant of our Augustus.
EDCS-23300122.summary: This inscription identifies Marcianus, a servant of the emperor (Augustus), likely on a tomb or dedication.

EDCS-23300134.summary: This inscription is a genitive identifying Lucius Valerius Herclanus, possibly for a tomb or ownership.

EDCS-23300138.translation: Of Publius Attius Avitus Iunianus, son of Publius Attius Iunianus.
EDCS-23300138.summary: This inscription identifies Publius Attius Avitus Iunianus and his father, Publius Attius Iunianus, likely on a tomb or dedication.

EDCS-23300205.summary: This inscription is a fragmented genitive naming Quintus, son of Aulus, possibly part of a tomb or ownership marker.

EDCS-23400471.summary: A short dedicatory or exclamatory inscription for Theudosia, possibly from a tomb or a personal object.

EDCS-23400484.summary: An ownership or dedication inscription, likely indicating the owner or dedicator of an object or property.

EDCS-23400486.summary: An inscription indicating ownership or dedication, likely referring to the owner of an object or property.

EDCS-23400550.translation: Of Menatis, prefect of Tiberius, procurator
EDCS-23400550.summary: An inscription honoring Menatis, who held the significant administrative positions of prefect and procurator, likely under an emperor named Tiberius.

EDCS-23400554.translation: Senaria, may you live in God!
EDCS-23400554.summary: A funerary or devotional inscription wishing Senaria eternal life in a divine context.

EDCS-26400002.translation: In the year when Marcus Cispius was praetor
EDCS-26400002.summary: A dating formula, referencing a specific year based on the tenure of Marcus Cispius as praetor.

EDCS-26500753.translation: Of Marcus Tuccius, son of Lucius, of the Tromentina tribe, Galeo
EDCS-26500753.summary: A full Roman name, including filiation and tribal affiliation, likely marking an individual's identity on a document or monument.

EDCS-26700415.summary: The full name of an individual, likely serving as a dedicator, owner, or the subject of the inscription.

EDCS-27700311.summary: The full name of an individual, possibly a dedicator, owner, or the person commemorated by the inscription.

EDCS-27700314.summary: An inscription identifying an individual named Chilo, of Germanic origin, possibly related to Pudentis.

EDCS-28200078.translation: A good memory laid to rest. On the sixth day before the Ides of January. Febraria, wife, who lived 25 years, 3 months. [She was] with her husband for 5 years, 11 months, 18 days. Macedonius laid this down for the deserving departed.
EDCS-28200078.summary: This inscription is a tombstone for Febraria, recording her age, the duration of her marriage, and who placed the monument, dating to likely the late Roman period.

EDCS-28300239.translation: Beronice dies in peace, a young person, in peace.
EDCS-28300239.summary: This is a brief epitaph for Beronice, a young person, noting her peaceful repose.

EDCS-28300473.translation: Sweet girl Adeodata, received by the holy martyrs, promised in marriage. She perished as dawn broke on Wednesday, on the seventeenth day before the Kalends of January. She lived for sixteen years more or less. She departed immaculate.
EDCS-28300473.summary: This inscription marks the death of Adeodata, a young girl who lived approximately sixteen years, likely from the early Christian period, referencing her devotion and reception by martyrs.

EDCS-28300474.translation: Turritana, worthy in God, who lived 33 years. Burdened with slaves, she adorned the tomb.
EDCS-28300474.summary: This epitaph for Turritana records her age at death and mentions her ownership of slaves, suggesting a date in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300475.summary: This is a very brief inscription commemorating Pelagius, identifying him by his relation as a grandson, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300476.translation: Burial of the distinguished man Pascalis. Here he lies, snatched away by a foreign death. [This monument] grants you worthy honor for your merit. He lived 60 years, faithful, he rests in peace. To the good memory, sacred to the innocent soul of Pascasia, daughter of [someone], who lived more or less ten years. Quintus, the father, made [this].
EDCS-28300476.summary: This funerary inscription memorializes both Pascalis, a respected man who died away from home, and his young daughter Pascasia, erected by their father Quintus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800001.summary: This is a brief honorific inscription for Olybrius, a man of high status, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-29600346.translation: 168 miles. Our lords Emperors Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, Germanicus maximus, holding tribunician power for the Vth time, consul for the IIIrd time, father of the country; and Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, Germanicus maximus, holding tribunician power for the IIIIth time, consul for the IIIrd time, father of the country; and Publius Cornelius Egnatius Valerianus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth. He restored the road which leads from Carales to Olbia, which was ruined, under the supervision of Publius Maridius Maridianus, his most excellent procurator.
EDCS-29600346.summary: This milestone records the restoration of a road between Carales and Olbia under the emperors Valerian and Gallienus and Caesar Valerianus, supervised by the procurator Publius Maridius Maridianus, dating to their joint rule in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-30000434.summary: This is a simple ownership inscription, likely for a tomb or monument, belonging to Caius Atilius Trophimus.

EDCS-30000446.summary: This inscription is a fragment mentioning a martyr named Vincentius, possibly part of a funerary or dedicatory text.

EDCS-30000448.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription stating that Marcus Calicius Diophantus created the object or monument.

EDCS-30000453.translation: To a good memory; she lived more or less 45 years and rested in peace on the 11th day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-30000453.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and date of repose for an individual, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-30200046.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a name inscribed on a tombstone or object, belonging to Statius Marcius Lucifer.

EDCS-31700067.translation: DDD miles of the road which leads from Caralis to Olbia, under the charge of Aurelius Marcus, governor of the province of Sardinia, by decree of the Senate, for our Lords Emperors Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletian and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian, Fathers of the Fatherland, Fortunate, Unconquered Augusti, and for the Caesars Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximian, Augusti.
EDCS-31700067.summary: This is a milestone inscription detailing the repair or construction of a road between Caralis and Olbia, commissioned by governors under the reign of Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Galerius, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-31700068.translation: DDD miles of the road, corrupted by age, under the charge of Maximinus, governor of the province of Sardinia, by decree of the Senate, for the Emperors Caesars Valerius Constantius, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, with tribunician power, proconsul, and Marcus Aurelius Valerianus, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-31700068.summary: This milestone inscription records the repair of a road that had fallen into disrepair, attributed to the rule of Emperors Constantius and Maximian, under the governorship of Maximinus, likely from the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-31700069.translation: One hundred and seventy miles. Our Lords the most holy Emperors Trebonianus Gallus, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, and Lucius Vibius Afinius Veldumnianus Volusianus, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, restored the road which leads from Caralis to Olbia, consumed by age, under the charge and dedication of Antonius Septimius Heraclitus, a distinguished man, his procurator.
EDCS-31700069.summary: This milestone inscription records the restoration of a road between Caralis and Olbia, funded by Emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, and overseen by the procurator Antonius Septimius Heraclitus, dating to their joint rule in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31700070.translation: One hundred and seventy miles. For our Lords the Emperors Julius Philippus, Pious, Fortunate, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, Consul, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul, and the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, Pious, Fortunate, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, Consul, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul, the road which leads from Caralis to Olbia, corrupted by age, was restored under the charge of Publius Aelius Valens, his procurator, by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-31700070.summary: This milestone inscription documents the restoration of a road between Caralis and Olbia, during the co-rule of Emperor Philip the Elder and his son Philip the Younger, overseen by the procurator Publius Aelius Valens, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31700071.translation: One hundred and sixty miles. For Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, Consul, and Emperor Caesar Licinius Publius Gallienus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, Proconsul, the road which leads from Caralis to Olbia, corrupted by age, was restored under the charge of Marcus Calpurnius Caelianus, his procurator.
EDCS-31700071.summary: This milestone inscription records the restoration of a road between Caralis and Olbia, under the joint rule of Emperors Valerian and Gallienus, with the work supervised by the procurator Marcus Calpurnius Caelianus, likely from the period of their co-emperorship in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31700072.translation: One hundred miles. For our Lords Emperors Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletian and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augusti, and for the noblest Caesars Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximian, the road which leads from Caralis to Olbia, under the charge of Aurelius Marcus, a most accomplished man, governor of the province of Sardinia, by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-31700072.summary: This milestone inscription records the construction or repair of a road between Caralis and Olbia, commissioned by governors under the Tetrarchy, specifically honoring Emperors Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Galerius, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-31700073.translation: 170 miles. Restoring and dedicating the road that leads from Cagliari to Olbia, consumed by age, under the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aemilius Aemilianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul; Maximianus, a man of perfect standing, governor of the province of Sardinia, saw to it that it was done, saluting our lords Valentinian and Valens, Augusti, born for the good of the republic.
EDCS-31700073.summary: This inscription records the restoration and dedication of a road between Cagliari and Olbia, commissioned by Emperor Aemilianus and overseen by the governor Maximianus, likely in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-31700074.translation: 5 miles. Under our lord Flavius Claudius Constantinus Victor semper Augustus, while the governor of the province of Sardinia, Munatius Genta, a man of perfect standing, was in charge.
EDCS-31700074.summary: This inscription marks 5 miles of a road, dedicated during the reign of Emperor Constantine, under the supervision of the governor Munatius Genta, likely in the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-31700075.translation: 4 miles. Under Flavius Julius Constantinus Pius Felix Caesar Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 7th time, consul, proconsul, while Florianus, a man of perfect standing, governor of the province of Sardinia, was in charge.
EDCS-31700075.summary: This inscription marks 4 miles of a road, dedicated during the reign of Emperor Constantine, under the supervision of the governor Florianus, likely in the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-31700076.translation: 168 miles. Under our lord Valerius Licinianus Augustus, Licinius Pius Felix Invictus, and our lord perpetuo semper Augustus, while Titus Septimius Januarius, a man of the highest distinction, governor of the province of Sardinia, dedicated it, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-31700076.summary: This inscription marks 168 miles of a road, dedicated under the emperors Licinius and another Augustus (likely Licinius II), overseen by the governor Titus Septimius Januarius, probably in the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-31700077.translation: 168 miles. Under our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul, restoring the road that leads from Cagliari to Olbia, corrupted by age, while Julius..., a distinguished man, governor of the province of Sardinia, was in charge, by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-31700077.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road between Cagliari and Olbia under Emperor Carus, overseen by the governor Julius..., in the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-31700078.translation: To Olbia, under Carinus, a distinguished man.
EDCS-31700078.summary: This very brief inscription likely honors the governor Carinus and possibly relates to the city of Olbia, date uncertain but possibly late Roman period.

EDCS-33600033.translation: To a good memory, Beneriosus, who lived about 23 years, rests in peace, deposited on the 3rd day before the Nones of November.
EDCS-33600033.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Beneriosus, who died around the age of 23, with the burial date specified as November 2nd, likely in the late Roman period.

EDCS-34700087.translation: To the divine spirits. Aulus Licinius Axius lived 45 years, 7 months, and 27 days. His wife made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-34700087.summary: This epitaph records the life span of Aulus Licinius Axius and was erected by his wife, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700105.translation: 7 miles. Under our lord Flavius Julius Constantius Victor semper Augustus, our perpetual lord, while Flavius Amachius, a man of perfect standing, governor of the province of Sardinia, was in charge, the road that leads from Nora to Quizam.
EDCS-34700105.summary: This inscription marks 7 miles of a road between Nora and Quizam, dedicated during the reign of Emperor Constantius, under the supervision of the governor Flavius Amachius, likely in the mid-4th century AD.

EDCS-34700122.translation: 100 miles. To Emperor Caesar Constantine, most noble Caesar, Augustus, the happy one, to our lords Constantinus and Licinius, the Augusti, the greatest.
EDCS-34700122.summary: This inscription marks 100 miles of a road, dedicated to Emperor Constantine and his co-emperors Licinius and possibly others, likely in the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-34700123.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul, restored the road from Karalis to Olbia, corrupted by age, under the supervision of his excellent procurator Septimius Necrinus. 116 miles. For the Emperor.
EDCS-34700123.summary: This inscription records the restoration of the road from Karalis to Olbia by Emperor Aurelian, supervised by his procurator Septimius Necrinus, with the distance noted as 116 miles.

EDCS-34700126.translation: Greetings to the Emperor Victor, from Sallustius Exsuperius, most perfect governor.
EDCS-34700126.summary: This inscription is a greeting to the Emperor from the governor Sallustius Exsuperius.

EDCS-34700127.translation: 19 miles. For the Pious Happy Invincible Emperor Augustus. The road which leads from Karalis to Olbia, corrupted by age, [was restored]. For the most perfect governor of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-34700127.summary: This inscription records the restoration of the road from Karalis to Olbia, noting 19 miles, and is dedicated by the perfect governor of Sardinia.

EDCS-34700128.translation: For Flavius Julius Constantinus Pius Felix BCES Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul, proconsul, under the supervision of Florinus, most perfect governor of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-34700128.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Flavius Julius Constantinus by his proconsul, under the supervision of the governor of Sardinia, Florinus.

EDCS-35200007.translation: Here lies Furiosus of good memory, who lived more or less 60 years, and rested in peace, buried on the 15th day before the Kalends of February. Here lies Agate of good memory, who lived more or less 26 years, and rested in peace, buried on the 17th day before the Kalends of June.
EDCS-35200007.summary: This is a double funerary inscription recording the lives and burial dates of Furiosus and Agate.

EDCS-35200008.translation: Enatus lived, Bonifatius lived more or less.
EDCS-35200008.summary: This fragmentary inscription seems to record the lives of two individuals, Enatus and Bonifatius.

EDCS-35200011.translation: Here lies Victor of good memory, who lived more or less 75 years, and rested in peace, buried on the 3rd day before the Kalends of May.
EDCS-35200011.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the life and burial date of Victor.

EDCS-35200012.translation: Here lies Pompeiainus of good memory, who lived more or less 16 years, and rested in peace, buried on the 3rd day before the Ides of September.
EDCS-35200012.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the life and burial date of Pompeiainus.

EDCS-35500586.translation: Procurator... prefect of the province... 100 sesterces.
EDCS-35500586.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording an official and a financial transaction, possibly related to a procurator and a prefect.

EDCS-39101508.summary: This is a Greek dedication of an object by Krates to the god Dionysos.

EDCS-39101509.summary: This is a personal name, likely a tombstone inscription for Hipparchos, son of Hegesistratus.

EDCS-39101510.summary: This is a personal name, likely a tombstone inscription for Aristodice, daughter of Anaxilus.

EDCS-39101511.translation: Euxenus, son of Anaxilas, from Massalia.
EDCS-39101511.summary: This is a personal name and origin, likely a tombstone inscription for Euxenus, son of Anaxilas, from Massalia.

EDCS-39300182.translation: Gnaeus Atellius Bulio, freedman of Gnaeus.
EDCS-39300182.summary: This is a Roman personal name and manumission record, likely for Gnaeus Atellius Bulio, a freedman of Gnaeus.

EDCS-39300198.summary: This is a Roman personal name, likely a tombstone inscription for Marcus Pinarius, son of Marcus.

EDCS-39300199.translation: Of Marcus Caius and the Pontilii, sons of Marcus.
EDCS-39300199.summary: This records two individuals, Marcus Caius and other Pontilii, sons of Marcus, possibly a family tomb or dedication.

EDCS-39300200.translation: Lucius Planus Russinus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-39300200.summary: This is a Roman personal name, likely a tombstone inscription for Lucius Planus Russinus, son of Lucius.

EDCS-39300201.summary: This is a Roman personal name, likely a tombstone inscription for Lucius Planus, son of Lucius.

EDCS-39501258.translation: In the time of Aleximachos, son of Panamos.
EDCS-39501258.summary: This inscription marks a period of time, referencing the magistracy or prominence of Aleximachos, son of Panamos.

EDCS-39501264.summary: This is a personal name, likely a tombstone inscription for Antonia Rufina.

EDCS-41700199.translation: Savinus, defender of the apostolic see, who lived for more or less 58 years, rests here in peace, buried on the day before the Nones of March in the indiction cycle.
EDCS-41700199.summary: This inscription records the burial of Savinus, a defender of the apostolic see, with his age and burial date.

EDCS-41700221.translation: Here rests Inbenia, handmaid of God. She departed from this world in the month of January, on the third day. May she live in the Lord. Amen.
EDCS-41700221.summary: This inscription records the death and burial of Inbenia, a consecrated woman, with her departure date.

EDCS-41700222.translation: Here lies Constantius, of good memory, who lived 27 years and rested in peace. Here lies Bindicius, of good memory, who lived 27 years and rested in peace. Here lies Sophronia, of good memory, who lived 52 years and rested in peace on the Kalends of March. Here lies Urbana, of good memory, who lived 45 years and rested in peace on the eighth day before the Kalends of July.
EDCS-41700222.summary: This inscription records the burial of four individuals (Constantius, Bindicius, Sophronia, and Urbana), including their ages and burial dates.

EDCS-41700223.translation: Here lies Lucifer, archbishop of Cagliari, primate of Sardinia and Corsica, dearest son of the Holy Roman Church, of good memory, who lived 81 years. Buried on the 20th day of May.
EDCS-41700223.summary: This inscription records the burial of Lucifer, an archbishop of Cagliari and primate of Sardinia and Corsica, with his age and burial date.

EDCS-41700224.translation: Saint Lucifer the bishop lived 81 years. Departed on the 20th day of May.
EDCS-41700224.summary: This inscription records the age and death date of Saint Lucifer, a bishop.

EDCS-41700225.translation: Numida of Cuicul, of good memory, lived 51 years and 5 months. Rested in peace.
EDCS-41700225.summary: This inscription records the burial of Numida of Cuicul, with his age and length of life.

EDCS-41700227.translation: Here lies Theodorus, of good memory, a physician, who lived for more or less 45 years and rested in peace on the Nones of January in the fourth indiction cycle.
EDCS-41700227.summary: This inscription records the burial of Theodorus, a physician, with his age and burial date.

EDCS-41700228.translation: Here lies Priis, of good memory. Here lies Ians, more or less. Here lies Ann.
EDCS-41700228.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to record the burial of at least three individuals with incomplete information.

EDCS-41700230.translation: Here lies Pompeianus, of good memory, who lived for more or less 16 years. He rested in peace on the third day before the Ides of September.
EDCS-41700230.summary: This inscription records the burial of Pompeianus, with his age and burial date.

EDCS-41700233.translation: Here lies Eugenius, of good memory, who lived for more or less 30 years. He rested in peace on the day of January in the tenth indiction cycle.
EDCS-41700233.summary: This inscription records the burial of Eugenius, with his age and burial date.

EDCS-43600115.translation: Dionysius set up here his good mother, mistress, and wife Amia, who lived with him for 40 years; he made the monument worthy of her. Be brave, mistress; no one is immortal. These things...
EDCS-43600115.summary: A funerary monument set up by Dionysius for his mother, mistress, and wife, Amia.

EDCS-45600047.translation: Publius Ortus, a native of Caralitta.
EDCS-45600047.summary: An epitaph identifying Publius Ortus as a native of Caralitta.

EDCS-45600048.translation: To the good memory of Matera, who often aided the poor and the common people, and to whom the people granted what she desired; she lived fearlessly as if all children were her own, not fearing death but conquering it through Christ, who will shine with eternal light. She was chosen as a mother by the poor and needy, and her husband thus speaks to his sweet wife: 'You lived 70 years, 3 months, and 15 days, departing on the 10th day before the Kalends of May.'
EDCS-45600048.summary: A tombstone inscription honoring Matera, praising her charitable deeds, her devotion to children, and her Christian faith, and recording her death date.

EDCS-48200140.summary: An ownership or dedication inscription for Marcus Valerius Albo.

EDCS-48200144.translation: Gaius Julius Primitius, the craftsman, made it.
EDCS-48200144.summary: An inscription stating that Gaius Julius Primitius, a craftsman, made the object.

EDCS-48200145.translation: Chilo, German of Emperor Augustus Caesar.
EDCS-48200145.summary: An identification of Chilo as a German belonging to Emperor Augustus Caesar.

EDCS-48200146.translation: Lucius Valerius Rufus, German of Emperor Augustus Caesar.
EDCS-48200146.summary: An identification of Lucius Valerius Rufus as a German belonging to Emperor Augustus Caesar.

EDCS-48300006.summary: A dedication or ownership inscription related to Saint Saturninus.

EDCS-48700578.translation: Here lies the blessed martyr Archelaus the presbyter; he died on the third day before the Kalends of March in the year...
EDCS-48700578.summary: This inscription is a funerary epitaph for Archelaus, a presbyter and martyr, recording his death date and implying a Christian context, likely from late antiquity.

EDCS-58100155.summary: A funerary inscription recording the maker of a monument.

EDCS-58200014.translation: To the Divine Manes. His sons made this for Julia M., their mother, who lived 56 years.
EDCS-58200014.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to a mother by her sons.

EDCS-60600029.translation: To one who deserved well, Editius.
EDCS-60600029.summary: A dedication to a deserving individual, possibly a benefactor or official.

EDCS-64000180.translation: To the Emperor, Caesar, our lord Flavius Julius Constantius, the greatest victor, son of Constantine.

EDCS-69200239.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, likely a dedication by a mother for her son.

EDCS-69200242.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Inia made [this] for her deserving husband.
EDCS-69200242.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, a dedication by a wife for her husband.

EDCS-70500029.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Decimus Veturius Fortunatus made [this] for himself, for Veturia Januaria his daughter, and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-70500029.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, a self-dedication and dedication for family members by Decimus Veturius Fortunatus.

EDCS-70500035.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a funerary epitaph or dedication, mentioning Julius, son of Marcus.

EDCS-70500056.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription with illegible text, possibly a funerary epitaph or dedication.

EDCS-70500110.translation: Vininus, with his wife Lucia, vowed to Ianus. Pyrrus the philosopher, Hilarus, Rufus, and Martinianus, his freedmen, made this for Epidius Li[--]pa.
EDCS-70500110.summary: This is a dedication to the god Ianus, likely from the Imperial period, mentioning specific individuals and their relationship to the deity.

EDCS-70500112.summary: This inscription consists of a single imperial name, likely part of a larger monument or dedication.

EDCS-70800047.translation: He lived 18 years. His parents made this for their dearest son.
EDCS-70800047.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of death of a young boy, dedicated by his parents.

EDCS-70800048.translation: To the departed spirits. [Iana] made this for her deserving wife.
EDCS-70800048.summary: A funerary dedication, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, to a deceased wife.

EDCS-70900496.summary: This inscription marks a boundary and specifies its length in feet, likely related to land ownership or administrative division.

EDCS-71000303.translation: To the divine and blessed spirits. To Salustius Esuperius, a most excellent man, governor of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-71000303.summary: This is a dedication or honorific inscription for Salustius Esuperius, a high-ranking official (governor) of Sardinia, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-71000586.translation: To the departed spirits. Terellus Urseccus lived 80 years, 5 months, and 15 days. Aurelius and Saidurus, his heirs, made this for him, the well-deserving.
EDCS-71000586.summary: A funerary epitaph dating to the Roman Imperial period, detailing the age at death of Terellus Urseccus and stating it was erected by his heirs.

EDCS-71500104.translation: She lived 37 years in peace and was buried in November of the 9th indiction, and the handmaid of God Maria lived 14 months in peace and was buried in March of the 3rd of the 4th indiction.
EDCS-71500104.summary: A bilingual (Greek and Latin elements) funerary inscription from the Byzantine period, recording the deaths of two women, Maria and another unnamed woman, with specific dates and ages.

EDCS-72800071.summary: This is a fragmented inscription mentioning the name Valerianus Pius, likely part of a larger text, possibly honorific or funerary.

EDCS-72900126.translation: Here lies the one of good memory, Fel[--]. He lived more or less [X] years. In peace, on the third day before the Kalends of [Month].
EDCS-72900126.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the name Fel(ix?) and approximate age at death, with a date of burial.

EDCS-72900127.translation: Heunnos, son of Lieu and Heua, of the Ceti tribe, lived 57 years, 5 months, 13 days. Ne Patronia erected this for him, deserving well.
EDCS-72900127.summary: A funerary epitaph for Heunnos, set up by Ne Patronia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72900128.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Crementius lived 17 years, 2 months. His parents erected this for their sweetest son.
EDCS-72900128.summary: A funerary epitaph for Crementius, erected by his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-76000035.translation: Lucius Planus, son of Lucius Russinus.
EDCS-76000035.summary: A funerary epitaph or ownership mark identifying Lucius Planus, son of Lucius Russinus.

EDCS-76000036.translation: Marcus Valerius Rectus, son of Sextus Ul[pius?].
EDCS-76000036.summary: A funerary epitaph or ownership mark identifying Marcus Valerius Rectus, son of Sextus Ulpius (partially restored).

EDCS-76000044.translation: Of the two Juniuses, Melissus and Melissa.
EDCS-76000044.summary: This inscription appears to name two individuals, Melissus and Melissa, possibly as owners or dedicators of a monument, likely from the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-76000046.summary: This fragmentary inscription refers to Germanicus, adopted son of Emperor Augustus, likely commemorating a victory or achievement of his, possibly dating to the early 1st century CE.

EDCS-76000047.translation: Gaius Utius, son of Gaius, of the Menenia tribe.
EDCS-76000047.summary: This is a standard Roman naming formula, identifying Gaius Utius and his tribal affiliation, likely part of a tombstone or dedication from the Republican or Imperial period.

EDCS-76000048.translation: Gaius Utius, son of Gaius, of the Menenia tribe.

EDCS-76000049.translation: Gnaeus Atelius, son of Gnaeus, from Bulio.
EDCS-76000049.summary: This inscription provides the full Roman name and origin of an individual, Gnaeus Atelius, possibly serving as an owner or subject of a monument from the Republican or Imperial period.

EDCS-76000050.translation: Gnaeus Atelius, son of Gnaeus, from Bulio.
EDCS-76000050.summary: This inscription provides the full Roman name and origin of an individual, Gnaeus Atelius, possibly serving as an owner or subject of a monument from the Republican or Imperial period.

EDCS-76000058.summary: This genitive inscription likely refers to the property or dedication of Marcus Procilius Melager, possibly a tombstone or dedication from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-76000062.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Marcus Procilius Melager, likely from a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-76000123.translation: Here lies Aristea, dutiful daughter of her parents, on the 13th day before the Kalends of January.
EDCS-76000123.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aristea, recording her name and date of death.

EDCS-76000125.translation: Servius Secundus, who was his freedman and a faithful procurator in managing his affairs, and his loving wife Verla and their deserving children erected this for Atilius Lucius, born in Turris, his dearest husband, his cherished psatria (a type of musician or dancer), a most devoted man, who lived about 65 years.
EDCS-76000125.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a wife and freedman for Atilius Lucius, an educated man and procurator, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-76000126.translation: Dis Manibus. Faustus placed this for his well-deserving son, Marcus Florus, son of Sempronius, born in Caralis, who was chosen by the people of Turris as a most eloquent orator and came there, and after 20 years joined himself to his wife Favia, daughter of Caius.
EDCS-76000126.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Marcus Florus, an orator who moved from Caralis to Turris and married Favia, and was commemorated by his son Faustus.

EDCS-76000127.translation: Here lies Pomtella, who on the Kalends of February was well-deserving and born with her father Sofronius under Marcus Titius, procurator of Turris.
EDCS-76000127.summary: This inscription records the death of Pomtella, noting her birth date and association with her father Sofronius and a procurator named Marcus Titius.

EDCS-76000128.translation: Statilius Optimo of Turris, aged 20, refusing to offer his service in the restoration of the temple dedicated to Fortuna, at the entreaties of his fiancée Nerina, a Christian, was captured in the Capitol of that very city, and there after...
EDCS-76000128.summary: This inscription fragment describes the capture and likely death of Statilius Optimo, a 20-year-old Christian man from Turris, who refused to participate in the restoration of a temple.

EDCS-76000130.translation: Subrius, now an elder, wisely completed this ample threshold; Marcus Taurus, rejoicing, adorns it with marble.
EDCS-76000130.summary: This inscription records the completion of a building's threshold by Subrius and its adornment with marble by Marcus Taurus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78300134.translation: Here lie dutifully Adonius and his wife Eurie, and their children Palus and Palonia, who lived 14, 31, and 7 years respectively.
EDCS-78300134.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a family, Adonius, his wife Eurie, and their children Palus and Palonia, listing their ages at death.

EDCS-78300135.translation: Here lie dutifully Amatellus and Ferdinandus.
EDCS-78300135.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription commemorating Amatellus and Ferdinandus.

EDCS-78300136.translation: Here lies Sanctus Amatus, bishop and martyr, who lived 78 years, with other saints.
EDCS-78300136.summary: This inscription commemorates Sanctus Amatus, a bishop and martyr who lived to 78 years old, and is associated with other saints.

EDCS-78300137.translation: Here lies the blessed martyr Iesmundus, who lived for 9 years. Here lies the blessed martyr Victoria, who lived for 10 years. Here lies the blessed martyr Florus, who lived for about 6 years, a native of Cagliari.
EDCS-78300137.summary: This is a funerary inscription for three individuals, two named and one identified as 'blessed martyr', likely from late antiquity or the early medieval period.

EDCS-78300560.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the beginning of a dedication or ownership mark, naming Caius Timinius Paniscus.

EDCS-79000010.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely part of a larger text or an ownership mark, referring to Emperor Augustus.
